title: 错误日志 2026-04-01

content:
Monthly Summary — 2026-03 This month’s work was highly focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics multimodal clustering research project: after completing environment initialization on the DCC HPC cluster, we systematically integrated the TEDDY-G 400M foundation model, Leiden clustering algorithm, and STHD pathology annotation Ground Truth. Multi-encoder benchmarks revealed STAIG framework’s high robustness to gene encoders and the systematic transfer loss of Foundation Models on clustering tasks. The paper’s Fig 1–6 framework was finalized, marking the project’s transition from the experimental phase to the paper-writing phase.
Monthly Overview Metric Value Active Days 4 / 31 Total Conversations 14 Projects Involved 5 Tasks Completed 19 Tasks In Progress 3 Total Tokens 63,798,066 Total Cost $39.58 Daily Average Cost $9.89 Project Progress MIHD (Spatial Transcriptomics Multimodal Clustering) (4 active days) — 🔄 active The month began with issue triage and documentation, mid-month saw new encoder integration and bug fixes, and the end of the month brought systematic expansion of evaluation dimensions (Leiden, HD dataset, Ground Truth), culminating in a finalized paper structure with experimental work largely complete. Best ARI ~0.546 (DLPFC), STAIG framework robustness verified; slice 151676 remains an unresolved systematic challenge.
Key Milestones:
Identified root cause of cross-slice retrieval failure (PCA/STAIG coordinate spaces are incomparable per slice; scGPT used as cross-slice encoder) Integrated TEDDY-G 400M and completed full 11-slice embedding extraction (185MB) Multi-encoder STAIG fusion benchmark confirmed framework robustness (Teddy vs PCA ARI difference: 0.002) Integrated Leiden clustering (binary search to match n_clusters), STHD Ground Truth (100% match), and HD ARI/NMI evaluation Paper Fig 1–6 framework finalized, referencing structures from four papers: MISO/STAIG/QueST/Yuan DCC HPC Environment Setup (1 active day) — ✅ completed Completed from-scratch environment initialization in a single day, establishing the foundation for running MIHD experiments on DCC. The main obstacle was that config files do not migrate automatically and had to be reconstructed from existing configs.
Key Milestones:
rclone + sync.py completed Google Drive sync for four data categories Investigated GPU partition resources (scavenger-h200 H200×8 available) Installed cchelper skill Key Achievements This Month Identified root cause of MIHD cross-slice retrieval failure and found a solution (2026-03-23, MIHD) — Confirmed root cause is that PCA/STAIG processes each slice independently, producing incomparable embedding spaces. scGPT, due to its shared coordinate system, is the optimal encoder for cross-slice retrieval (average SL@50=0.416, 100% hit rate), revealing the architectural principle that single-slice clustering and cross-slice retrieval require different encoders. Discovered and fixed 4 critical bugs in extract_teddy() (2026-03-29, MIHD) — Systematic source code review revealed: incorrect Tokenizer class name, wrong model forward parameter name (input_ids→gene_ids), wrong output key (last_hidden_state→outputs[‘cell_emb’]), and missing gene symbol to Ensembl ID mapping. After fixes, the first slice (151508, 4384 spots) was successfully validated. Verified STAIG framework’s high robustness to gene encoders (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Teddy 1024d (ARI=0.495) and PCA 50d (ARI=0.497) performed nearly identically under the STAIG framework, with a gap of only 0.002, demonstrating that STAIG’s spatial graph contrastive learning mechanism dominates clustering performance and the impact of gene embedding quality is greatly diluted. Paper Fig 1–6 framework planning completed (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Referencing four competing papers — MISO (Nat Methods), STAIG (Nat Comms), QueST, and Yuan (Nat Methods) — established a complete paper structure: Fig1=framework diagram, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=cross-slice Query, Fig6=Query biological discovery. Written into ECL YAML and plans.md, marking the project’s official transition to the writing phase. TEDDY-G 400M full 11-slice embedding extraction completed (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Completed 1024d gene embedding extraction for all 11 DLPFC slices (151508–151676) in a dedicated conda teddy environment, totaling 185MB, providing a complete data foundation for multi-encoder comparison benchmarks. STHD Ground Truth integration (100% match rate) (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Added _load_sthd_annotations() function, achieving 17502/17502 spots 100% match via r_big/c_big coordinates; CE+Tumor biological merging (Neoplasm + Non-neoplastic Epithelium → Epithelium) yielded 3 meaningful clusters, providing quantitative Ground Truth for HD evaluation. HD full fusion strategy evaluation revealed naive fusion information dilution (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Completed 10/13 HD fusion strategy experiments; found that simple fusion strategies (concat/mean/element_wise_sum) produced ARI (0.19–0.20) lower than unimodal PCA (0.351), while attention fusion approached PCA (ARI=0.342). UNI2 vision-only achieved the highest NMI (0.387) but lower ARI than PCA, revealing that NMI and ARI can be inconsistent across different datasets. Summarized systematic transfer loss pattern of Foundation Models on clustering (2026-03-30, MIHD) — Teddy gene-only ARI (0.186) underperformed PCA (0.255). Systematic analysis confirmed the root cause: Foundation Models are optimized for disease discriminability (not variance maximization), high-dimensional spaces (1024d) degrade KMeans performance, and training data (dissociated scRNA-seq) differs in distribution from spatial transcriptomics. Leiden clustering binary search integration (+15% ARI) (2026-03-31, MIHD) — Implemented binary search in clustering.py to match target n_clusters (range [0.01, 10], up to 20 iterations), enabling fair comparison between Leiden and KMeans. Leiden improved ARI by ~15% over KMeans on DLPFC, but improvement over STAIG was limited. First structured documentation of MIHD project historical issues (2026-03-23, MIHD) — By consolidating plans.md, RESULTS.md, and diagnostic reports, generated docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md — the first centralized record of best ARI 0.546, root cause of cross-slice retrieval failures, fixed bug inventory, and a 4-phase ablation experiment plan, providing a structured entry point for subsequent research. Recurring Issues 1. Systematic failure on a specific slice (151676) (occurred 3 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Slice 151676 has data-specific issues: zero-variance embeddings cause mclust to fail and fall back to KMeans (dragging down the global mean), and model collapse under the STAIG framework cannot be recovered. The root cause remains unidentified, possibly related to anomalous gene expression distribution or spatial structure in this slice. Status: 🔄 Ongoing
2. Pipeline hardcoded configuration inconsistent with slice-specific parameters (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Pipeline global configuration (e.g., top_genes=3000, dataset defaults, image path structure) has implicit divergences from slice-specific config (151676 uses 5000 genes) or dataset-specific structure (HD vs DLPFC image paths), causing cache check false positives (sys.exit) and visual extraction failures. Status: 🔧 Workaround available
3. HPC environment configuration obstacles (conda activation, missing config files) (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-31 Root Cause: Config files do not automatically migrate to new machines (sync.json must be manually reconstructed), and conda initialization on SLURM compute nodes differs from login nodes (requires eval $(conda shell.bash hook) rather than source activate). Both issues stem from a lack of experience with environment migration. Status: ✅ Resolved
4. Cross-session context loss requiring repeated background provision (occurred 2 times) Dates: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-29 Root Cause: Claude’s session isolation mechanism means key research progress (e.g., Teddy test results) is not persisted to the memory system, requiring users to re-provide arXiv links or background information in subsequent sessions. Status: 🔄 Ongoing
Human-AI Collaboration Trends Human-led key insights: 7 AI limitation pattern: Cross-session memory loss: context at critical research milestones is not automatically persisted, requiring users to repeatedly provide background AI limitation pattern: Hardcoded assumptions delay bug discovery: defaults to global config rather than dynamically resolving slice-specific parameters, tracing root causes only after actual errors occur AI limitation pattern: Excessive reliance on cluster resources: unable to proactively judge whether small datasets (17502 spots) require GPU/SLURM, defaulting to job submission AI limitation pattern: Convention assumptions about non-standard APIs: assumed HuggingFace standard interfaces without reading source code, leading to 4 TEDDY API usage errors AI limitation pattern: Inefficient monitoring strategy: background tasks used multi-round sleep+ls polling instead of event-driven mechanisms Improvement direction: Proactively trigger memory persistence at critical research milestones to avoid cross-session context loss Improvement direction: Proactively identify path/parameter dataset-specific risks when dealing with multi-dataset pipelines Improvement direction: Do not assume API conventions for non-standard libraries before reading source code; declare upfront that verification is needed Improvement direction: Provide proactive experiment prioritization suggestions rather than just listing options and waiting for user decisions Improvement direction: Estimate compute requirements based on data scale, distinguishing locally runnable tasks from those requiring the cluster Selected Insights This Month Architecture STAIG framework is highly robust to gene encoders: Teddy 1024d vs PCA 50d under STAIG shows only 0.002 ARI difference, demonstrating that spatial graph contrastive learning dominates clustering performance and the impact of gene embedding quality is greatly diluted. Therefore, the bottleneck for optimizing MIHD clustering performance does not lie in the choice of gene encoder. (Source: 2026-03-30) The optimal encoder for single-slice clustering (STAIG/PCA) differs from the optimal encoder for cross-slice retrieval (scGPT): the comparability of embedding spaces (shared coordinate system) is more critical than local quality. Foundation Models naturally suit cross-slice retrieval due to their fixed representation space output, while PCA processes slices independently, producing incomparable coordinate systems. (Source: 2026-03-23) Foundation Models exhibit systematic transfer loss on cross-domain downstream tasks: TEDDY achieves SOTA on disease classification (ACC=0.72) but underperforms PCA on spatial transcriptomics clustering (ARI 0.186 vs 0.255). Root cause is optimization objective mismatch (discriminative representation vs variance maximization), and high-dimensional space (1024d) is unfavorable for KMeans. (Source: 2026-03-30) Domain Knowledge HVG1500 (direct concatenation of highly variable genes without dimensionality reduction) achieves ARI=0.330 on single slices, outperforming all Foundation Models; Leiden clustering improves ARI by ~15% over KMeans on DLPFC, but improvement over STAIG is limited — STAIG’s advantage comes from spatial graph structure and is relatively independent of the clustering algorithm. (Source: 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31) NMI and ARI show inconsistency on HD data: UNI2 vision-only achieves the highest NMI (0.387) but lower ARI than PCA (0.344 vs 0.351); naive fusion (concat/mean) on HD yields ARI (0.19–0.20) lower than unimodal PCA (0.351), indicating information dilution from simple fusion. Both metrics should be reported together. (Source: 2026-03-31) Tools Single-cell foundation model forward interfaces in the HuggingFace ecosystem vary significantly and standard interfaces cannot be assumed: TEDDY uses gene_ids (not input_ids), GeneTokenizer (not Tokenizer), outputs[‘cell_emb’] (not last_hidden_state), and its vocab uses Ensembl IDs (43808 tokens) rather than gene symbols. Source code must be read to confirm. (Source: 2026-03-29) DCC HPC cluster practical notes: SLURM compute nodes require eval $(conda shell.bash hook) && conda activate to initialize conda (source activate does not work); scavenger-h200 provides H200×8 preemptible nodes; account TRES limits are cpu=100/mem=500G; Glob tends to time out on large HPC filesystems — use exact path search instead. (Source: 2026-03-23, 2026-03-31) Debugging Implicit divergence between slice-specific configuration (e.g., staig num_gene) and pipeline global defaults is a high-risk bug type: cache check logic should dynamically resolve actual configuration rather than hardcoding defaults; multi-dataset pipeline image paths should be explicitly differentiated rather than using a unified data_root/section_id logic. (Source: 2026-03-30, 2026-03-31) Token Usage Statistics Daily Cost Trend Date Tokens (millions) Cost ($) 2026-03-23 1.1 1.79 2026-03-29 17.7 10.00 2026-03-30 1.7 1.85 2026-03-31 43.2 25.94 Peak Day: 2026-03-31 — $25.94 / 43.2M tokens

summary: This month’s work was highly focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics multimodal clustering research project: after completing environment initialization on the DCC HPC cluster, we systematically integrated the TEDDY-G 400M foundation model, Leiden clustering algorithm, and STHD pathology annotation Ground Truth. Multi-encoder benchmarks revealed STAIG framework’s high robustness to gene encoders and the systematic transfer loss of Foundation Models on clustering tasks. The paper’s Fig 1–6 framework was finalized, marking the project’s transition from the experimental phase to the paper-writing phase.
title: Monthly Summary 2026-03

content:
Daily Log — 2026-03-31 Today’s Overview What was done: Completed MIHD paper preparation: defined Fig 1-6 framework, integrated Leiden clustering and STHD pathology annotation Ground Truth, ran DLPFC Leiden baseline and HD multimodal evaluation experiments How it was done: Used ccplan to generate a structured weekly plan, parallelized modifications across multiple pipeline modules (evaluation_planner, runner, data_loader), submitted SLURM jobs and fixed conda activation and vision extraction path bugs before switching to local execution Why it matters: Obtained Leiden vs KMeans comparison data (+15% ARI) and HD STHD Ground Truth evaluation baseline, providing quantitative support for paper Figs 2/4; the paper framework is now clear and ready for the writing phase Completed this week’s work plan for the MIHD paper, integrated Leiden clustering and STHD Ground Truth, successfully ran DLPFC Leiden baseline and HD full-pipeline evaluation, obtaining key experimental data
Today’s Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Paper Fig 1-6 Framework Planning — Referenced the structure of four papers (MISO (Nat Methods), STAIG (Nat Comms), QueST (arXiv), Yuan (Nat Methods)) to plan: Fig1=Framework, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=Ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=Cross-section Query, Fig6=Query biological discovery; written into ECL YAML and plans.md ✅ FEAT-003: STHD Ground Truth Integration — Added _load_sthd_annotations() function to automatically load 8um_squares_annotation.csv (545,913 entries); merged CE+Tumor (Neoplasm + Non-neoplastic Epithelium → Epithelium); 17,502/17,502 spots at 100% match, 3 clusters; data_preparer.py saves GT to cache 🔄 FEAT-005: HD Full Fusion Strategy Evaluation — Ran HD pipeline locally, completed 10/13 experiments (pca, mlp, uni2, hipt, resnet50, concat, mean, element_wise_sum, attention, staig_fusion); status of basic_contrastive, adaln_attention, llava_mlp pending confirmation 🔄 FEAT-002: Run Leiden Baseline Experiments — Submitted SLURM job 44829398 (resubmitted after fixing conda activation), totaling 99 experiments (9 combos × 11 sections). 7/9 methods completed; staig_fusion_leiden and llava_mlp_leiden did not finish due to 4h timeout on first submission, second submission with 8h still running Implementation & Fixes ✅ FEAT-001: Leiden Clustering Integrated into Pipeline — Modified utils/clustering.py to add binary search matching target n_clusters; evaluation_planner.py added clustering_method field affecting key/exp_name; runner.py uses job.clustering_method; pipeline_config.yaml added leiden_baseline experiment group ✅ FEAT-004: HD Evaluation Support for ARI/NMI — Modified runner.py to compute ARI/NMI when HD has ground truth, replacing the previous Silhouette-only logic ✅ Fixed HD Vision Extraction Bug — VisionExtractor was not receiving the crop_dir parameter, causing HD vision extraction to fail (could not find cropped_fullres.tif); fixed vision_extractor.py to add crop_dir field and models/vision_extractors.py to add image_dir_override kwarg Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Leiden clustering needs a fair comparison with KMeans, but Leiden uses a resolution parameter to control cluster count and cannot exactly match n_clusters Solution: Implemented binary search in cluster_with_leiden(): iteratively adjusts resolution in the [0.01, 10] range until the cluster count matches, up to 20 iterations
Key Insight: The relationship between Leiden’s resolution and cluster count is monotonic, so binary search can efficiently match the target cluster count for a fair comparison with KMeans
General Issues 2. HD pipeline Vision Extraction failure: VisionExtractor looked for images using the data_root/section_id path, but HD data is in crop_dir, causing 11/13 experiments to fail Solution: Added crop_dir parameter to VisionExtractor.__init__; passed image_dir_override=crop_dir in extract(); _load_image_and_coords() prioritizes image_dir_override; runner.py passes crop_dir when creating VisionExtractor
Key Insight: The image storage structures of DLPFC and HD data are completely different; the pipeline needs to explicitly distinguish image paths for the two datasets
3. SLURM script used source activate General, causing conda environment activation to fail; Leiden experiments all ran on the login node without GPU Solution: Replaced source activate in the SLURM script with eval $(conda shell.bash hook) && conda activate, then resubmitted the job
Key Insight: SLURM nodes on the DCC cluster require explicit conda shell hook initialization; source activate cannot be used directly
4. Default dataset logic error in run_pipeline.py: the presence of the hd_global config section automatically switched to HD dataset, causing Leiden DLPFC experiments to run in HD mode Solution: Changed the default dataset value to DLPFC, switching only when --dataset HD is explicitly specified
Key Insight: The presence of the hd_global section in the config file should not affect the default dataset selection
Human Thinking vs. AI Thinking Strategic Level Paper Fig 1-6 Layout Design Role Approach Human Proposed referencing the structure of MISO, STAIG, and QueST, emphasizing the unique value of biological findings and Query AI Retrieved the specific figure structures from four papers, synthesized a detailed Fig 1-6 layout and section structure, and finalized the plan based on STAIG’s structure Difference Analysis: The human provided creative direction and reference points; AI handled retrieval of details and integration into a concrete plan; the final plan is a collaborative result
CE+Tumor Ground Truth Merge Strategy Role Approach Human Explicitly specified merging Neoplasm (Tumor) and Non-neoplastic Epithelium (CE) into the same cluster as HD evaluation ground truth AI Asked for clarification (whether CE+Tumor should be merged) but could not infer this directly from the original description Difference Analysis: Design decisions driven by domain knowledge (pathology) require human input; AI can implement correctly but needs human clarification of intent
The Meaning of P5 T Cell Role Approach Human P5 = Patient 5 from the STHD paper, not a patient in our own HD dataset AI Mistakenly assumed the HD dataset contained Patient IDs, confused by HD having only a single sample Difference Analysis: The human understood that the Query target references an external publication (STHD paper), not an internal patient grouping within the current dataset
Implementation Level Whether HD Experiments Need a SLURM Job Role Approach Human Directly pointed out that HD data is small (17,502 spots) and can run locally without SLURM AI Defaulted to assuming all experiments require GPU compute resources, first submitted a SLURM job (44830562) Difference Analysis: The human had better intuition about matching data scale to compute resources; AI was overly conservative in using cluster resources; canceling the SLURM job and running locally saved queue wait time
AI Limitations General Limitations Did not account for the structural difference in image paths between DLPFC and HD datasets; the VisionExtractor implementation hardcoded the data_root/section_id lookup logic, requiring human discovery of failure before fixing Misjudged conda activation on HPC environments; the SLURM script used source activate, which does not work on compute nodes, causing environment issues in both first-submission jobs Could not proactively judge whether the 17,502-spot HD dataset requires GPU/SLURM, over-relied on cluster resources, requiring human correction Launched a background agent for web search but wait times were too long, resulting in poor user experience; lacked expectation management for async task duration Today’s Takeaways Core Takeaways Leiden clustering performs comparably to KMeans on HD data (pca_leiden ARI≈0.333 vs pca ARI≈0.288 on DLPFC), with clear improvement on simple gene-only methods, but diminishing returns for graph contrastive learning methods like STAIG — STAIG’s advantage comes from spatial graph structure and is relatively independent of the clustering algorithm Simple fusion strategies (concat/mean/element_wise_sum) on HD have lower ARI than unimodal PCA (0.19–0.20 vs 0.351), suggesting naive fusion may cause information dilution on large-scale HD data; attention and staig_fusion are exceptions (attention ARI=0.342) On HD data (STHD Ground Truth, 3 clusters: Epithelium/Connective Tissue/Smooth Muscle), vision-only UNI2 has the highest NMI (0.387) but lower ARI than PCA (0.344 vs 0.351) — NMI and ARI can be inconsistent across datasets, and both metrics should be reported together MISO (Nat Methods)’s HD section used Visium HD colorectal cancer to demonstrate spatial expression of SPP1 (tumor-associated macrophages) and CEACAM6, which can be directly used as a reference framework for marker gene validation in MIHD Fig 4 Practical Takeaways Matching the STHD annotation file (8um_squares_annotation.csv) to adata obs requires using r_big/c_big coordinates, achieving 100% match rate; after merging CE+Tumor, 3 meaningful clusters are obtained (Outside has only 4 spots and can be ignored) Session Summary ✅ ccplan generated MIHD paper preparation weekly plan (deadline 4/5) 04:10:39.284 | claude_code The user submitted 7 tasks for the week via /ccplan (Leiden baseline, HD pipeline, Query, paper planning, etc.). AI concurrently investigated the codebase and searched for papers, generating a Fig 1-6 layout proposal and a 4-track parallel workflow ECL YAML. After the user approved the plan, AI created 7 tasks with dependencies established, launching three tracks (Leiden/STHD GT/paper structure) in parallel.
✅ FEAT-001/003/004: Leiden Clustering and STHD Ground Truth Integration 04:10:39.284 | claude_code Simultaneously implemented two features — Leiden clustering (binary search to match n_clusters) and STHD pathology annotation loading (CE+Tumor merge) — touching 6 files: clustering.py, evaluation_planner.py, runner.py, data_loader.py, data_preparer.py, and pipeline_config.yaml. All import tests passed, STHD match rate 100% (17,502/17,502). HD load_section_data() correctly returns 3 clusters after integration.
🔄 HD Local Experiments Completed, Results Written to plans.md 04:10:39.284 | claude_code Ran HD pipeline locally (--phase all --override), completing 10/13 experiments. Key results: PCA gene-only ARI=0.351 is best overall, UNI2 vision has the highest NMI (0.387), simple fusion (concat/mean) performs worse than unimodal. Updated plans.md with Leiden comparison results and HD results, adding key findings #6 (Leiden +15% on gene-only) and #7 (HD 3-cluster evaluation established).
🔄 Submitted SLURM Experiment Jobs and Fixed Two Bugs (conda activation + vision path) 04:10:39.284 | claude_code After the first SLURM job submission (Leiden 44767007, HD 44767008), conda activation failure and HD vision extraction path errors were discovered. After fixing, resubmitted Leiden (44829398, 8h); after the user pointed out that HD data is small enough to run locally, the HD SLURM job (44830562) was canceled and execution switched to local.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-31

content:
Daily Log — 2026-03-30 Today’s Overview What was done: Integrated TEDDY-G 400M into the MIHD pipeline, completed gene embedding extraction for all 11 DLPFC slices, evaluated 5 non-STAIG fusion strategies, and ran STAIG fusion experiments How it was done: Extracted embeddings slice-by-slice in a dedicated conda teddy environment and saved them in standard cache format, fixed a false-positive cache bug for staig_gene_feat in evaluation_planner.py, and ran Phase 1 extraction and Phase 2 evaluation in stages Why it matters: Confirmed that Teddy+UNI2+STAIG (ARI≈0.495) and PCA+UNI2+STAIG (ARI≈0.497) perform comparably, revealing the robustness of the STAIG framework to gene encoder choice and providing experimental evidence for future encoder replacement strategies Completed TEDDY-G 400M gene foundation model embedding extraction across all 11 DLPFC slices with multi-strategy fusion benchmarking, and wrote the W13 weekly report summarizing last week’s experimental findings.
Today’s Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Teddy + STAIG Fusion Evaluation — Completed evaluation on 10/11 slices (151676 failed due to model collapse); Avg ARI=0.495, vs. 0.497 for PCA+STAIG; Teddy outperformed on NMI for 6/10 slices ✅ Full TEDDY Embedding Extraction (11 slices) — Extracted 1024d gene embeddings for all 11 DLPFC slices (151508–151676) in the conda teddy environment and cached them to embeddings_cache/gene/teddy/; total size 185MB ✅ Teddy Non-STAIG Fusion Evaluation (5 strategies × 11 slices) — Evaluated 5 configurations in Phase 2: teddy_only, teddy_uni2_concat, teddy_uni2_mean, teddy_uni2_attention, teddy_uni2_llava_mlp; results: gene-only ARI=0.186, best llava_mlp ARI=0.305 ✅ UNI2 staig_strict Visual Feature Extraction — Extracted UNI2 staig_strict variant (256×256 patches, no ImageNet normalization, frequency-domain filtering) for STAIG fusion across all 11 slices Implementation & Fixes ✅ Wrote W13 Weekly Report — Synthesized docs/plans.md, git history, and file timestamps to write docs/WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W13.md, summarizing last week’s two main experiments and core conclusions ✅ Fixed evaluation_planner.py staig_gene_feat False-Positive Bug — Slice 151676 uses 5000 genes (staig_gene_feat_5000.npz), but the planner hard-coded a check for 3000, causing sys.exit(1); fixed by dynamically querying the actual file ✅ TEDDY Foundation Model Background Research — Via web search, compiled a complete technical overview of the TEDDY paper (arXiv:2503.03485): bidirectional Transformer + supervised ontology pretraining (disease/tissue/cell type), trained on 116M cells, available in 5 scales (10M–400M) ✅ TokenMonitor HPC Build Feasibility Assessment — Inspected the RHEL 9.7 HPC environment: Node.js/npm available, but Rust and Tauri dependencies such as webkit2gtk4.1-devel are missing from system libraries, and no sudo access is available, making an HPC build infeasible Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Teddy gene-only ARI (0.186) underperforms PCA (0.255) — Foundation Model fails to beat a simple baseline on clustering Solution: Analyzed root causes: Teddy is optimized for disease classification, not variance maximization; the 1024d high-dimensional space degrades KMeans performance; training data is dissociated scRNA-seq, with a distribution mismatch from spatial transcriptomics
Key Insight: There is a fundamental tension between the “general representation” of foundation model embeddings and “clustering adaptability”: PCA directly maximizes data variance and is naturally suited for KMeans, whereas Foundation Models learn disease-discriminative representations, incurring systematic transfer loss on downstream clustering tasks
General Issues 2. evaluation_planner.py false-positive on staig_gene_feat cache check for 151676, causing Phase 2 to terminate with sys.exit(1) Solution: Modified the has_staig_gene_feat check logic to dynamically resolve the actual top_genes count from the slice configuration (151676 uses 5000 vs. the default 3000), correctly recognizing the cache as present
Key Insight: Slice-specific configuration (top_genes from the num_gene field in staig_utils.py) diverging silently from the planner’s hard-coded defaults is a category of “implicit configuration fork” that easily produces hard-to-detect false-negative checks
3. Model collapse on slice 151676 during STAIG fusion evaluation, producing no valid clustering result Solution: Skipped the slice and reported results averaged over 10/11 slices (ARI=0.495)
Key Insight: 151676 is a known difficult slice for STAIG; its collapse is unrelated to the gene encoder and is a systematic issue driven by the data characteristics of that particular slice
4. Cannot build TokenMonitor on HPC (missing Rust, webkit2gtk4.1-devel, and other Tauri dependencies; no sudo access) Solution: Proposed three paths: build on a local machine with sufficient privileges and copy the binary, ask HPC administrators to install dependencies, or build a Linux release package via GitHub Actions CI
Key Insight: Tauri applications depend on GTK/WebKit system libraries that are typically unavailable in HPC environments, and the module system does not provide such libraries either; HPC systems are fundamentally not a target deployment environment for GUI applications
Human Thinking vs. AI Thinking Strategic Level Whether to proceed with STAIG fusion after non-STAIG results were available Role Reasoning Human After seeing that the best non-STAIG llava_mlp ARI was only 0.305 and all other fusions underperformed PCA, proactively requested additional STAIG fusion experiments AI When presenting non-STAIG results, AI only provided a list of options without proactively recommending a priority order Analysis: The human had prior knowledge about which fusion strategy was most likely to approach SOTA (STAIG being the known strongest), and therefore pushed forward proactively; AI tends to defer decisions to the user without anticipating outcomes
Implementation Level Discovery and fix of the staig_gene_feat false-positive bug Role Reasoning Human Not involved; the bug was entirely discovered and fixed by AI AI AI traced the sys.exit trigger path, inspected the actual file for 151676 (5000.npz vs. 3000.npz), identified the implicit configuration fork, and applied a fix Analysis: This was a proactive AI contribution: the human user did not notice the bug; AI independently discovered and fixed it while diagnosing the Phase 2 failure
Approach to reconstructing last week’s work Role Reasoning Human Asked “what was the plan last week and what got done,” expecting an integrated narrative AI AI systematically combined docs/plans.md planning documents, git log timeline, find file timestamps, and experiment results from the outputs directory to reconstruct a full picture of last week’s work from multiple dimensions Analysis: The human’s question was high-level; AI decomposed it systematically into three layers — “planned vs. actually executed vs. incomplete” — supported by quantitative results
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Did not deeply analyze the root cause of STAIG model collapse on slice 151676; attributed it solely to “known difficult slice” without attempting hyperparameter adjustments (e.g., tau, num_epochs) to work around it General Limitations Did not proactively identify the hard-coded staig_gene_feat top_genes issue in evaluation_planner.py; only traced back to the root cause after sys.exit was actually triggered Background task progress monitoring strategy was inefficient: used multiple rounds of sleep+ls polling rather than more elegant solutions (e.g., inotifywait or structured progress files), resulting in excessive wait time Encountered syntax errors (unterminated string literal) when constructing multi-line Python f-strings in bash commands, requiring command modifications before execution Today’s Takeaways Core Takeaways The STAIG fusion framework is highly robust to gene encoder choice: Teddy 1024d (ARI=0.495) and PCA 50d (ARI=0.497) perform nearly identically under the STAIG framework, indicating that STAIG’s spatial graph contrastive learning mechanism dominates clustering performance and substantially dilutes the impact of gene embedding quality Systematic transfer loss of Foundation Models on cross-domain downstream tasks: Teddy achieves SOTA on disease classification (ACC=0.72), but underperforms PCA on spatial transcriptomics clustering (0.186 vs. 0.255); the root cause is a mismatch in optimization objectives (discriminative vs. variance maximization) HVG1500 (raw highly variable genes concatenated directly, no dimensionality reduction) achieves ARI=0.330 on single slice 151673, outperforming all Foundation Models; this suggests that high-dimensional raw features may benefit certain slices by preserving more local variance information Practical Takeaways Implicit forks between slice-specific configuration (e.g., STAIG num_gene) and pipeline global defaults are a high-risk bug category; cache check logic should resolve these dynamically rather than hard-coding values Session Summaries MIHD Pipeline ✅ TEDDY-G 400M Full DLPFC Benchmark: Embedding Extraction → Multi-Fusion Evaluation → STAIG Fusion 00:01:05.759 | claude_code Extracted TEDDY 1024d embeddings slice-by-slice on DCC using the conda teddy environment (11 slices total, ~185MB), then ran 5 non-STAIG fusion strategy evaluations (gene-only ARI=0.186, llava_mlp best ARI=0.305). User requested additional STAIG fusion; AI extracted UNI2 staig_strict visual features, discovered and fixed the evaluation_planner.py staig_gene_feat 5000 vs. 3000 false-positive bug, and ultimately completed Teddy+STAIG evaluation on 10/11 slices (ARI=0.495), comparable to PCA+STAIG (ARI=0.497).
✅ W13 MIHD Experimental Work Review and Weekly Report Generation 21:13:39.566 | claude_code User asked about last week’s plans and actual work. AI synthesized docs/plans.md, git log (most recent commit at 2026-02-22, no new commits), file timestamps, and experiment results to reconstruct W13’s two main workstreams: 8-encoder benchmark (single slice 151673) and Teddy full-scale fusion experiments. Then used this as the basis to write docs/WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W13.md, including experimental objectives, methods, results, and core conclusions.
✅ TEDDY Foundation Model Technical Background Research and Overview 22:58:07.566 | claude_code User requested a detailed overview of the TEDDY foundation model. AI used web search to compile complete technical details from the paper (arXiv:2503.03485, Merck/BCG X): bidirectional Transformer BERT-style architecture, 5 parameter scales (10M–400M), two variants TEDDY-G/TEDDY-X, supervised ontology pretraining (disease/tissue/cell type/sex via four special tokens), 116M cell training data, and benchmarks against scGPT/Geneformer. Integration paths and limitations in the context of the MIHD project were also discussed.
TokenMonitor 🔍 Feasibility Assessment for Building TokenMonitor on HPC (RHEL 9.7) 21:22:13.566 | claude_code User asked about current system info and whether TokenMonitor could be built. Inspection found Node.js/npm available, but Rust/Cargo not installed, Tauri-required system libraries such as webkit2gtk4.1-devel missing, no relevant modules in the module system, and no pre-built releases on GitHub. Recommended installing Rust via rustup, contacting HPC administrators to install system dependencies, or building a Linux package via GitHub Actions CI; noted that running this GUI application on an HPC login node itself requires X11/Wayland support.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-30

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W13 (2026-03-23 ~ 2026-03-29) This week’s core work focused on multi-model embedding expansion and issue triage for the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. On March 23, environment initialization was completed on the DCC HPC cluster, and historical challenges in the MIHD project were systematically documented — for the first time structuring the root cause of cross-section retrieval failure (incomparable embedding spaces) and the single-section ARI gap as formal documents. On March 29, four critical bugs in the TEDDY foundation model extraction script were successfully fixed, the first DLPFC section embedding extraction was completed, and batch runs were launched, establishing the foundation for a multi-model comparison benchmark.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-03-23 ~ 2026-03-29 Active Days 2 / 7 Total Conversations 6 Projects Involved 3 Completed Tasks 6 In-Progress Tasks 1 Total Tokens 18,874,903 Total Cost $11.79 Daily Average Cost $5.89 Project Progress MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics Multimodal Clustering (2 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Systematically reviewed historical issues and persisted them as docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md, including root cause analysis of cross-section retrieval failure Identified root cause of cross-section task failure: PCA/STAIG independent processing produces incomparable embedding coordinate systems; scGPT shared space achieves average SL@50=0.416 Fixed 4 critical bugs in the extract_teddy() script, successfully completed TEDDY embedding extraction for section 151508 (4384 spots) Launched batch TEDDY embedding extraction for the remaining 10 DLPFC sections, establishing a four-model comparison framework: scGPT/Geneformer/C2S/TEDDY Blockers:
⚠️ Single-section ARI remains below target (current 0.546 vs. target 0.60+); 4-phase ablation experiment plan not yet executed ⚠️ Root cause of mclust falling back to KMeans due to zero-variance embedding in section 151676 unknown ⚠️ Batch TEDDY embedding extraction (10 sections) still running as of March 29 DCC HPC Cluster Environment Initialization (1 active day) — ✅ completed Completed:
Configured rclone sync.json; completed sync of 4 data categories (summarize/research/test/website) via sync.py pull Investigated GPU resources: common (RTX 2080), scavenger-gpu (A6000/RTX 5000-6000 Ada), scavenger-h200 (H200×8) Installed cchelper skill to ~/.claude/skills/cchelper/ Key Tasks ✅ Fixed 4 critical bugs in extract_teddy() script (2026-03-29) — Discovered and fixed via systematic source code review: ① incorrect import of Tokenizer changed to GeneTokenizer; ② model forward parameter input_ids changed to gene_ids; ③ output access last_hidden_state changed to outputs[‘cell_emb’]; ④ added gene symbol to Ensembl ID mapping logic. ✅ MIHD project issue review and documentation (2026-03-23) — Reviewed current state of the MIHD project (best ARI 0.546, root cause of cross-section retrieval failure, list of fixed bugs), generated structured issue summary document docs/PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md. ✅ TEDDY paper analysis and architecture evaluation (2026-03-29) — Analyzed arXiv 2503.03485, reviewed TEDDY model architecture (Teddy-G/X two encoding types, 6 variants, up to 400M parameters), evaluated performance on disease classification benchmarks (held-out donor multi-class classification 8% higher than Nicheformer, linear probe far superior to fine-tuning). 🔄 DLPFC embedding batch extraction (2026-03-29) — Extracting 1024-dimensional cell embeddings for 11 DLPFC sections in the teddy conda environment; section 151508 complete, remaining 10 sections still running. Issues and Solutions 1. TEDDY extraction script used a non-existent Tokenizer class and incorrect model API (input_ids, last_hidden_state), making the script completely non-functional [MIHD] (2026-03-29) Solution: Read TEDDY source code to confirm correct API: GeneTokenizer, gene_ids parameter, outputs[‘cell_emb’] output key, and added Ensembl ID mapping (60% of genes in the DLPFC dataset are mappable)
2. MIHD cross-section retrieval almost completely failed (10 out of 14 query directions with SL@50=0) [MIHD] (2026-03-23) Solution: Diagnosed root cause as PCA/STAIG independent processing per section producing incomparable embedding spaces; switched to scGPT embeddings (shared space), achieving average SL@50=0.416 with 100% hit rate
3. TEDDY inference with batch_size=32 caused GPU OOM (32GB VRAM), because each sequence is up to 2048 tokens long [MIHD] (2026-03-29) Solution: Reduced batch_size from 32 to 8, bringing memory usage under control
4. New DCC machine had no ~/.config/gadget/sync.json, preventing sync.py from running [DCC Environment Initialization] (2026-03-23) Solution: Inferred rclone_remote configuration from existing summarize config, manually created sync.json and verified remote directory structure before executing pull
Learnings Domain Knowledge (domain) In cross-section tasks in spatial transcriptomics, the comparability of embedding spaces (shared coordinate system) is more critical than local embedding quality. Foundation models (scGPT/TEDDY) are naturally suited for cross-section retrieval due to their fixed representation spaces; PCA/STAIG independent processing produces incomparable coordinate systems and is not suitable for cross-section tasks. The optimal method for single-section clustering (STAIG) and the optimal method for cross-section retrieval (scGPT) are different; different encoders should be selected for different task objectives, and a unified optimal solution should not be pursued. TEDDY-G (rank-value encoding) outperforms TEDDY-X (binned expression); linear probe far outperforms fine-tuning (RA: 0.56→0.93), indicating high embedding quality but susceptibility to overfitting during fine-tuning; 8% higher than Nicheformer on held-out donor multi-class classification. Tools (tools) Single-cell foundation models in the HuggingFace ecosystem (TEDDY, scGPT, etc.) have significantly different forward interfaces; standard HuggingFace interfaces cannot be assumed. Source code must be read to confirm the gene_ids parameter name and output dict structure. TEDDY’s vocab uses Ensembl IDs (43808 tokens) rather than gene symbols. Architecture (architecture) TEDDY uses rank-value encoding, retaining the top 2048 genes as a token sequence. The sequence length is longer than models like scGPT, resulting in higher VRAM requirements and the need for smaller batch sizes during inference. AI Usage Notes Effective patterns:
✓ Systematic code review (read source → verify API → compare implementation), proactively discovering hidden bugs unknown to the user, saving debugging time ✓ Choosing to confirm rather than guess when requirements are ambiguous (private file sync requirements), avoiding incorrect execution ✓ Persisting verbal analysis results as structured documents (PROBLEM_SUMMARY.md), completing knowledge externalization with user initiative Limitations:
✗ Cross-session memory not persisted: unable to recall previous TEDDY test discussions, requiring users to re-provide background information ✗ PDF parsing failure: unable to process binary content when directly fetching arXiv PDFs, requiring fallback to HTML version ✗ Relies on existing documentation for project summaries; cannot infer undocumented implicit progress or autonomously prioritize ablation experiments Outlook for Next Week Next week’s priorities: ① Confirm completion of batch TEDDY embedding extraction (10 DLPFC sections) and integrate four-model (scGPT/Geneformer/C2S/TEDDY) comparative evaluation results; ② Execute the 4-phase ablation experiments in the MIHD single-section ARI improvement plan, focusing on diagnosing the root cause of zero-variance embeddings in section 151676; ③ Based on multi-model comparison results, decide whether a combined encoder strategy is needed (scGPT for cross-section, STAIG for single-section clustering).

title: Weekly Summary 2026-W13

content:
Daily Log — 2026-03-29 Today’s Overview What was done: Successfully deployed the TEDDY-G 400M foundation model on the DLPFC spatial transcriptomics dataset, completing the repair and validation of the embedding extraction pipeline How it was done: Identified 4 bugs in the existing extract_teddy() function through systematic code review — tokenizer import error, wrong model parameter name, wrong output key, and missing gene ID mapping — fixed them one by one, then validated in the teddy conda environment and launched batch runs Why it matters: Added a TEDDY encoder to the MIHD project, forming a comparative baseline alongside existing models such as scGPT, Geneformer, and C2S, advancing multi-model evaluation for disease state classification tasks Analyzed the TEDDY single-cell foundation model paper on an HPC cluster, fixed 4 critical bugs in the existing extraction script, and successfully deployed embedding extraction for the DLPFC dataset.
Today’s Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Fixed extract_teddy() script bugs — Identified and fixed 4 critical bugs: ① incorrect Tokenizer import replaced with GeneTokenizer; ② model forward parameter input_ids changed to gene_ids; ③ output access last_hidden_state changed to outputs['cell_emb']; ④ added gene symbol to Ensembl ID mapping logic ✅ TEDDY paper analysis — Retrieved and analyzed arXiv 2503.03485, reviewing the TEDDY model architecture (two encoding variants Teddy-G/X, 6 variants, up to 400M parameters), and evaluated its performance on disease classification benchmarks and comparison with SOTA models Implementation & Fixes 🔄 DLPFC embedding extraction — Extracted 1024-dimensional cell embeddings for 11 DLPFC sections in the teddy conda environment; section 151508 completed (4,384 spots), remaining 10 sections running in batch. GPU OOM issue resolved by reducing batch_size from 32 to 8 Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. extract_teddy() used a non-existent Tokenizer class and incorrect model API (input_ids, last_hidden_state), causing the script to fail entirely Solution: Read the Teddy source code to confirm the correct API: GeneTokenizer, gene_ids parameter, outputs['cell_emb'] output key, and added Ensembl ID mapping
Key insight: TEDDY vocab uses Ensembl IDs rather than gene symbols, and the model forward signature differs from the standard HuggingFace interface — reading the source directly is required rather than relying on convention
General Issues 2. GPU OOM at batch_size=32 (32GB VRAM) because each sequence is up to 2048 tokens long Solution: Reduced batch_size from 32 to 8, bringing memory usage under control
Key insight: Rank-value encoding retains the top 2048 genes as the token sequence, which is longer than models like scGPT and requires more VRAM
3. Claude could not recall the conversation from the previous Teddy testing session Solution: User re-provided the arXiv link and Claude re-analyzed the paper
Key insight: Persistent memory across sessions was not properly saved, causing context loss — important research checkpoints require proactively saving memory
Human Thinking vs. AI Thinking Strategic Level Identifying script bugs Role Approach Human User was unaware of the bugs, assuming the script may have been tested previously AI AI proactively discovered 4 hidden bugs through systematic code review (read source → verify API → compare implementation), without user involvement in debugging Differential analysis: AI’s proactive inspection at the code level helped uncover technical blockers unknown to the user, saving debugging time
Determining task priorities and research direction Role Approach Human User proactively recalled and followed up on previous Teddy testing progress, explicitly requested deploying to the DLPFC dataset, and provided the specific arXiv link AI AI focused on the technical implementation layer: paper analysis, environment exploration, bug localization, and code fixing, advancing per user instructions Differential analysis: Research direction and project selection were entirely user-driven; AI was responsible for translating a vague requirement (“deploy Teddy”) into concrete, executable engineering steps
AI Limitations General Limitations Cross-session memory not persisted: Unable to recall the user’s previous discussion about Teddy testing, requiring the user to re-provide context — reflects the session-isolation constraint of Claude’s memory system PDF parsing failure: Attempting to fetch the arXiv PDF directly resulted in inability to parse binary content; falling back to the HTML version succeeded — indicates limited ability to directly process PDF format Today’s Takeaways Core Insights TEDDY-G outperforms TEDDY-X (rank-value encoding outperforms binned expression), and achieves 8% higher accuracy than Nicheformer on held-out donor multi-class tasks; however, results are mixed against Nicheformer on held-out disease binary classification; linear probe greatly outperforms fine-tuning (RA: 0.56→0.93), indicating high embedding quality but susceptibility to overfitting during fine-tuning Single-cell foundation models in the HuggingFace ecosystem (TEDDY, scGPT, etc.) have significantly different forward interfaces — standard HuggingFace interfaces cannot be assumed; source code must be read to confirm the gene_ids parameter name and output dict structure Practical Takeaways TEDDY vocab uses Ensembl IDs (43,808 tokens); 60% of genes in the DLPFC dataset (20,044/33,538) can be mapped to the vocab, with the remainder being lncRNAs and other non-protein-coding genes — this is a normal coverage rate Session Summary 🔄 TEDDY paper analysis + fixing DLPFC embedding extraction script and launching batch runs 21:19:18.201 | claude_code User asked about previous Teddy testing progress; Claude, lacking memory of that session, asked the user to provide the arXiv link before re-analyzing the paper. The user then used the /ccplan command to request deploying Teddy to the DLPFC dataset. After systematically exploring the HPC environment, Claude discovered 4 critical bugs in the existing extract_teddy() function (wrong tokenizer class name, wrong model parameter name, wrong output key, missing gene ID mapping), fixed and validated each one. The first section (151508) successfully extracted 1024-dimensional embeddings for 4,384 spots; the GPU OOM issue was resolved by reducing batch_size from 32 to 8; the remaining 10 sections are running in batch.
