title: 错误日志 2026-03-19

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-18 Today’s Highlights What was done: Parallelized progress across DCC, tianhe, and local devices for MIHD cross-slice alignment/batch effect evaluation. Completed the implementation of π₀.₅ task completion detection header with a full-linkage approach, repaired the VLA evaluation pipeline and expanded experiments (manip_progress logging/LIBERO environment/Exp5-9). Added BATCH_UPDATE support to CalendarPro’s batch intent recognition for four independent research lines. How it was done: MIHD used harmonypy post-processing for alignment + pure Python batch effect metrics rewriting + full-linkage pipeline integration. π₀.₅ adopted a JAX/NNX inheritance model to maintain checkpoint path compatibility, fixing the root cause of progress fields being silently discarded due to output transform data flow issues. CalendarPro added BATCH_UPDATE support at the routing-classification-processing full linkage stage and used keyword counting rules to compensate for embedding blind spots. What’s achieved: MIHD cross-slice alignment achieved end-to-end integration (Harmony improved batch_entropy from 0.33 to 0.52); π₀.₅ task completion detection header training was officially launched (loss ≈ 0.253), and the VLA progress evaluation foundation was established. Exp5-9 system covered five conditioning strategies; CalendarPro’s batch task status natural language interaction issues were resolved. DCC What was done: Completed the full implementation of MIHD cross-slice Embedding alignment (HarmonyAligner+JointSTAIGAligner) and batch effect evaluation metrics (ASW_batch/batch_entropy/batch_kl/graph_connectivity). Fixed alignment propagation bugs and completed end-to-end verification. Updated CLAUDE.md in parallel. How it was done: Created new files utils/batch_metrics.py and pipeline/alignment.py, modified six files including cache_manager/evaluation_planner/runner/run_pipeline to complete full-linkage integration. Identified and fixed three critical bugs where alignment parameters were not injected into the evaluate stage under all_aligned mode. Run three verification experiments with real DLPFC data. What’s achieved: End-to-end alignment process was completed, Harmony improved batch_entropy from 0.33 to 0.52 (batch_kl reduced from 0.39 to 0.25), and the core bug causing alignment function to fail completely was fixed. tianhe What was done: Completed the full implementation of π₀.₅ task completion detection header from design to training launch. Fixed the root cause of manip_progress fields being silently discarded in the VLA evaluation pipeline and improved sinusoidal encoding. Fixed missing registration of libero_object_com and multiple blocking issues in the evaluation environment. Designed experiments with five conditioning strategies for Exp5-9; updated the error-recovery-benchmark CLAUDE.md document. How it was done: Maintained checkpoint path compatibility using JAX/NNX inheritance mode (not combination). Traced RobotwinOutputs.call to locate the root cause of progress being discarded. Traced Python module loading paths across repositories to identify the root cause of LIBERO multi-path contamination. Added a cond_mode field in pi0_config.py to support four modes: from_pred,/from_hidden, Sinusoidal, and detach. What’s achieved: π₀.₅ completion detection header training started normally (loss ≈ 0.253), progress logging was established for the pipeline; the LIBERO evaluation environment could be reproduced; Exp5-9 expanded experimental space covering end-to-end gradients, intermediate layer conditioning, detached conditioning, etc. TzJsDesktop What was done: Implemented BATCH_UPDATE intent for CalendarPro to solve the core issue where the system returned “I’m not sure” when users reported batch task status. Updated the CLAUDE.md document in parallel. How it was done: Analyzed the root cause chain (low similarity between semantic routing and LLM classifier → LLM classifier had no such intent → clarification branch discarded AI response output硬编码 prompt). Modified eight files across three layers: semantic routing/LLM classifier/producer. Added keyword counting rules (2+completion verbs → +0.30 boost), all 21 new tests + 72 related tests passed. What’s achieved: Fixed high-frequency daily interaction scenarios. Users can update complete/cancel/reschedule/note operations in batches via natural language. The GENERAL fallback was improved to prevent substantial LLM responses from being discarded. Parallelized four research lines across DCC, tianhe, and local devices: MIHD cross-slice Embedding alignment and batch effect evaluation system were fully implemented in DCC; π₀.₅ task completion detection header training was launched in tianhe, the VLA evaluation pipeline’s manip_progress missing issue was fixed, LIBERO environment repairs were completed, and Exp5-9 experimental configurations were designed. CalendarPro’s batch task status updates were implemented locally to fix core issues in high-frequency daily interactions.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ MIHD Batch Effect Evaluation Metrics Implementation (utils/batch_metrics.py) — Created new utils/batch_metrics.py, using sklearn.neighbors.NearestNeighbors pure Python to implement ASW_batch, batch_entropy, batch_kl, graph_connectivity four cross-slice batch effect quantification metrics, independent of R packages. ✅ MIHD Cross-Slice Embedding Alignment Implementation (pipeline/alignment.py) — Created new pipeline/alignment.py, implemented HarmonyAligner (harmonypy post-processing alignment) and JointSTAIGAligner (block-diagonal multi-slice joint training). Modified cache_manager/evaluation_planner/runner/run_pipeline to complete full-linkage integration. ✅ π₀.₅ Task Completion Detection Header Design and Implementation — Full-linkage completion: Determined prefix_output mean pooling as the feature source during design phase, reused freeze_filter+nnx.DiffState mechanism; implemented CompletionHead/Pi0WithCompletionHead (inheritance method)/train_completion_head.py, fixed dataset key name mapping (observation.task_completed) and checkpoint path issues. Training started normally (loss ≈ 0.253, parameter freezing verification correct). ✅ π₀.₅ Exp5-9 Experimental Configuration Design and Implementation — Added a cond_mode field to pi0_config.py, refactored pi0.py’s compute_progress return (cond, pred). Added five experiments in config.py: Exp5(from_hidden+last_token), Exp6(from_hidden+special_token), Exp7(sinusoidal+last_token), Exp8(sinusoidal+special_token), Exp9(detach_cond+last_token). ✅ CalendarPro BATCH_UPDATE Intent Implementation — Modified eight files to add BATCH_UPDATE enumeration, semantic routing (21 utterances), keyword counting rules (2+completion verbs → +0.30 boost), LLM prompt schema, handler (support for complete/cancel/reschedule/note). Fixed GENERAL fallback to prevent substantial LLM responses from being discarded; all 21 new tests + 72 related tests passed. ✅ MIHD Alignment Propagation Bug Fixing and End-to-End Verification — Fixed three critical bugs where –alignment parameter was not injected into the evaluate stage under all_aligned mode (3 lines of code). Ran three end-to-end verification experiments: Harmony (6/6 successful), baseline batch metrics (CSV generated correctly), RM-IDEAL+batch_metrics, confirming the entire process was correct. ✅ manipProgress Reasoning Log Implementation and Output Transform Fixing — Implemented writing progress/episodeN.txt after each episode evaluation (step index as actual action step N×chunk_size). Identified and fixed the root cause: RobotwinOutputs.call only returned {actions} field, silently discarding manip_progress, making upstream prediction results completely invisible to external parties. ✅ π₀.₅ Progress Conditioning Improvement (clip + Sinusoidal Encoding) — Added clip to [0,1] after PredictProgress output to prevent outliers; changed scalar→linear(1024) to scalar→sinusoidal(1024)→linear(1024→1024) for more reasonable encoding. Implementation and Fixes ✅ MIHD CLAUDE.md Audit Improvement — Audited CLAUDE.md through Explore agent, fixed STAIG num_epochs document error (550→300), added information on automatic vision variant selection mechanism, scGPT integration path, NumPy vs PyTorch fusion distinction. ✅ LIBERO Evaluation Environment Fixes (Missing Registration + Multiple Environmental Blockages) — Fixed libero_object_com not being registered under the openpi/third_party/libero path (confirmed correct location through three path tracings). Fixed serve_policy.py container hostname DNS parsing failure, main.py client host default value error (0.0.0.0→127.0.0.1); resolved MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID conflicts with container GPU isolation; optimized rollout videos by saving tasks in subfolders. ✅ error-recovery-benchmark CLAUDE.md Documentation Update — Added explanations for recovery_types.py/recovery_segmenter.py modules, expanded env_wrapper.py method list, added new recovery_collection.yaml configuration item, added Sawyer gripper normalization trap explanation, compressed redundant Slurm code blocks. ✅ CalendarPro CLAUDE.md Documentation Improvement — Analyzed the codebase through .init command, removed enumeration lists that could be directly found from the directory, added step-by-step guidance for multi-file changes (adding intent types, AI providers, backend services), and supplemented with singleton test isolation notes. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. π₀.₅ Pi0WithCompletionHead uses a combination method (self.pi0 = Pi0(…)), causing all parent module parameter paths to have an extra pi0/ prefix. When merging pytree, it throws a ‘2 children vs 1 child’ ValueError, making checkpoint loading impossible. Solution: Changed to inheritance method (class Pi0WithCompletionHead(Pi0)). Pi0 parameters were directly aligned with the checkpoint path at the top level; completion_head retained random initialization through missing_regex.
Key Insight: The checkpoint path for Flax NNX was determined by the module tree structure: Combination added a prefix before all parent module parameter paths, while inheritance did not—requiring loading of existing checkpoints’ extended models. Inheritance was the only feasible solution.
2. Robotwin output transform silently discards manipProgress: RobotwinOutputs.call only returns {“actions”:…}. Even if the model correctly predicts the progress field, it cannot be accessed externally, and the eval txt file remains empty. Solution: Modified the output transform’s return dict to include the manipProgress field, re-establishing data flow.
Key Insight: The output transform is an implicit filter in the reasoning chain; any field not in the return dict is silently discarded. To troubleshoot such bugs, trace upstream from the end of the data flow instead of assuming correct output upstream.
3. CalendarPro system returns ‘I’m not sure what you want to do’ when users report batch task status, rather than processing the request. Solution: Analyzed the entire root cause chain: Low similarity between semantic routing and LLM classifier (≈0.20 vs 0.50 threshold) → LLM classifier had no batch_update intent → GENERAL handler clarification branch discarded AI response output hard-coded prompt. Added BATCH_UPDATE support at the routing-classification-processing full linkage stage, with keyword counting rules (2+completion verbs → +0.30 boost) to compensate for embedding blind spots.
Key Insight: The single-intent architecture naturally has low similarity in embedding multi-task messages; pure semantic routing is insufficient. GENERAL fallback should first determine whether the LLM response is substantial content (>20 words and not a template sentence) before deciding whether to use it.
4. In all_aligned mode of MIHD, the –alignment parameter was not injected into the evaluate stage, resulting in EvaluationJob.alignment being always None, and Harmony alignment results being completely ignored. Solution: Added three lines of code before the run_pipeline.py evaluate phase: When phase == ‘all_aligned’ and args.alignment has a value, inject the alignment into extra_config[‘alignment’] for each experiment.
Key Insight: The isolation design between CLI parameters and experimental configurations in multi-stage pipelines can cause data flow disruptions when introducing new stages. Each new parameter across stages requires checking the entire data flow path.
5. The semantics of prefix_output in pi0.5 are unclear: The documentation does not explain the physical meaning of prefix/suffix. Current code discards prefix_output, making it difficult to determine the appropriate feature source for classification. Solution: Confirmed through code exploration that prefix=[BOS]+image+language global token, suffix=action expert and VLM interaction token; mean pooling semantics of prefix_output is clear for classification tasks.
Key Insight: Analyzing the feature boundaries of VLM-Action expert is a key prerequisite for designing classification headers. It’s necessary to read the entire forward call chain.
6. Explanation ambiguity in ‘multiple forward passes’ during training with from_hidden mode: Users cannot understand the difference between training and reasoning calculations multiple times. Solution: Compared and analyzed to clarify the mechanism: Reasoning always involves two steps; during training, Exp1-4 can use teacher forcing (teacher labels known in advance) to combine prefix+suffix into a single joint forward optimization; from_hidden mode introduces cyclic dependencies due to conditioning dependent on model output, making this optimization impossible. An additional prefix-only forward is required.
Key Insight: The calculation diagrams for training and reasoning are different: Teacher forcing makes conditioning a constant that can be used for joint forward optimization; from_hidden mode introduces cyclic dependencies, requiring returning to the same two-step process as reasoning.
7. STAIG original batch effect metrics (batch_kl, batch_entropy) depend on R package nabor and cannot run in a general Python environment. Solution: Replaced R nabor with sklearn.neighbors.NearestNeighbors, rewriting all kNN query logic in pure Python.
Key Insight: Language-dependent dependencies (Python↔R) are common friction points in ML projects. Python alternatives are usually available and easier to maintain.
General Issues 8. Multi-path contamination in LIBERO caused registration repairs not to take effect: There were three LIBERO paths in PYTHONPATH, the wrong path was modified, but what actually loaded was openpi/third_party/libero. Solution: Used python -c 'import libero.libero.benchmark; print(__file__)' to confirm the actual loading path at runtime. Modified and copied the bddl file to that path.
Key Insight: When multiple versions of packages coexist, the editable install .pth file may not work because MAPPING is empty. The actual loading order is determined by PYTHONPATH and must be confirmed at runtime.
9. RepackTransform reported a KeyError. In the dataset, the task completion label actually has the key name observation.task_completed instead of task_completed.Solution: Change the mapping in config.py from ’task_completed’:’task_completed’ to ’task_completed’:‘observation.task_completed’ Key Insight: The LeRobot dataset key names use dot-separated nested paths, and the value field of RepackTransform must exactly match the original dataset path
10. When manually exporting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1 in K8s containers, JAX reports a cuInit failure with no visible GPU devices Solution: Remove the manual setting and use the container’s default allocation: The container actually only allocates physical GPU 1, forcing the inclusion of GPU 0 results in a complete failure of cuInit due to lack of access rights
Key Insight: CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES within K8s containers can only be remapped within the allocated devices. Use jax.devices() instead of nvidia-smi to verify the actual visible devices at the framework level
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level π₀.₅ Task Completion Detection Head Architecture Design Decisions (Config Class Design + Inheritance vs Composition) Role Thought Human Humans actively pointed out that TrainConfig already has a freeze_filter mechanism that can be directly reused, without the need to create a new Config class; immediately realized the difference in checkpoint path caused by inheritance/composition after seeing the pytree structure error AI AI’s initial proposal was to create a new independent TaskCompleteHeadConfig class (more formal type isolation) and use composition (which aligns better with the principle of single responsibility). Neither anticipated the actual constraints brought about by framework-specific behaviors Difference Analysis: Humans are more familiar with the scalability of existing code frameworks and the checkpoint behavior of Flax NNX, so they tend to reuse existing mechanisms with minimal changes. AI tends to approach formal design, but has blind spots when predicting framework-specific behaviors
Locating the bug in silent discarding output transform (persistent investigation) Role Thought Human Users pushed for further investigation through continuous experimental feedback (‘It still doesn’t work after running it again’), refusing to accept superficial explanations AI AI initially assumed it was an issue with the old task. After users proved otherwise, it continued tracking the reasoning chain until it found the root cause in RobotwinOutputs Difference Analysis: Humans push for deeper investigation of issues through continuous pressure. AI tends to stop investigating after finding a reasonable but non-essential explanation, requiring external pressure to continue
Analyzing the design motivation and cost of Exp9 detach conditioning Role Thought Human Users independently proposed a variant not considered by AI: passing progress_cond to action expert when detaching, allowing MLP to be supervised only by aux_loss, in order to save computation AI AI analyzed the limitations of this approach: Only prefix-only backward was saved, while forward remained. It also noted that it would degenerate into an experiment with little difference from Exp3/4 (losing the core value of end-to-end gradients). But respecting users’ judgment, Exp9 was implemented Difference Analysis: Users propose exploratory ideas, and AI provides a critical analysis of costs and benefits. AI can clearly articulate trade-offs, but does not refuse to implement them
Judging the reasonableness of the large difference between action/aux loss ratios Role Thought Human Users observed that action loss was ~0.0002 and aux loss was ~0.04, intuitively feeling that the ratio was disproportionate. They asked whether it was necessary to reduce the coefficients AI AI analyzed: stop_gradient causes both losses to act on completely different parameter sets, so a disproportionate ratio does not mean an imbalance. Aux loss of 0.04 is normal for predictions in [0,1]. The conclusion is that no adjustment is needed Difference Analysis: Humans get intuition from surface-level numbers, while AI provides more accurate analysis through parameter set separation mechanisms. AI can clearly articulate trade-offs but does not refuse to implement them
Actively dealing with R-dependent batch effect indicators Role Thought Human The original plan involved using an implementation in the STAIG repository that uses R packages AI After environmental detection, AI took active action: rewritten all R-dependent functions in pure Python using sklearn to avoid introducing new dependencies Difference Analysis: AI’s active environmental awareness prevents dependency issues. Humans’ reference implementations ensure the correctness of algorithms
Implementation Level Direction deviation in LIBERO path contamination troubleshooting Role Thought Human Humans discovered that errors still occurred even after changing to a new window, and pointed out that it was the openpi/third_party/libero path, not the Openpi-moe path that AI was concerned about Difference Analysis: Humans are more familiar with their own code repository layout and can quickly lock in the correct paths. AI requires systematic diagnosis to converge
AI Limitations Major Limitations Incomplete data flow tracking leads to two hidden defects: Omission of the alignment parameter bridge from the implementation stage to the evaluate stage (alignment function actually fails completely); insufficient investigation into the assumption of silent discarding fields in output transform during troubleshooting (requires user feedback twice to reach the root cause) - both are issues where there is a lack of global perspective on implicit truncation points in multi-stage data flows Insufficient prediction of framework-specific behaviors: Failure to anticipate the impact of Flax NNX inheritance and composition on checkpoint parameter paths during the design stage (only discovered through runtime pytree errors); underestimating the reuse potential of JAX/NNX’s existing TrainConfig mechanism (creating unnecessary new Config classes) General Limitations The first interpretation of the from_hidden training mode was inaccurate (did not distinguish between inference and training scenarios’ computation graphs), requiring user follow-up twice to form a clear comparative analysis Continuous deviation in external toolchain path resolution: In LIBERO path contamination troubleshooting, three consecutive errors were found. User prompts and multiple import traces were needed to converge Low-level technical errors: Conflicts between K8s GPU solutions (MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0) and binding_utils.py caused secondary errors; incorrect import paths in CalendarPro test files required fixing after failure; default command timeouts are insufficient for GPU-intensive tasks Today’s Achievements Core Achievements π₀.₅ training computation graph characteristics: Inference naturally has two steps (VLM prefix forward → action expert denoising). During training, Exp1-4 can combine prefix+suffix into a single joint forward using teacher forcing (conditioning=GT labels are known in advance). The from_hidden mode introduces cyclic dependencies due to conditioning relying on model output, requiring an additional prefix-only forward. This maintains the same two-step process as inference π₀.₅ gradient flow mechanism: Action loss is transmitted back to VLM backbone through cross-attention of action expert (K/V comes from VLM prefix tokens). stop_gradient strictly limits aux loss to MLP parameters. Since both losses act on completely different parameter sets, a large difference in the ratio between action/aux losses does not indicate an imbalance during training When extending Flax NNX with trained models, inheritance (class Child(Parent)) is the only way to maintain compatibility with checkpoint paths. Composition will result in a complete failure of checkpoint merge because adding a prefix before all parent module parameter paths causes issues Harmony cross-slice alignment actually improves PCA embedding (batch_entropy 0.33→0.52, batch_kl 0.39→0.25), but the improvement is limited - The batch effect of per-section PCA mainly stems from inconsistencies in the feature space itself. Post-processing corrections are just a relief measure Data flow transparency principle: In multi-stage pipelines (extract → align → evaluate), each stage’s configuration must be explicitly passed to downstream stages and cannot rely on implicit sharing. Output transform is an implicit filter in the inference chain, and fields that need to be transparently maintained must be clearly defined - “The model predicts something, but nothing can be seen from outside” is the most hidden type of bug, which requires tracking upstream from the end of the data flow VLM’s prefix_output (global[BOS] + image + language token mean pooling) is more suitable for task completion classification than action expert’s suffix_output because it encodes global state understanding rather than step-by-step action information Batch intent detection requires a specialized architecture: Embed similarity naturally has a low value for “multi-task state messages”. Keyword counting rules (2+completion verb→+0.30 boost) are effective supplements to fill in the blanks of embedding. GENERAL fallback should first determine whether the LLM response contains substantial content before deciding whether to use it or not. It should not discard it unconditionally The freezing mechanism in existing frameworks (such as NNX’s freeze_filter + nnx.DiffState) is usually designed with scalability in mind. Reuse is preferred over creating new ones, which can significantly reduce the amount of code changes Practical Achievements K8s container GPU isolation: All physical GPUs are visible to nvidia-smi, but CUDA runtime is isolated by the container’s cgroup. Manually setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES beyond the allocated range will cause a complete cuInit failure, not just limiting visibility of cards. It should always be verified using jax.devices() or torch.cuda.device_count() for the number of visible devices at the framework level When multiple versions of packages coexist, the editable install .pth file may not take effect if MAPPING is empty. The actual loading path is determined by the PYTHONPATH order. It is necessary to confirm the actual loading path using python -c 'import pkg; print(pkg.__file__)' during runtime Orbax checkpoint directory hierarchy: Steps directory (29999/) stores metadata and assets, while params/subdirectory stores actual parameters. The weight_loader path must point exactly toparams/subdirectory Conversation Summary MIHD ✅ Complete implementation of slice-based Embedding alignment + batch effect evaluation system (CLAUDE.md audit, implementation, bug fixing, end-to-end verification) 15:54:08.591 | claude_code Completed the complete MIHD alignment system in DCC throughout the day: First, fixed the STAIG num_epochs document error (550→300) through the/init audit of CLAUDE.md and added architectural details. Then, created new utils/batch_metrics.py (4 pure Python batch effect indicators, no R dependencies) and pipeline/alignment.py (Harmony + JointSTAIG alignment methods), modifying 6 existing files to complete full-link integration. During code review, a key bug was found where the alignment parameters were not passed to the evaluate stage under all_aligned mode (3 lines of fixes). Finally, all three end-to-end verification experiments passed. Harmony improved batch_entropy from 0.33 to 0.52.
RoboBrain π₀.₅ ✅ Complete implementation from architecture design to normal training start of task completion detection head 08:27:35.886 | claude_code Design phase: After exploring pi0.py/gemma_pytorch.py/config.py, it was determined that prefix_output mean pooling was the best feature source. Reused freeze_filter + nnx.DiffState instead of creating a new Config class. The complete design was written into the plans document. Implementation phase: Created CompletionHead/Pi0WithCompletionHead/train_completion_head.py, fixing the dataset key name mapping (observation.task_completed), missing params suffix in checkpoint path, and pytree structure mismatch caused by composition (resolved by inheritance). Training started normally (loss≈0.253, parameter freezing verification correct, git commit 4032363).
RoboTwin VLA ✅ Fixing manip_progress records (root cause of output transform issues) + improving conditioning + designing experiment configurations for Exp5-9 03:19:42.227 | claude_code manipProgress line: After implementation, no files were generated. AI misjudged it as an issue with the old task. Users persisted and traced to the root cause - RobotwinOutputs.call silently discarded progress only when steps were aligned with actual action steps. After fixing, it generated normally (step indices aligned with actual action steps). Conditioning improvement line: Implemented clipping of abnormal values + sinusoidal encoding. Analyzed that a large difference in action/aux loss ratios under the stop_gradient mechanism did not indicate an imbalance. Exp5-9 line: Users proposed variants of from_hidden/detach. After in-depth discussion on differences between training and inference computation graphs and gradient flow, added cond_mode field to pi0_config.py to rework compute_progress to support four modes, completing all 5 experiment configurations.
LIBERO Evaluation ✅ Fixing multiple blocking issues in the LIBERO evaluation environment 03:02:37.695 | claude_code Fixed sequentially: serve_policy.py’s hostname DNS resolution failure inside the container, main.py client host default value error (0.0.0.0→127.0.0.1), libero_object_com not registered in the openpi/third_party/libero path (confirmed to be correct after three path tracing attempts), MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID conflict with container GPU isolation, and K8s container manually setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES beyond the allocated range causing cuInit failure. Also optimized rollout videos to save them in subfolders based on task names.
Error Recovery Benchmark ✅ Improving CLAUDE.md documentation and confirming job status 21:57:16.386 | claude_code Improved CLAUDE.md with five improvements: added recovery_types.py/recovery_segmenter.py modules, expanded env_wrapper.py method list, supplemented recovery_collection.yaml configuration items, added Sawyer gripper normalization trap (abs(qpos)/0.04 instead of mean), and compressed redundant Slurm code blocks. Also confirmed that job 49363 was completed, GPU resources were fully released, and followed user requests to analyze the phoenix_comparison checkpoint directory (Phoenix framework’s comparison model for MimicGen 9 tasks).
CalendarPro ✅ Implementing BATCH_UPDATE (fixing batch task status update failures) + improving CLAUDE.md documentation 23:05:55.704 | claude_code Users demonstrated real scenarios (received ‘I’m not sure’ after completing a batch report task). AI analyzed the root cause chain and implemented complete BATCH_UPDATE support: modified 8 files to add enumeration/semantic routing (21 utterances)/keyword counting rules/LLM prompt schema/handler (complete/cancel/reschedule/note), and fixed GENERAL fallback so that substantial LLM responses were not discarded. All 21 new tests + 72 related tests passed. Synchronized with the/init analysis code library, rewritten CLAUDE.md to remove redundant enumeration lists, added step-by-step guidance for multi-file changes, and isolated singleton testing注意事项.

summary: Cross-DCC, Tianhe, and local devices are working together to advance four research lines simultaneously. In DCC, we have completed the end-to-end implementation of MIHD cross-slice Embedding alignment and batch effect evaluation systems. In Tianhe, we have successfully launched the training of π₀.₅ task detection heads, fixed the issue with missing manip_progress records in the VLA evaluation pipeline, and completed the repairs for the LIBERO environment along with the design of experiments from Exp5 to Exp9. Locally, we have implemented batch task status updates for CalendarPro, thereby addressing the core defects in high-frequency daily interactions.
title: 错误日志 2026-03-18

content:
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Root Cause of MimicGen Download Failure Role Thoughts Human The user assumes it’s a code issue and seeks help from AI after triggering an error during the download command. AI The AI systematically investigates by checking the script logic, registry registration, and the content of box_links.json to determine that the root cause is the unpublished upstream data link. It also distinguishes between two types of error messages. Difference Analysis: The human assumption was a local code bug, while the AI discovered through systematic investigation that it was an issue with the upstream data release—this conclusion changed the human’s action direction (no need to fix the code).| Role | Approach | |——|——| | Human | Humans take “how the task status reporting classification is implemented, and whether there are any issues with the related changes” as their starting point. They initiate reviews with implicit doubts about specific submissions. | | AI | AI systematically traces the data flow from pi0.py to real_policy.py, utils.py, and vla_infer.py. It identifies 5 bugs and sorts them by severity. Bug 2 (infinite loop) and Bug 3 (None crash) were identified independently by AI as potential risks. |
Difference Analysis: Humans have intuitive goals (doubting the chunk counting logic), while AI provides systematic coverage and priority sorting. The potential crash in the None check order was discovered through code logic reasoning by AI, while humans focus mainly on runtime behavior.
Locating Duplicate Processes in gpumon Role Approach Human Users directly observe the phenomenon (6, 7 stuck with only one process but showing multiple). They provide accurate descriptions of the problem, eliminating the need for AI to actively detect it. AI AI analyzes each process’s FD collection and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES by running debugging scripts. It identifies two independent causes: false reporting of environment variables and duplicate subprocesses. AI proposes a dual filtering solution. Difference Analysis: The issue was discovered through user observation, while the technical analysis and solution design came from AI.
Decision on the Scope of Modifying the Return Type of compute_loss Role Approach Human Humans only describe the requirement (wandb should be able to monitor three indicators separately), without specifying the implementation plan or whether they accept breaking the abstract interface. AI AI chooses to modify the signature of the BaseModel abstract method (requiring simultaneous modification of pi0_fast.py to fill zeros). This is considered the most clean and JAX-native solution, rather than alternative solutions like calculating loss outside of train.py or using global side effects. Difference Analysis: The impact of AI’s solution exceeds the principle of minimal modification, but it maintains code consistency and maintainability.
Data Verification Strategy for Error Recovery Benchmark Role Approach Human Humans ask “where are we now in the generation process”, indicating their concern about progress and quality. AI AI first checks the squeue to confirm job status, then checks the number of files to determine completion level. Finally, it performs detailed statistics through JSON parsing (classified by error×degree), actively detecting two issues: less than 10 D0s and anomalies in the threading phase label. Difference Analysis: AI actively identified two data quality issues that went beyond the original problem scope, demonstrating an inclination towards proactive diagnosis.
AI Limitations Major Limitations Threading phase labeling is all pre_reach problems: AI can diagnose symptoms (checking NPZ files and get_task_completion_stages code), but it cannot run the robosuite environment to actually verify sensor readings. It can only infer the root cause and provide directions for troubleshooting. General Limitations AI tends to check external documentation/remote resources rather than prioritizing local information: During LIBERO suite analysis, multiple Explore subagents were started consecutively, while git status already contained the answer. When diagnosing CALVIN dataset issues, it first tries to fetch external GitHub URLs instead of reading user-specified local code files. It should prioritize reading diff/status and local files before deciding whether to delve deeper. Git remote authentication cannot handle this on its own: SSH keys, GitHub tokens, and VS Code credential helpers are all on the client side. AI can only provide command solutions but cannot execute pushes directly. In server environments, when credential helpers are unavailable, user intervention is required. CALVIN conversion script requires multiple iterations (name errors → key name errors → directory existence errors) to be resolved. Each fix exposes the next problem, and it failed to foresee all issues through a一次性 review of features.json and the entire code. Today’s Takeaways Key Takeaways Task state machine design principle: Completing an event must involve two actions simultaneously - resetting trigger conditions (clearing current_prompt) and broadcasting state changes (_publish_state(‘idle’)). Doing either alone will result in hidden bugs that lead to infinite loops or failure of upstream perception. Both are essential. JAX has_aux mechanism: nnx.value_and_grad supports has_aux=True parameter, compute_loss can return(loss_array, aux_dict), gradients are calculated only for loss_array, and aux_dict transparently carries monitoring indicators. This is the standard way to carry auxiliary information in JAX functional design. Spatial data nearest neighbor query: For large-scale point clouds (>10K points), radius nearest neighbor queries should prioritize KD trees (O(N log N) preprocessing), rather than cdist (O(N²) memory). In HD spatial transcriptomics scenarios with 17K+ cells, the difference between the two can result in OOM vs normal operation. Reliable method for detecting GPU processes: In a Kubernetes PID namespace isolation environment, nvidia-smi cannot display process information. Scanning/proc//fd for.dev/nvidia* FDs is a more reliable way to detect processes, but FD evidence must be provided to avoid false reporting due to inherited environment variables. Single Source of Truth principle: Constant collections that need to reference multiple files (such as NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS) must be imported from the only definable source. Manually maintaining multiple copies inevitably leads to inconsistencies, which often occur in edge cases (such as missing adln_attention). Error messages for dataset download tools may come from multiple levels (registry not registered vs download link not exist). It’s necessary to distinguish them to correctly determine the root cause. Directly checking configuration files (such as box_links_ds.json) is more efficient than analyzing error messages, sometimes allowing us to discover that the root cause is an unpublished upstream data rather than a local code bug. Benchmark data quality: Uneven distribution of data (some error types with D0<10) is a systematic issue. Distribution monitoring and balancing mechanisms should be incorporated into the pipeline design phase. General framework adaptation for specific tasks (such as threading needle insertion) requires clear task-level testing and verification. Practical Takeaways The field names in TFRecord datasets in RLDS format vary depending on the dataset. It’s necessary to confirm the actual schema through features.json, rather than relying on experience from other datasets or default keys in code templates. On shared servers, the priority of the WANDB_API_KEY environment variable is higher than ~/.netrc (location for wandb login storage). This can serve as a lightweight solution that overrides global accounts without requiring modifications to wandb configuration files. Session Summary MIHD ✅ MIHD project code refactoring (/simplify 9 fixes) and documentation整理 13:54:46.661 | claude_code A three-way parallel code review was conducted on 21 Python files that modified the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project, fixing 9 issues (replacing O(N²)cdist with KDTree to prevent OOM, consolidating NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS constants and adding missing adln_attention, eliminating 8 duplicate judgments in hoisted device resolution). All modifications were verified using Python AST syntax. Subsequently, CLAUDE.md was updated to reference the new constants, and notes were organized into three-level time priorities in plans.md, with an update to the architecture roadmap.
RoboBrain-Pi ✅ robobrain_pi task status bug fixes, separation monitoring of training loss implementation, and git workflow management 11:09:28.373 | claude_code A systematic review was conducted on the task_completed data flow in vla_infer.py (pi0.py→real_policy.py→utils.py→vla_infer.py), identifying and fixing 5 bugs (infinite loop triggered by not clearing current_prompt after task completion, potential crash due to incorrect None check order, lack of broadcasting idle state, incomplete debug log thresholds, and incorrect text description). Action_loss/task_loss separation monitoring was implemented simultaneously: four files were modified together (model.py returned a tuple, pi0.py carried an auxiliary dictionary, pi0_fast.py filled with zeros, train.py used has_aux=True for unpacking), and wandb added three separate curves. During this process, multiple git workflow issues were addressed, including handling git proxy conflicts (local configuration overriding unreachable global proxies), cherry-picking synchronization of dev/mlp_old branches, and erroneous merges back to the repository.
LIBERO-Benchmark ✅ Integration analysis and confirmation of LIBERO libero_object_com test suite 09:16:47.373 | claude_code Analysis of the required file changes for adding a custom libero_object_com test suite revealed that most work was already completed: 10 task names were added to libero_suite_task_map.py, the LIBERO_OBJECT_COM class was registered in init.py, 10.bddl files under bddl_files/libero_object_com existed, and main.py updated the default suite name and max_steps=300. The init_files directory, which wasn’t created because relevant code was commented out in main.py, didn’t need to be created anymore. Integration was completed without additional modifications.
Error-Recovery-Benchmark 🔄 Completed statistics for Error Recovery Benchmark and issue diagnosis 14:03:00.373 | claude_code It was confirmed that the pipeline job was complete, generating 2920 error scenarios in 6 tasks (coffee had a maximum of 1076, threading had at least 150). Statistics were taken on the distribution of each task×error type×D0/D1/D2, revealing that there were 7 D0 subtypes with less than 10 cases (which needed to be supplemented), and all traces in the threading phase labels were pre_reach (affecting the injection of other error types). Further diagnosis revealed that threading tasks were not correctly phased labeled, and get_task_completion_stages() implementation needed to be checked. This issue will be addressed later.
CALVIN Dataset Converter ✅ Fixed multiple runtime errors in CALVIN RLDS→LeRobot conversion script and added a progress bar 06:51:06.033 | claude_code User chenjunye requested the addition of a progress bar and discovered inconsistencies between the data directory structure and code configuration. By reading the directory structure and features.json, it was possible to fix dataset name errors (calvin_abc_d→calvin_abc), observation key errors (image→rgb_static/rgb_gripper), and FileExistsError when the output directory already existed. Interaction logic for user confirmation of overriding was also added. The script could run correctly.
GPU Monitor (gpumon) ✅ Fixed gpumon.py issue with displaying duplicate processes and added keyboard interaction navigation 03:01:28.167 | claude_code User chenxingping noticed that gpumon.py displayed multiple duplicate processes on GPU 6/7. Through debugging, two causes were identified: irrelevant processes inheriting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES (bash/claude, etc.) and duplicate counting of DDP worker subprocesses. The fix involved double filtering (FD evidence requirement + parent process chain de-duplication), reducing the number of processes from 35 to 8. Subsequently, according to user requests, functions like nvitop-style up/down key selection and left/right key command scrolling were added.
RoboCasa MimicGen Data Download ✅ Diagnosed and confirmed the upstream root cause for all failed MimicGen pre-training data downloads 03:38:33.780 | claude_code When user chenjunye ran download_datasets –source mimicgen, all tasks reported errors. Through systematic inspection (script logic→dataset_registry.py registration situation→box_links_ds.json content), the root cause was identified: the file contained no MimicGen path (0/350 entries). Only 60 out of 317 tasks had registered mg_path, indicating an issue with upstream data links not being published. During the second session, grep was used to verify key conclusions, distinguishing between two types of error messages (no mg_path registration vs registration but no Box link). It was confirmed that no code fixes were needed.
chenxingping Work Environment Configuration ✅ Configuring a personal wandb account at the directory level on shared servers 07:46:38.767 | claude_code A solution was provided for user chenxingping to use a personal wandb account in the personal directory on shared servers. It is recommended to prioritize using direnv (set with .envrc) and ~/.bashrc to export environment variables, indicating that the WANDB_API_KEY environment variable has higher priority than ~/.netrc.

summary: 在DCC端，我们对MIHD多模态空间转录组项目进行了代码重构，解决了9个相关问题，并更新了相关文档。在tianhe端，多位研究员同时完成了robobrain_pi任务状态机的修复工作，以及训练损失值的分离监控、LIBERO测试套件的集成验证、error recovery benchmark 2920场景数据的质量检查、CALVIN格式转换脚本的修复、GPU监控工具的去重处理以及键盘导航功能的新增。此外，还确认了RoboCasa MimicGen预训练数据下载的上游原因。

Enes Eken — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:57:05
Rating: Active Researcher (30.2/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 7 Total Citations 138 Recent Citations 17 Number of Papers 21 Papers in Top Conferences 0 Publication Years 2014 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 2072299 Research Trajectory Enes Eken’s academic career shows a clear three-phase evolution. The first phase (2014–2018) focused on the reliability design of Spin-Transfer Torque Random Access Memories (STT-RAM). He developed techniques related to read-write reliability enhancement, mutation-aware simulation tools, and error-correcting code schemes. Most of his highest-cited papers were published during this period. The second phase (2021–2022) shifted towards research on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), focusing on evaluating GANs using Fréchet distances to address overfitting/underfitting issues. However, the overall citation response was limited. The third phase (2023–2025) involved interdisciplinary applications of laser spectroscopy and deep learning. He used Transformer models to process spectral data from quantum cascade lasers and explored object detection, with a somewhat scattered approach.
Overall, this researcher is a moderately influential figure rather than a leading scholar in the field. With an h-index of 7 and 138 total citations, although there were only 17 citations in the past five years, it indicates that his research direction changes frequently, resulting in inconsistent citation accumulation. He has not published any papers in top conferences such as NeurIPS, ISSCC, or DAC A-levels. His academic trajectory is characterized by crossing three distinct fields: hardware architecture, machine learning, and optical sensing. While this approach offers flexibility, it also sacrifices depth of knowledge in a single field.
Breakthrough Works 1. A Novel Self-Reference Technique for STT-RAM Read and Write Reliability Enhancement (2014) Description: Proposed a self-reference reading scheme that enhances read and write reliability by introducing an adaptive comparison mechanism during reads, reducing misreads and miswrites.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Early research on STT-RAM focused mainly on device physics, with insufficient systematic modeling of how process variations and thermal fluctuations affect read-write windows. There was a lack of methodological frameworks for integrating self-reference schemes with write reliability enhancement.
Impact: This work opened up the direction of STT-RAM reliability circuit design. It was cited in several simulation tools and ECC scheme papers, laying the foundation for 24 citations in this field.
2. NVSim-VXs: An Improved NVSim for Variation Aware STT-RAM Simulation (2016) Description: Based on the open-source NVSim simulation framework, a process variation modeling module was introduced, allowing researchers to quantitatively evaluate trade-offs between yield and performance in STT-RAM. This provided estimates of latency, power consumption, and area with mutation awareness.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The original NVSim assumed nominal process parameters and lacked statistical modeling capabilities for variations. Before the variation effects became significant at nodes below 7nm, there was no urgent need for mutation-aware simulation tools in industry, and relevant parameter databases were not yet fully available.
Impact: This paper has 17 citations, making it one of Enes Eken’s most cited papers. It provided a practical tool for exploring the design space of STT-RAM and also contributed to subsequent work on manufacturing cost modeling (2016 IGSC paper).
3. Sliding Basket: An Adaptive ECC Scheme for Runtime Write Failure Suppression of STT-RAM Cache (2016) Description: Proposed an adaptive error-correcting code scheme that dynamically adjusts the strength of ECC and cache line mapping strategies based on runtime write failure rates, achieving a balance between hardware overhead and reliability.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Traditional static ECC schemes fixed the protection strength during design, failing to adapt to the dynamic changes in STT-RAM write latency and failure rates with temperature and aging. Adaptive strategies required sufficient statistical understanding of runtime characteristics of write failures.
Impact: This paper has 16 citations, providing new ideas for runtime-optimized ECC designs for STT-RAM caches. It was published at the DATE conference, achieving some dissemination.
4. Recent Technology Advances of Emerging Memories (2017) Description: Summarized the latest developments in emerging memory technologies such as STT-RAM, PCM, and RRAM. This paper systematically reviewed the challenges and opportunities faced by various non-volatile memories in terms of device physics, circuit design, and system applications.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Such review papers benefited from a timing opportunity—around 2017, emerging memory technologies entered the phase of large-scale production. Both industry and academia needed a cross-sectional comparison that took into account devices, circuits, and systems. Previous reviews were scattered and not systematic enough.
Impact: This paper has 25 citations, making it Enes Eken’s most cited single paper. It shows that review work has high dissemination value in aggregating knowledge in the field.
5. A novel breath molecule sensing system based on deep neural network employing multiple-line direct absorption spectroscopy (2023) Description: Applied deep neural networks to multi-line direct absorption spectroscopy data analysis, enabling accurate detection of various molecules in exhaled gases. This provides a technical approach for non-invasive medical diagnosis.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Early spectroscopic analyses relied on traditional numerical fitting algorithms like Voigt line fitting, which had limited capabilities for handling noise and overlapping peaks from multiple components. The engineering challenges of deploying large-scale deep learning models in resource-constrained sensors were only fully addressed in the 2020s. It also took time to accumulate high-quality exhaled gas spectroscopy datasets.
Impact: This paper has 12 citations, making it Enes Eken’s most cited paper in recent work. It indicates that he has some research capabilities in the interdisciplinary field of laser spectroscopy and AI. However, this direction is still in its early stages, and its influence needs to be evaluated.
Research Directions Reliability design and mutation-aware simulation of STT-RAM (2014–2018) Evaluation methods for GANs and applications of Fréchet distance (2021–2022) Interdisciplinary applications of laser spectroscopy and deep learning/Transformer models (2023–2025) Design of object detection networks (2025) Methodological Evolution In the early stages (2014–2018), the methodology focused on hardware architecture design and circuit-level simulation, using SPICE simulations, mutation statistics modeling, and VHDL/Verilog implementation. The focus was on manufacturability and process robustness. The tool contribution (NVSim-VXs) was a typical output of this phase.
After 2021, the methodology shifted towards data-driven approaches. First, weight matrix subspace analysis from GAN training was used to improve evaluation metrics. Then, Transformer encoders were utilized to process temporal spectral data. This evolution reflects that the popularity of deep learning tools has reduced the barriers to cross-field transfer. However, this also means that the depth of methodological accumulation at each stage is relatively limited—from hardware simulation to GAN evaluation to spectral modeling. There is no inherent methodology inheritance between these three transformations; rather, it’s more about changing application scenarios rather than continuous evolution of core methods.
Domain Impact Enes Eken’s contributions to the field mainly lie in two specific areas: STT-RAM design tools (NVSim-VXs) and reliability methods (self-reference technology, Sliding Basket ECC). During the period from 2014 to 2017, when non-volatile memory research was active, he provided valuable practical tools and solutions. His total citations during this period were about 110. His impact is limited to the STT-RAM subfield and does not have a significant influence in broader computer architecture or electronic design automation fields. After 2021, although his research direction changed, showing cross-disciplinary adaptability, the low number of citations (only 17 in the past five years) indicates that he has not established significant influence in new directions. Overall assessment: Enes Eken is a qualified contributor to the STT-RAM field, but he is not a core figure who shapes the direction of this field.
Top 20 Citations Papers # Year Citations Title 1 2017 25 Recent Technology Advances of Emerging Memories 2 2014 24 A Novel Self-Reference Technique for STT-RAM Read and Write Reliability Enhancement 3 2016 17 NVSim-VXs: An improved NVSim for variation aware STT-RAM simulation 4 2016 16 Sliding Basket: An adaptive ECC scheme for runtime write failure suppression of STT-RAM cache 5 2014 13 A new field-assisted access scheme of STT-RAM with self-reference capability 6 2023 12 A novel breath molecule sensing system based on deep neural network employing multiple-line direct absorption spectroscopy 7 2016 8 Modeling STT-RAM fabrication cost and impacts in NVSim 8 2015 7 Spin-hall assisted STT-RAM design and discussion 9 2017 5 Giant Spin-Hall assisted STT-RAM and logic design 10 2021 4 Determining overfitting and underfitting in generative adversarial networks using Fréchet distance 11 2016 3 Adaptive refreshing and read voltage control scheme for FeDRAM 12 2018 2 Modeling of biaxial magnetic tunneling junction for multi-level cell STT-RAM realization 13 2018 1 Developing Variation Aware Simulation Tools, Models, and Designs for STT-RAM 14 2024 1 Compact laser spectroscopy-based sensor using a transformer-based model for analysis of multiple molecules 15 2014 0 USING EXTERNAL MAGNETIC FIELD FOR INCREASING STT-RAM READ/WRITE RELIABILITY 16 2015 0 Recent progresses of STT memory design and applications 17 2021 0 Using subspaces of weight matrix for evaluating generative adversarial networks with Fréchet distance 18 2022 0 Content loss and conditional space relationship in conditional generative adversarial networks 19 2025 0 Improving resolution of grating-coupled external cavity quantum cascade laser without sacrificing time by leveraging transformer encoder 20 2025 0 Design Space Exploration of Backbone Network for Single Shot Object Detection
summary: Enes Eken의 학문적 경력은 세 단계로 나뉩니다. 첫 번째 단계는 2014년부터 2018년까지였습니다. 이 기간 동안 그는 스핀 트랜스폼 메모리(STT-RAM)의 신뢰성 설계에 집중했습니다. 특히, 읽기/쓰기 성능 향상, 변동 감지 시뮬레이션 도구, 그리고 오류 수정 코드 관련 기술을 크게 발전시켰습니다. 이 시기에 발표된 논문들이 그의 가장 중요한 연구 성과였습니다.

content:
Fan Chen — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:41:44
Rating: Active Researcher (41.4/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 12 Total Citations 542 Citations in Last 5 Years 287 Number of Papers Published 33 Papers on Top Journals 0 Publication Period 1992 - 2022 Semantic Scholar 49040579 Research Trajectory The “Fan Chen” in this dataset is likely a combined record of multiple researchers with the same name, not a complete trajectory for a single scholar. From the content of his papers, at least two distinct types of researchers can be identified: (1) A researcher who has been engaged in analytical chemistry/food chemistry for a long time, from 1992–2022, developing from early forensic gas chromatography to ion liquid extraction and natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES); (2) A group of computer science papers published after 2019, focusing on reinforcement learning, ReRAM accelerators, quantum computing, optimization theory, diffusion models, etc. These topics are unrelated to the first researcher and clearly come from different groups of researchers with the same name.
In terms of the main analytical chemistry direction that can be traced, his academic trajectory is clear: From 1992–2008, he focused on forensic toxicological gas chromatography detection. In the 2010s, he shifted to HPLC-DAD food/drug fingerprinting. In 2015–2016, he made a significant breakthrough by introducing ion liquids into liquid phase microextraction and countercurrent chromatography, which was published in Food Chemistry and J. Chromatography A, with 85 and 61 citations respectively. From 2019–2021, he expanded into the field of natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) green extraction, publishing several high-citation papers that established his academic status in this niche area of green analytical chemistry.
His h-index of 12, total citations of 542, and the ratio of 287 citations in the past five years indicate that he is a mid-level researcher with continuously growing influence in a specific niche area (ion liquids/DES green pretreatment), rather than a cross-disciplinary expert.
Breakthrough Works 1. Determination of chlorophenols in honey samples using in-situ ionic liquid-dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2015) Description: Proposed an in-situ ionic liquid dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction method, where hydrophilic ionic liquids [C4MIM][BF4] react with anions to generate hydrophobic extractants [C4MIM][NTf2] in situ. This method enables the enrichment and HPLC detection of six chlorophenols in honey, with 85 citations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Traditional DLLME relied on toxic organic solvents (such as chlorobenzene, carbon tetrachloride) as extractants. There were many interfering substances in food matrices, and the strategy of generating ionic liquids in situ had not been systematically applied to honey trace pollutant analysis before. Additionally, the cost of commercial ionic liquids decreased significantly in the late 2010s, making this method feasible.
Impact: It opened up a practical path for ionic liquid microextraction in the field of food safety. Many subsequent papers followed this approach, extending it to complex matrices such as wine and vegetable oils, becoming landmark papers in this niche area.
2. Determination of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice using ionic liquid modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2016) Description: Introduced ion liquids into the countercurrent chromatography solvent system to achieve preparatory enrichment and purification of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice. Published in J. Chromatography A, with 61 citations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Countercurrent chromatography traditionally relied on standard solvent systems such as n-hexane/ethyl acetate/methanol/water. The selectivity for polar mycotoxins was limited. The adjustable polarity of ionic liquids had not been systematically integrated into countercurrent chromatography system design before 2015, and the low purity of ionic liquids in the early days led to unstable phase separation.
Impact: It established the feasibility of ionic liquid-modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment tool for natural products and food pollutants. This led to a series of related works on oil separation and anthocyanin purification.
3. Selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent composed with an iron oxide-based nanoferrofluid (2019) Description: Combined hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents with iron oxide nanofluids to achieve magnetic-assisted selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Published in Microchimica Acta, with 40 citations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Hydrophobic NADES was not systematically synthesized and characterized until around 2017. Combining DES with magnetic nanoparticles to create “magnetic DLLME” required both DES design and nanofluid preparation techniques, which were rarely explored before 2019.
Impact: It promoted the application of DES in environmental pollutant detection. The solution combining green solvents and magnetic separation has been widely used in subsequent research.
4. A priori design of new natural deep eutectic solvent for lutein recovery from microalgae (2021) Description: Based on a prior design framework for intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions, systematically screened NADES for green extraction of microalgae lutein. Published in Food Chemistry, with 74 citations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Previous NADES research relied heavily on trial-and-error experiments, lacking rational design methods based on molecular structure prediction of solubility. The combination of molecular dynamics simulations and Hansen solubility parameters was not fully developed until the early 2020s for food extraction NADES design.
Impact: It shifted NADES research from empirical synthesis to prior design driven by mechanisms, providing a methodological example for green extraction chemistry. This had a direct impact on microalgae biomethanolization.
5. A Theoretical Study on Terpene‐Based Natural Deep Eutectic Solvent: Relationship between Viscosity and Hydrogen‐Bonding Interactions (2021) Description: Through quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics simulations, clarified the microscopic mechanism of terpene-based NADES low viscosity - the quantitative relationship between hydrogen bonding interactions and viscosity. Published in Global Challenges, with 72 citations.
Why it couldn’t be done before: High viscosity was a core obstacle to the industrialization of NADES, but its microscopic causes were not clear for a long time. Terpene-based low viscosity systems had not been systematically studied before 2019, and linking DFT calculations with rheological measurements required interdisciplinary integration between computational chemistry and analytical chemistry.
Impact: It theoretically explained the viscosity advantage of terpene-based NADES, providing a thermodynamic/dynamic basis for the subsequent design of low-viscosity green solvents. This promoted the conversion of such solvents to practical separation applications.
Research Directions Ionic liquid-assisted microextraction and countercurrent chromatography (for food/environmental trace pollutant analysis) Design, characterization, and green extraction applications of natural deep eutectic solvents Development of chromatographic detection methods for forensic and food analysis (GC, HPLC, LC-MS) Preparative separation and purification of bioactive natural products Methodological Evolution The methodological evolution of this analytical chemist shows a clear three-stage transition. The first stage (1992–2008) focused on gas chromatography (GC/FPD, GC-MS) as the core tool, focusing on quantitative detection of forensic toxins and food components. Methods were mainly based on single components and matrices, with limited technical depth. The second stage (2010–2014) shifted to simultaneous multi-component determination using HPLC-DAD/ELSD, attempting to establish traditional Chinese medicine fingerprinting. However, technological innovation was still limited.
The third stage (since 2015) represents a fundamental paradigm shift: researchers introduced ionic liquids (IL) and deep eutectic solvents (DES/NADES) into sample pretreatment processes. The core innovation shifted from “detection methods themselves” to “green and efficient sample enrichment/separation strategies.” This transition aligns with the overall trend of “green analytical chemistry” in the field of analytical chemistry, elevating his work from技术应用型到方法创新型, resulting in a significant increase in citations. More recently (2021), quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics simulations were introduced, upgrading empirical solvent design to mechanism-driven rational design. This marks an evolution towards higher-level computational-assisted experiments.
Domain Impact In the analytical chemistry niche of green sample pretreatment methods, this researcher is one of the important contributors to the technology of ionic liquid-countercurrent chromatography coupling and natural deep eutectic solvent extraction. His representative papers (Food Chemistry 2015, J. Chromatography A 2016, 2021 series NADES papers) contributed approximately 470 citations, accounting for 87% of total citations. This indicates that his influence is highly concentrated during the development phase of green pretreatment methods from 2015–2021. His work has promoted NADES from concept verification to practical applications in food safety and natural product separation. However, he has not produced disruptive results that would change the cognitive framework of the entire analytical chemistry field. His influence is limited to the niche area of green analysis methods. It should be noted that a large number of computer science papers (reinforcement learning, quantum computing, optimization algorithms, etc.) appearing after 2019 in his paper list come from other researchers with the same name and should not be attributed to this analytical chemist.

summary: Fan Chen: 在这个数据集中，“Fan Chen”很可能指的是多位同名研究者的合并记录，并非某一位学者的完整研究轨迹。根据论文内容来看，可以明显区分出至少两类截然不同的研究者：（1）一位长期从事分析化学/食品化学研究的研究者，其研究时间贯穿了1992年至2022年，他的研究方向从早期的法医气相色谱技术发展到了……

Hsin-Pai Cheng — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:37:41
Rating: Active Researcher (45.7/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 13 Total Citations 450 Citations in Last 5 Years 7 Number of Papers Published 29 Papers Published in Top Journals 7 Publication Period 2016 - 2024 Semantic Scholar 3461946 Research Trajectory Hsin-Pai Cheng is a researcher specializing in efficient AI systems and edge deployment. His academic trajectory can be divided into three clear stages. From 2016 to 2018, he focused on the IBM TrueNorth neuromorphic computing platform, studying the quantization of neural networks under hardware constraints and their robustness against adversarial attacks. This stage laid the foundation for his dual perspective on algorithms and hardware.
From 2019 to 2020 was a period of high productivity and influence. He led the review article “Low-Power Computer Vision” (63 citations), which defined the subfield of low-power computer vision. In the same year, several NAS papers (MSNet, AutoShrink, SwiftNet) focused on architecture search for IoT/edge devices. The paper “Ordering Chaos” (MLSys, 58 citations) solved the engineering bottleneck in memory scheduling for irregular NAS networks, marking a key step forward in practical deployment of NAS research.
After 2021, his output slowed significantly (almost no publications in 2021). From 2022 to 2024, his work focused on zero-sample NAS and efficient Transformers. In 2025-2026, new papers emerged in the field of end-to-end autonomous driving systems (RoCA, FALO, generative scene generation). Given the lack of institutional information, it is speculated that he has entered the industrial sector, shifting his focus to edge deployment of autonomous driving systems. Overall, he is a “efficiency engineer” rather than a pioneer in a new field, but he has made significant contributions to low-power AI deployment.
Breakthrough Works 1. Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, and Opportunities (2019) Description: This paper systematically reviewed the current state, challenges, and opportunities in low-power computer vision, covering model compression, hardware acceleration, and mobile device deployment. It became a landmark review article in this field.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Before 2018, the mobile AI ecosystem was not yet mature. Lightweight models like MobileNet/ShuffleNet were just starting to emerge, NAS methods were not widely used, and there was insufficient data for actual chip applications (such as Kirin, A-series NPUs), making it impossible to produce a systematic review based on real-world data. 2019 was the right time for industrial implementation and academic breakthroughs.
Impact: With 63 citations, this paper became his most cited work. It provided a unified framework and reference for low-power vision research, promoting systematic attention to “edge computing efficiency.”
2. Ordering Chaos: Memory-Aware Scheduling of Irregularly Wired Neural Networks for Edge Devices (2020) Description: This paper proposed a memory-aware scheduling algorithm for irregularly wired neural networks, enabling efficient operation on edge devices with limited memory. It filled the gap between NAS design and practical deployment.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Irregularly wired NAS networks like Random Wiring Network only emerged in 2018-2019, so the problem of memory scheduling was new. Previously, regular networks like VGG and ResNet had fixed computation graphs, making scheduling simple. Meanwhile, MLSys, a system-and-machine learning conference, was founded in 2019, providing a platform for publishing papers. The key insight was to treat irregular graphs found in NAS research as scheduling optimization problems rather than pure algorithm problems.
Impact: Published at MLSys 2020 (a top system conference), it received 58 citations. It clarified the paradigm that “NAS should not only search for accuracy but also deployability,” influencing subsequent research on hardware-aware NAS.
3. MAT: A Multi-strength Adversarial Training Method to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks (2018) Description: This paper proposed a multi-intensity adversarial training strategy, which significantly improved the robustness of DNNs against various types of adversarial attacks while reducing the loss of accuracy for clean samples.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The problem of adversarial examples was first proposed by Goodfellow et al. in 2014, but early defense methods (such as single-intensity training) quickly became ineffective against strong attacks. The emergence of PGD attacks and C&W attacks in 2017-2018 revealed the limitations of single-intensity training, leading to the need for multi-intensity strategies. In terms of computational resources, generating multiple rounds of adversarial examples and training was too costly before 2017.
Impact: With 29 citations, this paper was one of his most cited algorithmic works. It made significant contributions to methods for improving adversarial robustness and provided a foundation for subsequent research on neuromorphic computing security (AdverQuil, 2019).
4. NASGEM: Neural Architecture Search via Graph Embedding Method (2020) Description: This paper introduced graph embedding methods into NAS, using graph neural networks to encode the topological structure of architectures and constructing performance predictors. This resulted in a more flexible and scalable NAS framework based on estimators.
Why it couldn’t be done before: Predictor-based NAS was just starting to emerge before 2019, and the encoding methods were mostly simple sequences or manual features. Graph neural networks began to mature in 2018-2019 (such as GraphSAGE, GIN), but using GNNs for architectural topology encoding became feasible only in 2019-2020.
Impact: Published at AAAI 2020, it received 24 citations. It promoted the NAS technology of “graph-based architecture representation” and was one of his two peak outputs in 2020, along with Ordering Chaos.
5. Towards Decentralized Deep Learning with Differential Privacy (2019) Description: This paper proposed algorithms for protecting privacy in decentralized learning frameworks, maintaining good convergence speed and communication efficiency while respecting privacy budgets. It was aimed at federated learning scenarios.
Why it couldn’t be done before: The combination of differential privacy and deep learning (such as DP-SGD) was proposed by Abadi et al. in 2016, but it mainly targeted centralized scenarios at that time. Decentralized training without parameter servers had high communication costs and complex privacy analysis before 2018. With advancements in distributed optimization theory and communication compression technology, conditions were met to handle both dimensions simultaneously in 2019.
Impact: With 36 citations, this paper received significant attention in the field of privacy-protected distributed learning, demonstrating its research scope from hardware efficiency to system security.
Research Areas Efficient neural network design and edge deployment (quantization, NAS, memory scheduling) Low-power computer vision Adversarial attacks and defenses Neuromorphic computing (early stage) End-to-end autonomous driving systems (recent focus) Methodological Evolution Cheng’s methodological evolution shows a three-stage transformation from “single-point optimization” to “system-level coordination” and then to “task-driven”. In the early stages (2016-2018), empirical algorithms were used: compensation learning strategies were proposed for IBM TrueNorth’s quantization losses, and multi-intensity training data augmentation was designed for adversarial attacks. These methods were direct and verifiable, but their focus was limited to a single dimension.
From 2019 to 2020, his methodology evolved into a systematic approach of “searching + graph structure + constraint awareness”. NAS works generally introduced graph representations (NASGEM used GNNs to encode architectural topologies, MSNet/SwiftNet used graph propagation for meta-knowledge). The optimization goals expanded from single accuracy to multi-objectives such as accuracy × latency × memory. Ordering Chaos further transformed the problem into scheduling optimization, crossing the boundaries between algorithms and systems. After 2022, zero-sample NAS (ZiCo-BC) emerged, attempting to replace expensive architecture evaluations with training-independent metrics, representing a extreme example of efficiency pursuit. Papers on autonomous driving from 2025 onwards reflect an engineering transformation from “how to design efficient models” to “how to deploy in real-world closed-loop systems”.
Domain Impact Cheng has a clear but medium-scale influence in the field of low-power/edge AI: h-index 13, total citations 450. Two of his core papers (Low-Power CV review with 63 citations, Ordering Chaos with 58 citations) contributed about one-fourth of the citations. His contributions mainly lie in “breaking through the last mile between NAS and practical deployment” - this engineering problem is often overlooked in academia. The publication of Ordering Chaos at MLSys marked the formal recognition of this issue by the system community. Only 7 citations in the past five years indicate that his academic activity has significantly declined, suggesting that he has shifted his focus to industrial research. Overall, he is a valuable contributor to the field of edge AI deployment, but he has not reached the level of being a pioneer in a new research paradigm.
Top 20 Cited Papers # Year Citations Title 1 2019 63 Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, and Opportunities 2 2020 58 Ordering Chaos: Memory-Aware Scheduling of Irregularly Wired Neural Networks for Edge Devices 3 2019 36 Towards Decentralized Deep Learning with Differential Privacy 4 2018 33 Neuromorphic computing’s yesterday, today, and tomorrow - an evolutional view 5 2018 29 MAT: A Multi-strength Adversarial Training Method to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks 6 2017 27 Understanding the design of IBM neurosynaptic system and its tradeoffs: A user perspective 7 2018 26 LEASGD: an Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Decentralized Algorithm for Distributed Learning 8 2020 24 NASGEM: Neural Architecture Search via Graph Embedding Method 9 2016 23 ApesNet: a pixel-wise efficient segmentation network 10 2019 17 MSNet: Structural Wired Neural Architecture Search for Internet of Things 11 2020 15 Adversarial Attack: A New Threat to Smart Devices and How to Defend It 12 2018 14 Differentiable Fine-grained Quantization for Deep Neural Network Compression 13 2018 13 2018 Low-Power Image Recognition Challenge 14 2019 11 SwiftNet: Using Graph Propagation as Meta-knowledge to Search Highly Representative Neural Architectures 15 2019 10 Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, Opportunities 16 2020 10 ScaleNAS: One-Shot Learning of Scale-Aware Representations for Visual Recognition 17 2016 9 Exploring the optimal learning technique for IBM TrueNorth platform to overcome quantization loss 18 2019 9 AutoShrink: A Topology-aware NAS for Discovering Efficient Neural Architecture 19 2019 6 Learning Efficient Sparse Structures in Speech Recognition 20 2018 4 Bamboo: Ball-Shape Data Augmentation Against Adversarial Attacks from All Directions
summary: Hsin-Pai Cheng is a researcher specializing in efficient AI systems and edge-based deployments. His academic journey can be divided into three distinct stages. From 2016 to 2018, he focused on the IBM TrueNorth neuromorphic computing platform, conducting deep research into the quantization of neural networks under hardware constraints, as well as aspects related to adversarial robustness. He also made significant contributions to the field of “computational capabilities”.
title: Hsin-Pai Cheng — Research Analyst Report
