summary: On the supercomputer cluster, the BC-RNN training configuration was reverted to the image mode, and a successful 5-task parallel training was initiated. Additionally, the evaluation framework was extended to support 5 MimicGen tasks. It was also identified and resolved the issue of mismatch in task distribution, which resulted in a 0% success rate for Pi0.5.
title: 缺陷日志 2026-02-26

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-23 Today’s Overview What was done: Parallel progress was made on DCC and Tianhe clusters for three core tasks: comprehensive optimization of MIHD fusion training and full slice benchmark testing, initiation of the Error Recovery Benchmark M14 tripartite evaluation with key bug fixes, and establishment from scratch to stable operation of Pi0.5 LoRA fine-tuning training. How it was done: On DCC side, CPU bottlenecks were eliminated through three methods: vectorization using NumPy, GPU-based random number generation, and batch Transformer forward. The encoder architecture was decoupled. On Tianhe side, data compatibility issues and GPU memory overflow problems were solved through progressive debugging (downgrading dependencies, patching source code, switching to LoRA architecture, submitting sbatch jobs). Meanwhile, scene-level try-except修复了 evaluation process crashes. What’s achieved: QFormer training is expected to accelerate by 20-50x. The average ARI for STAIG full slices is 0.546. It was found that refine has a pattern of improving fusion effects in weak and strong cases. Pi0.5 LoRA training (Job 46553) operates stably at 2.0s/step (expected to complete in 53 hours). After fixing the M14 tripartite evaluation, full progress was resumed, confirming that ~519 out of 649 scenarios are valid evaluation targets. DCC What was done: Comprehensive optimization of MIHD fusion training was completed on RTX 5000 Ada GPU nodes: three CPU acceleration methods were implemented, the vision encoder architecture was decoupled, and comparative experiments were conducted between Vision Refine and Baseline (8 fusion methods × with/without refine). All 11 DLPFC slices of STAIG benchmark testing were carried out. How it was done: PyTorch training was run using the conda General environment. Scipy cdist was used for vectorizing edge weights, GPU-native random number generation eliminated cross-device transfers, and batch Transformer forward replaced per spot iterations. Pyc cache cleanup resolved ImportError issues. Long-time experiments were monitored in parallel through TaskOutput background processes. What’s achieved: STAIG fusion on 10/11 DLPFC slices was successful (151676 known collapses), with an average ARI of 0.546. The optimal combination for qformer+no-refine is 151673 (ARI=0.4832). It was found that scan_cluster refine is beneficial for weak fusion but harmful for strong fusion. Tianhe What was done: The entire process of Pi0.5 LoRA fine-tuning was completed on Tianhe clusters (an46/an49/an51) (data conversion → normalization statistics → LoRA training), as well as the Error Recovery Benchmark M14 tripartite evaluation (CPU analysis + GPU evaluation + environment fingerprint crash fixing). How it was done: Progressive debugging was used to solve data format compatibility issues (downgrading datasets, patching lerobot source code), GPU memory overflow problems (switching from full training to LoRA architecture), and training process management issues (srun → sbatch). The evaluation process was started in parallel through srun –overlap. Scene-level try-except fixed EnvironmentMismatchError issues. What’s achieved: A LoRA checkpoint up to step 1000 was obtained. Job 46553 operates stably with a loss of 0.068 and at 2.0s/step. After fixing the M14 tripartite evaluation (m14_cpu completed, m14_pi05 completed, m14_pi0 in progress), it recovered from crashing at scene 122. It was confirmed that ~130 natural_* scenarios in the 649 scenario database are incompatible, while the actual valid evaluation scenarios amount to about 519. On DCC nodes, system optimization of MIHD spatialomics fusion training was carried out (3 CPU acceleration methods + architecture decoupling + full slice benchmark testing + Vision Refine comparison experiments). Meanwhile, on Tianhe clusters, data preparation for MimicGen was completed, environment fingerprint crashes in the M14 tripartite evaluation were fixed, and OOM issues in Pi0.5 full fine-tuning were resolved. Pi0.5 LoRA training (Job 46553) successfully reached stable operation.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Three CPU acceleration methods for MIHD fusion training (vectorization + GPUization + batching) — Comprehensive optimization was applied to three files: STAIGTrainer.py, STAIGTrainerE2E.py, and QFormerFusion.py. O(n²) nested loops were replaced with scipy cdist (initialization phase, ~100-500x acceleration). Adaptive dropout adjacency random number generation was moved to GPU (eliminating the approximately 1-2s synchronization overhead between GPU and CPU per epoch). A batch forward interface was added to QFormerFusion (forward_batched() includes key_padding_mask + pre-built padded tensor, expected to accelerate by 20-50x). 🔄 Pi0.5 LoRA fine-tuning training (Job 46551→46553, in progress) — After experiencing OOM issues during full fine-tuning (pi0.5 training state requires ~62GB of space on an A800, with FSDP=4 being ineffective), the architecture was switched to gemma_2b_lora+gemma_300m_lora (memory reduced to ~22.5GB). Job 46551 was submitted through sbatch from step 3000, but exited due to incorrect stdout buffering. After adding PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1+stdbuf+ERR trap and restarting with –resume, it currently operates stably at an average of 2.0s/step with a loss of 0.068, expected to complete in about 53 hours. 🔄 M14 tripartite evaluation (m14_cpu/Pi0/Pi0.5) initiation and environment fingerprint fixing — After fixing the M14_cpu restart due to mismatch between MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID, Pi0 (an49 GPU6, port 5556) and Pi0.5 (an49 GPU5, port 5557) VLA evaluations were initiated. All three scenarios crashed at scene 122/649 (about 130 natural_* scenario xml hashes were incompatible). After adding try-except for EnvironmentMismatchError in collector.py and resuming with –resume, m14_cpu and m14_pi05 completed with exit code 0, while m14_pi0 is still in progress. ✅ Key bug fixes to evaluate_mimicgen.py and collector.py — In evaluate_mimicgen.py: added env.seed() for reproducibility, verified state dimension of 8D, fixed the in-place array modification bug in quat2axisangle (using np.clip+copy instead). In collector.py: EnvironmentMismatchError was caught in collect_on_scenes(), warnings were recorded and incompatible scenarios were skipped to prevent the entire evaluation process from crashing. ✅ STAIG fusion supports any vision encoder (decoupling UNI hard dependencies) — Constants were renamed (STAIG_FUSION_VISION_ENCODER → STAIG_FUSION_DEFAULT_VISION_ENCODER). A new STAIG_UNI_FAMILY collection was added ({uni, uni2}). The branch logic in the four files extraction_planner.py/evaluation_planner.py/phase2_evaluate.py/run_benchmark.py was updated. UNI series automatically used staig_strict preprocessing, while other encoders used standard. Pyc cache cleanup resolved ImportError issues. ✅ Benchmarking STAIG fusion on all 11 DLPFC slices — pca+uni2+staig_fusion and none+uni2+staig_fusion were run on all 11 DLPFC slices. Compatibility bugs with UNI2 patch_size (resizing from 256×256 to 224×224) and NaN KMeans fallback were fixed. Success was achieved in 10/11 slices, with an average ARI of 0.546 and NMI of 0.639. 151676 known collapses occurred (ARI=0). ✅ M13 CPU analysis set of three (baseline report + classifier reliability + error type discrimination) — On m14_cpu with 726 episodes, 4_analyze_results.py, 7_classifier_reliability.py (fixing missing tabulate), and 8_error_type_discriminability.py (fixing crash in int(‘False’)) were run, producing six analysis files. Fleiss’ kappa was -0.02 (poor), drop↔grasp_slip kappa was 0.71 (non-redundancy verification failed), and SR was zero (CPU baseline could not be restored). ✅ Comparison experiment of MIHD Vision Refine vs Baseline (8 fusion methods) — On slice 151673, a full comparison of concat/mean/element_wise_sum/attention/basic_contrastive/adaln_attention/llava_mlp/qformer with or without scan_cluster refine was conducted. Visualizations of three sub-charts were generated (GT | Baseline | Refine), and the results were summarized in vision_refine_summary_151673.txt. qformer baseline had an ARI of 0.4832, which was the best among all experiments. refine is beneficial for weak fusion (attention +0.086) but harmful for strong fusion (-0.054). Implementation and Fixes ✅ MimicGen dataset preparation (HDF5→LeRoBot conversion + normalization statistics) — The 9 core tasks of MimicGen were converted to LeRoBot format (4500 sets, 1,034,176 frames). Compatibility issues with datasets 4.4.1 (downgraded to 3.6.0) and bugs in the prev_delta_indices property of lerobot source code were fixed. Then, 16159 batches of normalization statistics were calculated (taking about 3.5 hours), and norm_stats.json was output. ✅ Project overview update.md (number of scenarios increased from 454 to 649, version v4.12) — The number of scenarios was comprehensively updated (from 454 to 649, with 9 types of errors). Milestones were updated, small goals were completed, and the version history was updated (v4.11.1 → v4.12), along with actual values for gap analysis. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. The state of pi0.5 full fine-tuning training (params+optimizer+EMA) requires ~62GB/GPU space. Even with FSDP=4 splitting, the initial phase still results in each GPU temporarily holding the entire model due to replicated_sharding, leading to repeated OOM issues. Solution: Switch to LoRA fine-tuning (gemma_2b_lora+gemma_300m_lora). The trainable parameters are reduced by ~90%, and the memory requirement is reduced to ~22.5GB/GPU.
Key insight: JAX FSDP only splits parameter storage but does not reduce the activation memory during forward propagation. A single A800 with 80GB of memory cannot accommodate pi0.5 full fine-tuning. LoRA is the only feasible solution for an A800 cluster. When encountering the warning “Can’t reduce memory use below 62.46GiB,” switch to LoRA immediately instead of continuing with FSDP configuration.
2. All three M14 evaluations crashed at scene 122/649: Natural capture scenarios (~130 natural_*) had inconsistent xml hashes compared to the current mimicgen environment, causing EnvironmentMismatchError without any detection, which led to the entire evaluation process crashing. Solution: Add try-except for EnvironmentMismatchError in collector.py’s collect_on_scenes() for each scenario. Record warnings and skip that scenario, then resume the three-part evaluation with –resume after fixing the issue.
Key insight: The 649 scenario database contains two types of environments: ~519 impulse/augmented-compatible scenarios (xml hashes match) and ~130 natural capture scenarios (generated in a VLA environment, with different xml hashes). The actual valid evaluation targets for M14 are ~519 scenarios, so the number of target episodes needs to be adjusted accordingly.
3. UNI2 (ViT-H/14, patch_size=14) is incompatible with STAIG strict mode’s 256×256 patch input (256/14 = 18.28 not an integer, AssertionError). Solution: When detecting STAIG mode during UNI2 extraction, automatically resize patches from 256×256 to 224×224 before batch inference (since 224 is divisible by 14) if STAIG mode is detected.
Key insight: UNI v1 supports any input with dynamic_img_size=True. UNI2 is standard for ViT-H/14. The 256×256 patch in STAIG was designed for UNI v1 and needs to be adapted to the patch_size constraints when transplanted to new models.
4. Training logs caused serious inaccuracies due to stdout buffering: Pi0.5 training seemed to only run ~580 steps, but actually reached step 3000, leading to an incorrect determination of training termination. Solution: Add PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 and stdbuf -oL in the training script and sbatch submission scripts to ensure real-time log updates. Also add ERR trap and background GPU memory monitoring.
Key insight: All long-training scripts should standardize with PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1. Otherwise, stdout buffering will cause significant inaccuracies in progress monitoring, leading to unnecessary restarts.
5. Dual problems with Slurm resource management: srun –overlap blocks the command execution on allocated jobs when running commands; long-training processes are terminated by SIGTERM/SIGKILL (exit code 137/143) after an interactive session times out. Solution: Use srun –jobid=XXXXX –overlap to resolve resource conflicts and run commands on allocated nodes. For long-training tasks, use sbatch to submit independent batch jobs, decoupling them from the interactive session lifecycle.
Key insight: Interactive development (srun) should be strictly separated from long-training (sbatch). Sbatch jobs have independent resource allocation and lifecycle management, making them the correct way for cluster training.
6. For DLPFC slice 151676, STAIG training loss became NaN from the first epoch, and KMeans fallback crashed due to NaN. Solution: Add nan_to_num cleanup in the KMeans fallback path. Training collapses are known issues (high temperature tau=30 + special data distribution) and have no fundamental solution.
Key insight: 151676 is an abnormal slice and may require separate adjustments of tau/dropout rate/graph construction parameters for a dedicated investigation.
General Issues 7. After multiple training failures, zombie GPU processes (nvitop, previous training) occupied over 70GB of memory, preventing new training from acquiring sufficient memory. All OOM issues were caused by these zombie processes rather than configuration problems. Solution: Use fuser /dev/nvidiaX and kill -9 to clean up zombie processes one by one. Pay special attention to nvitop, which holds a CUDA context even when there is no active training.
Key insight: Before starting training, it’s necessary to check and clear all non-essential GPU processes. Monitoring tools like nvitop are common sources of hidden memory usage.
8. Python .pyc cache causes module modifications not to take effect: After renaming constants, phase2_evaluate.py references were omitted (ImportError); after modifying config.py’s batch_size/fsdp_devices, the cache was not cleared (configuration did not take effect). Solution: After modifying module constants or configuration, actively clear all pycache and .pyc files to ensure Python is recompiled with the latest code.
Key insight: Python bytecode caching is a common pitfall in rapid iteration development. After renaming constants, it’s necessary to globally search for all references before submitting the modification to avoid omissions.
9. MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0 does not match CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=5, causing an AssertionError crash when starting m14_cpu evaluation. Solution: Set MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID to the same GPU number as the string CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES (both should be set to 5).
Key insight: Robosuite’s EGL binding implements assertions through string inclusion. MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be the actual GPU number in CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, not a relative index after mapping.
10. Collection of dataset and script compatibility issues: Datasets 4.4.1 are incompatible with lerobot 0.1.0 (column access API changes), int(‘False’) in scripts/8 causes a crash (JSONL boolean values are serialized as strings), tabulate is not installed. Solution: Downgrade datasets to 3.6.0 and re-convert the dataset. When reading success fields, add ‘True’/‘False’ strings to the bool conversion and protect against kruskal degeneration cases. Install tabulate with pip.### Key Insights
The compatibility of Python dependencies in shared cluster environments requires verification one by one during the first run. Boolean values in JSONL may be converted to strings after being serialized using json.dumps, so explicit conversion is necessary when reading them.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Architecture Discovery Based on a Database of 649 Scenarios Role Approach Human Assuming that all 649 scenarios can be evaluated, no compatibility issues were预见ed. AI After the evaluation in three stages failed in scene 122, AI independently identified two incompatible environments in the database through analyzing EnvironmentMismatchError: ~519 impulse-compatible and ~130 natural_*-compatible. It proposed a scene-level try-except fix and realized that the actual number of valid scenarios should be ~519 Difference Analysis: This was the most important architectural discovery of the day, independently identified by AI during the debugging process. AI’s problem-solving ability is truly valuable here. However, if we had checked the scenario fingerprint distribution in meta.json in advance, we could have avoided issues before starting the evaluation.
Identification of CPU Performance Bottlenecks and Training Acceleration Role Approach Human Based on human intuition, it was concluded that performing CPU-intensive tasks for each epoch is the root cause of slow training. The general direction of “preprocessing acceleration” was proposed, and a phased execution plan with precise command-line parameters was designed in advance. AI AI systematically analyzed three key files, precisely identified three bottlenecks: O(n²) initialization loops, GPU↔CPU transfer per epoch, and individual Python loops. Quantitative analysis showed that 2700 spots × 200 epochs = 540,000 independent forward operations were required. A complete implementation plan with boundary handling was designed, but several unexpected runtime bugs were found during execution Difference Analysis: Humans provided strategic direction and architectural judgment, while AI was responsible for quantitative analysis, detailed implementation, and debugging adaptation. AI’s judgment on FSDP memory savings (believing that FSDP=4 could be reduced to ~16GB, although it still resulted in OOM) and new model constraints pre-checking (UNI2 patch_size) was not very accurate.
Multi-GPU Parallel Training Strategies and Memory Management Role Approach Human The user actively proposed using multiple GPUs for parallel training, prompting AI to explore FSDP solutions. The user also took a pragmatic approach to starting training with some data (to verify as early as possible). AI AI was overly optimistic about FSDP memory optimization in JAX. It switched to the LoRA architecture only after several OOMs. Its judgment on dataset format integrity constraints was correct (no partial training allowed). Switching to LoRA was the right solution, but it should have been identified earlier from JAX warnings." Difference Analysis: The user’s intuition about parallelization was correct, but AI had misunderstandings about FSDP principles (only splitting storage, not reducing activated memory). Architectural solutions like LoRA should have been proposed earlier.
Implementation Level Plan Mode Workflow and AI’s Autonomous Execution Boundaries Role Approach Human The user twice refused AI’s automatic call to ExitPlanMode, explicitly requiring review of the plan before approval. It was understood that the plan is a user decision checkpoint. AI After writing the plan, AI directly tried to call ExitPlanMode to proceed with execution without waiting for the user’s confirmation." Difference Analysis: AI failed to correctly understand Plan Mode semantics—the plan requires explicit user approval, not an automatic signal from AI. This reflects AI’s insufficient understanding of the workflow pattern where “the plan is a user decision checkpoint.”
AI Limitations Major Limitations Misunderstanding of JAX FSDP memory optimization principles: Believing that FSDP=4 could reduce each GPU’s memory to about 16GB (62/4), but in reality, FSDP only splits parameter storage. The full model still needs to be loaded during initialization, and the forward activation memory does not decrease. LoRA training (gemma_2b_lora) should have reduced memory requirements to ~22.5GB, making it the only feasible solution for the A800 cluster. Failure to pre-check model architecture constraints and data compatibility: During implementing UNI2 compatibility, no check was made on ViT-H/14’s patch_size divisibility requirements. The scenario fingerprint distribution in meta.json was not checked before evaluation. Only fixes were found after runtime crashes, leading to additional debugging cycles. Mistakes in monitoring progress of long-term tasks: PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 was not set in the training script, resulting in misjudgment of log buffering and progress (580 steps → actual 3000 steps). Training was restarted multiple times without checking whether GPU memory was actually released. Multiple OOMs were caused by zombie processes rather than configuration issues. General Limitations Incomplete cache cleanup and reference searching after code modifications: After renaming constants, not all references were searched immediately (scripts/phase2_evaluate.py was missed). After modifying config.py, pycache was not cleared, leading to ImportError or ineffective configuration during runtime. Additional fixes were required. Insufficient understanding of Plan Mode workflow: Multiple attempts to call ExitPlanMode after writing the plan were rejected by the user. It was not understood that the plan is a user decision checkpoint, not an automatic signal from AI. Today’s Takeaways Key Takeaways For pi0.5 full training state (parameters + AdamW + EMA), ~62GB of memory is required. A single A800 cannot handle full fine-tuning. JAX FSDP only splits parameter storage, so it does not affect the loading of the full model during initialization and the forward activation memory. LoRA fine-tuning (gemma_2b_lora) reduces memory requirements to ~22.5GB, making it the only feasible solution for the A800 cluster. The 649-scenario database consists of two categories: ~519 impulse/pose_perturb/augmented-compatible scenarios (xml_hash matches), and ~130 natural_* natural capture scenarios (generated in a VLA environment with cameras enabled, with different xml hashes). In M14 evaluation, the actual number of valid scenarios was ~519. The number of target episodes and all documentation need to be updated accordingly. Vision Refine has a bimodal effect on Fusion: scan_cluster refine (1536d → 256d dimensionality reduction) is beneficial for weak fusion (attention +0.086 ARI), but harmful for strong fusion (qformer -0.054). Strong fusion (QFormer) can handle high-dimensional inputs, but reducing dimensions actually loses information. The optimal result on that day was qformer+no-refine (ARI=0.4832). Vectorization and batching acceleration are highly effective in scientific computing: O(n²) nested loops → cdist matrix operations are approximately 100-500x faster; Python loop iteration → batch GPU forward is expected to be 20-50x faster. This is the core method for accelerating space group training, and it also verifies the general optimization strategy of “preprocessing instead of calculating per epoch.” Best practices for Slurm cluster training engineering: Long-term training requires using sbatch (not srun) to avoid session timeout due to SIGTERM. All training scripts should include PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 + stdbuf -oL. Before starting training, use fuser /dev/nvidiaX to clean up zombie GPU processes (monitoring tools like nvitop also hold CUDA context). MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be exactly the same as the number of GPUs in the CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES string. STAIG fusion is an end-to-end refine+fuse integrated solution (GCN encodes spatial relationships). Adding scan_cluster refine at the input end leads to functional overlap and information redundancy. Neither should be used together. DLPFC slice 151676 has a stable training collapse problem (NaN from epoch 1), requiring independent adjustment of tau/dropout rate/figure construction parameters. Preliminary analysis conclusion for M13: Random+BC-RNN two CPU baselines have SR=0 in all scenarios, with Fleiss’ kappa = -0.02 (poor), indicating no significant discrimination—because the baseline strategies simply cannot succeed, lacking SR variability.有意义的统计结果需要VLA (Pi0/Pi0.5) data. Session Summary MIHD ✅ Comprehensive optimization of MIHD fusion training: CPU acceleration in three aspects + architecture decoupling + Vision Refine comparison + STAIG full slice benchmark 00:02:10.477 | claude_code Completed six tasks on DCC’s RTX 5000 Ada nodes: ① Implement cdist vectorization (100-500x), GPU-based dropout random numbers, and batch forward operations for STAIGTrainer/STAIGTrainerE2E/QFormerFusion; ② Remove staig_fusion’s hard dependency on UNI, add support for any visual encoder in the STAIG_UNI_FAMILY set, and clear pyc cache to solve ImportError; ③ Complete full comparison experiments with and without scan_cluster refine for 8 fusion methods on slice 151673, generating three-dimensional visualization. It was found that qformer baseline ARI=0.4832 is optimal, refinement is beneficial for weak fusion (attention +0.086), and harmful for strong fusion (qformer -0.054); ④ Fix UNI2 patch_size compatibility bugs (256→224 resize) and NaN KMeans fallback; ⑤ Complete benchmark tests for all 11 DLPFC slices using STAIG fusion, with 10/11 successful, average ARI=0.546 (slice 151676 has known collapses).
Error Recovery Benchmark • Setup of pi0.5 LoRA training process + M14 three-way evaluation and key bug fixes + M13 CPU analysis 00:00:18.651 | claude_code Completed two main tasks on Tianhe cluster. [Training line] Converted 9 MimicGen tasks to LeRoBot format (4500 sets, 1 million frames), fixed datasets version compatibility issues (downgraded to 3.6.0) and lerobot source code bugs; normalization took 3.5 hours. After repeated OOMs during full fine-tuning (pi0.5 training state requires ~62GB of memory, which exceeds A800 capacity, JAX FSDP is ineffective), switched to LoRA architecture and submitted Job 46551 through sbatch. The first run ended at step 3000 due to misjudgment caused by stdout buffering; restarted with PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 as Job 46553 (an46, 2.0s/step, loss=0.068, expected duration: 53 hours). [Evaluation line] Fixed bugs such as missing tabulate, int(‘False’) type conversion, and kruskal degeneration. Completed M13 CPU analysis with three pieces of equipment (kappa=-0.02, SR zero, requires VLA data for statistical significance); fixed MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID mismatch issues before starting m14_cpu/Pi0/Pi0.5 three-way evaluation. All failed in scene 122 due to EnvironmentMismatchError (identified 649 scenarios containing ~130 incompatible natural_* scenarios). Added scene-level try-except fixes to collector.py, then resumed with –resume. m14_cpu and m14_pi05 completed, m14_pi0 is still running. Synchronized update of project overview.md (number of scenarios increased from 454 to 649, version 4.12).

title: 缺陷日志 2026-02-23

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W08 (2026-02-16 ~ 2026-02-22) This week was focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. We completed a systematic investigation of the H&E Image-Only clustering method (established a literature baseline for ARI between 0.11–0.16), implemented three self-supervised enhancement schemes (SCAN improved ARI from 0.251 to 0.303, +20.6%), and established a two-stage fusion framework for Vision Refinement. Meanwhile, we parallelly carried out the Error Recovery Benchmark on the tianhe cluster (M14 evaluation of infrastructure verification, full-scenario evaluation started at 649 scenarios), and built up the data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5 (18.4GB MimicGen dataset stored, training configuration ready). Throughout the week, we resolved several engineering issues such as STEGO NaN, double normalization bug, lerobot version conflict, and HuggingFace proxy issues. The OOM issues of Pi0.5 and video verification for visualize_scene.py were postponed to next week.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-16 ~ 2026-02-22 Active Days 3 / 7 Total Conversations 9 Projects Involved 4 Completed Tasks 11 Ongoing Tasks 5 Total Tokens 28,509,501 Total Cost $14.13 Average Cost per Day $4.71 Project Progress MIHD (Spatial Transcriptomics Clustering) (3 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Completed four core technical documents: RM-IDEAL bilingual instructions, guidelines for using visual encoders, literature review of pathological PFM, evaluation analysis of UNI/UNI2 original papers Conducted a systematic investigation of the H&E Image-Only method, established a literature baseline for DLPFC ARI (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11), and analyzed five root causes of Foundation Model failure Implemented and verified three self-supervised enhancement schemes: STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN. SCAN achieved the best result with ARI=0.303 (baseline 0.251, +20.6%). We also verified the complementary nature of its embedding with gene features (mean fusion +0.065 ARI) Identified and fixed a double normalization bug in eval_scan_fusion.py for STAIG. Decided to use the correct path in run_benchmark.py instead of fixing the independent script Integrated CacheManager embedding caching in run_benchmark.py (loaded in seconds, supports custom cache names for scan_uni2) Implemented a two-stage fusion framework for Vision Refinement (~60 lines of minimal intrusion). Started batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies in the background Re-generated all 11 sections visualizations in batches (using .npz caching, no need for re-inference), and added an H&E original image panel Blocked Items:
⚠️ The coordinate dimension bug in eval_scan_fusion.py caused incomplete debugging of the SCAN fusion evaluation script ⚠️ The first experiment with Vision Refinement (scan_cluster + concat, ARI=0.313) was lower than direct concat (0.387). The issue of diversity of self-supervised compression loss features needs further analysis ⚠️ The implementation of ImageEncoder for the big goal 7 of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md has not yet started Error Recovery Benchmark & Phoenix pi0.5 Replica (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Developed a complete Phase II execution plan covering 7 steps, covering G1–G7 big goals and M12–M15 milestones. The critical path is about 16 days M14 baseline evaluation of infrastructure verification (10 scenarios passed sanity check), full-scenario CPU evaluation started at 649 scenarios (surpassing expectations by 454, +43%) Completed the full data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5: convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py, evaluate_mimicgen.py, OpenPI training configuration (100K steps, 4-GPUs), and downloaded 18.4GB MimicGen dataset with format conversion completed in tasks 7-8/9 Diagnosed the OOM bottleneck of Pi0.5, standardized GPU access from SSH to srun –overlap Resolved compatibility issues between lerobot 0.1.0 and datasets>=4.0 (downgraded to 3.6.0) Established hf-mirror.com as the standard data acquisition solution for the cluster’s HuggingFace Blocked Items:
⚠️ The OOM issue of Pi0.5 remains unresolved (GPU memory insufficient by 150MB), and baseline evaluation is still blocked ⚠️ The video verification of visualize_scene.py is blocked due to SLURM node permissions issues ⚠️ The Pi0 VLA Server port conflict (port 5555 occupied) caused session interruptions Key Tasks ✅ Systematic investigation of H&E Image-Only clustering method (2026-02-19) — Collected information on methods such as MILWRM/F-SEG/Deep Contrastive Clustering. Verified the value of DLPFC ARI in image-only experiments (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11). Conducted in-depth research on the application of BYOL/STEGO/SCAN in pathology. Reviewed frameworks for addressing domain gaps in the CV community ✅ Established MIHD technical documentation system (2026-02-19) — Created four core technical documents: RM-IDEAL bilingual structure document, guidelines for using visual encoders (12 chapters), literature review of pathological PFM, evaluation analysis of UNI/UNI2 original papers (34 clinical tasks) ✅ Analyzed root causes of Foundation Model failure in spatial domain recognition (2026-02-19) — Conducted a systematic analysis from five dimensions: training data domain mismatch, pre-training task mismatch, minimal morphological differences between brain tissue layers, feature redundancy, and lack of spatial context. Provided case studies using the UNI2 brown repetitive patch phenomenon ✅ Implemented Image-Only clustering enhancement schemes (STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN) (2026-02-19) — Created four model files: STEGOHead/BYOLAdapter/SpatialGATRefiner/SCANHead. Conducted comparative tests on 151673 sections, with SCAN achieving the best result with ARI=0.303 (+20.6%) ✅ Investigated double normalization bug in STAIG fusion and made architectural decisions (2026-02-20) — Confirmed that not passing staig_alignment_config in eval_scan_fusion.py caused StandardScaler to be used again within STAIGTrainer. Decided to use run_benchmark.py instead of fixing the independent script ✅ Integrated embedding caching mechanism in run_benchmark.py (2026-02-20) — Introduced CacheManager, loading cache before gene/vision encoding and skipping encoder instantiation if hit. Supported custom cache names for scan_uni2, with vision caches available in standard/freq/staig_strict variants 🔄 M14 baseline evaluation of infrastructure verification and full-scenario evaluation started (2026-02-22) — sanity check (60 episodes, about 7 minutes per episode, SR=0% as expected). Subsequently, started full CPU evaluation with 649 scenarios × 2 strategies × 3 seeds (about 3894 episodes), running in the background 🔄 Established the complete data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5 (2026-02-22) — Written convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py and evaluate_mimicgen.py, configured pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training parameters. Downloaded 18.4GB MimicGen dataset through hf-mirror.com (9 tasks with 9000 demos), completed format conversion in tasks 7-8/9 🔄 Joint evaluation of SCAN embedding and multi-modal fusion (2026-02-19) — Written eval_scan_fusion.py to compare the optimized 256-dimensional visual embedding with PCA gene features across all fusion methods. Mean fusion achieved ARI +0.065, partially fixed the coordinates dimension bug, script debugging in progress 🔄 Implemented two-stage fusion framework for MIHD Vision Refinement and batch experiments (2026-02-22) — Added –vision_refine parameter (scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial), with minimal intrusion of about 60 lines of code. The first experiment achieved ARI=0.313, which was lower than baseline concat 0.387. Batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies are running in the background ✅ Formulated complete execution plan for Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II (2026-02-22) — Analyzed dependencies between G1–G7 big goals and M12–M15 milestones, developed an execution plan covering 7 steps. Clarified GPU allocation strategy (srun –overlap with at least 50% free memory), with a critical path of about 16 days ✅ Diagnosed Error Recovery Benchmark baseline issues and updated GPU access specifications (2026-02-22) — Confirmed that Pi0.5 OOM (150MB insufficient GPU memory) and BC-RNN obs key issues have been resolved, and 649 scenarios are ready. Standardized GPU access methods in CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md to srun –overlap 🚫 Force parameter expansion for visualize_scene.py (2026-02-22) — Completed adding force_override/duration_override/settle_steps parameters and Phase 3 neutral action logic. Video verification is still blocked due to SLURM node permissions issues ✅ Special comparison experiment with STAIG fusion (151673) (2026-02-20) — Tested staig_fusion × {UNI2_raw, SCAN(UNI2)} separately. ARI values were 0.3929/0.3880 respectively, almost equal. Verified that StandardScaler + PCA within STAIG offset the optimization effect of SCAN ✅ Added H&E panel to UNI2 visualization and updated in batches (2026-02-19) — Modified to a 1×3 layout (H&E + GT + prediction). Re-generated all 11 sections visualizations using .npz caching, with 151510 successfully repaired through hires→lowres symlinks Issues and Solutions 1. STEGO training loss was NaN throughout the process. A dense similarity matrix of 3639×3639 caused overflow when temperature=0.07 [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Two-step fix: Normalize the input image_emb; replace InfoNCE with a numerically stable version (reduce maximum value before taking logsumexp, increase temperature to 0.1)
2. MILWRM was incorrectly classified as an Image-Only method. The first AI summary mixed multi-modal methods with image-only results. The conclusions of the first research need significant revision [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Use WebFetch to read the full PMC text to confirm that MILWRM actually relies on gene expression. Specifically extract image-only data points from ablation experiments in articles like SpaConTDS
3. There was a double normalization bug in staig_fusion in eval_scan_fusion.py (StandardScaler used again within STAIGTrainer) [MIHD] (2026-02-20) Solution: Abandon fixing the independent script and directly use run_benchmark.py, which correctly passes staig_alignment_config. Reuse the verified path
4. In run_benchmark.py, the encoder was re-instantiated to extract embeddings every time, lacking pipeline-level caching support [MIHD] (2026-02-20) Solution: Integrate CacheManager from pipeline/cache_manager.py. Load cache before encoding and write it back after extraction. Skip encoder instantiation if hit
5. Lerobot 0.1.0 was incompatible with datasets>=4.0 (torch.stack reported TypeError: Column object replaced list) [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Downgrade datasets from 4.4.1 to 3.6.0; for datasets 4.0, change the return value of dataset[‘column’] from list to Column object. Lerobot expects a list/tuple of tensors
6. HuggingFace official download failed due to proxy (Squid 503), and Python download script couldn’t connect [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Use hf-mirror.com + wget instead. URL format: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过集群 HTTP proxy available at 40–200MB/s
7. Unstable access to Slurm GPU nodes: srun without –overlap caused commands to hang, direct SSH connection was unreliable, multi-partition submissions were rejected [Error Recovery Benchmark / Phoenix pi0.5] (2026-02-22) Solution: Standardize the workflow: source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid= –overlap; prohibit direct SSH to GPU nodes
8. In eval_scan_fusion.py, there was an anomaly in spatial coordinate dimensions (became (1,2)), causing errors for multiple fusion methods [MIHD] (2026-02-19) Solution: Abandon calling load_spatial_coordinates() (barcode matching failed). Instead, directly read coordinates from adata.obsm[‘spatial’]. Fix the error in unpacking return values of load_dlpfc_data
Learnings Domain Knowledge### Architecture SCAN is the most effective method for image-only spatial transcriptomics tasks, with an ARI of 0.303 and a gain of +20.6%. Its core advantage lies in offline feature k-NN mining, which decouples embedding learning from clustering. The 256-dimensional optimized embeddings, when fused with gene features, show strong complementarity (mean fusion +0.065 ARI). Two-stage fusion is not necessarily superior to one-stage fusion: After compressing the visual embedding from 1536d to 256d using scan_cluster, the ARI of multi-modal fusion (0.313) is lower than that of direct concatenation (0.387). This indicates that self-supervised compression may lose the original feature diversity required for fusion tasks. The goals of self-supervised clustering optimization and downstream fusion tasks are inconsistent. STAIG fusion uses StandardScaler+PCA preprocessing to absorb the effects of external embedding optimization (the difference in ARI between SCAN and UNI2_raw is only 0.005). Embeddings should be saved as raw embeddings before normalization, and post-encoder normalization should be performed after loading to ensure consistent results across different call paths. VLA baseline evaluations must clearly state the source of checkpoints: Models fine-tuned on non-target datasets (pi0_libero, pi05_base) are evaluated for zero-shot cross-domain recovery capabilities. The paper must explicitly state this experimental setup, and additional comparative experiments after fine-tuning are needed to fully demonstrate the usefulness of the dataset. In complex experimental systems, it is advisable to reuse existing, validated tool paths (such as run_benchmark.py). Independent scripts can easily introduce hidden bugs due to inconsistent preprocessing. Before calling internal functions within the project, the source code should be read in real-time to verify function signatures, rather than relying on memory. Debugging When calculating large-scale dense similarity matrices (n>3000) using InfoNCE with comparative loss, it is necessary to use numerically stable log-sum-exp (minus the row maximum). Otherwise, exponential operations with temperature=0.07 under float32 precision may overflow and cause NaN values. This is a key engineering constraint for implementing large-scale comparative learning. Tools Lerobot 0.1.0 has strict constraints on dataset versions; it should be pinned to <4.0 (recommended 3.x). Dataset 4.0 changes the return value of dataset[‘column’] to a Column object, causing a TypeError in torch.stack. MimicGen is fully compatible with LIBERO obs/action format (84×84 images, 8D states, 7D actions), and can directly reuse OpenPI’s LeRobotLiberoDataConfig. The standard way to access HuggingFace from Chinese mainland HPC clusters is via hf-mirror.com. The URL format is: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过 wget + HTTP proxy with a speed of 40–200MB/s. This should be the default approach. OpenPI’s JAX training natively supports multi-GPU data parallelism. Just specify the list of GPUs using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, and JAX will automatically build a 2D mesh parallel without modification to TrainConfig. Slurm –overlap is a key parameter for running new commands on existing interactive jobs, and it is a core technique for calling cluster GPU nodes in the Claude Code environment. Notes on AI Usage Effective Patterns: ✓ Parallel sub-tasks are used to explore project structures and develop cleanup plans (MIHD organizes tasks), reducing the time spent on serial exploration. ✓ Three methods (STEGO/BYOL/SCAN) are executed in parallel on GPUs to compare and verify, significantly reducing the total experiment time. ✓ Using .npz caches allows for batch re-generation of visualizations, completely decoupling inference from visualization. This worked well with 11/11 sections. ✓ Independent temporary scripts (_test_staig_scan.py) isolate tests for individual fusion methods, reducing the task duration from 2 hours to 30 seconds. ✓ Independent exploration of JAX’s multi-GPU mechanism (no need to modify config) and alternative download solutions via hf-mirror.com are independent tasks that do not require user intervention. ✓ The minimum invasive code modification (about 60 lines) is made in run_benchmark.py by inserting the Vision Refinement stage, maintaining the stability of the architecture. Limitations: ✗ Inaccurate classification of literature: MILWRM was incorrectly classified as an image-only method. Initial research results were mixed with multi-modal results, requiring two interventions from users to converge to the correct research boundary. ✗ Lack of awareness to actively expose key experimental assumptions: The paper did not explicitly state how VLA evaluation using LIBERO fine-tuned checkpoints affects the validity of the experiment. This topic was only discussed after user inquiries. ✗ API usage tends to rely on memory rather than real-time verification: eval_scan_fusion.py had multiple errors in function signatures/return values. The source code should be read in before calling. ✗ Slurm permission issues led to multiple attempts to access partitions (5+ times). The correct account/partition information was not quickly obtained from users. ✗ Insufficient status monitoring of background tasks: Data conversion was still ongoing when LeRobot dataset verification was triggered, resulting in false timestamp violation errors. Frequent sleep polling was interrupted multiple times. ✗ In the HPC environment, CPU is used for model validation by default, hiding real performance issues and causing additional interaction problems. Next Week’s Plans Next week will focus on three parallel tasks: ①MIHD: Fixing bugs in eval_scan_fusion.py to evaluate SCAN embeddings and full fusion strategies. Analyze batch experiment results of 7 fusion strategies and diagnose the root causes of Vision Refinement compression loss features. Consider adjusting refinement hidden_dim or switching between stego_refine/byol_spatial methods. ②Error Recovery Benchmark: Organize results of the full CPU evaluation for M14 649 scenarios to solve Pi0.5 OOM issues (request for larger memory GPUs or reduce batch size). Start training Phoenix pi0.5 with 100K steps on 4-GPUs. ③Engineering Block Elimination: Resolve SLURM node permission issues to complete video verification of visualize_scene.py. Address Pi0 VLA server port conflicts (detect using lsof -i:5555 + alternate port mechanism) and advance the implementation of ImageEncoder enhancement in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md.

Claude Code Metric Value Total Tokens 28,509,501 Input Tokens 93,255 Output Tokens 16,437 Cache Creation 2,761,832 Cache Reading 25,637,977 Total Cost $14.13 Model Usage Distribution Model Cost ($) Input Tokens Output Tokens claude-opus-4-6 9.57 15,496 14,319 claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 4.14 77,744 2,084 claude-sonnet-4-6 0.42 15 34
summary: This week, we focused on the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. We completed a systematic investigation into H&E Image-Only clustering (establishing a literature baseline for ARI values ranging from 0.11 to 0.16), implemented three self-supervised enhancement methods (SCAN improved ARI from 0.251 to 0.303, a +20.6% increase), and established a two-stage framework for Vision Refinement. Meanwhile, we continued to work on Error Recovery Benchmarking on the tianhe cluster (M14 evaluation of infrastructure, initialization of full-scenario evaluations with 649 scenarios). We also prepared the Phoenix pi0.5 data pipeline (storage of 18.4GB MimicGen dataset, training configuration ready). Throughout the week, we resolved several engineering issues, including STEGO NaN, dual normalization bugs, version conflicts with lerobot, and problems with HuggingFace proxies. The remaining issues related to Pi0.5 OOM and visualizing scenes via video verification will be addressed next week.
title: 每周总结 2026-W08

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-22 Today’s Highlights What was done: Parallel progress was made on DCC and tianhe devices. On DCC, the MIHD project documentation was organized, and a two-phase fusion of Vision Refinement was implemented. On tianhe, blockage due to Error Recovery Benchmark baseline (Pi0.5 OOM) was diagnosed, M14 full CPU evaluation started, and a complete data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5_base was built from scratch (script writing → data download → format conversion → training configuration). How it was done: On DCC, a minimal invasive modification (about 60 lines of code) was added to run_benchmark.py to implement the refining phase. On tianhe, tasks were executed using Slurm srun –overlap on 8×A800 nodes. Data download was completed through hf-mirror.com without proxy restrictions. The datasets were downgraded to version 3.6.0 to fix lerobot compatibility issues, and support for multi-GPUs in JAX was investigated. What’s achieved: The structure of the MIHD project was significantly improved. The first experiment with Vision Refinement revealed key phenomena related to self-supervised compression loss fusion features. The M14 evaluation infrastructure was verified and full operation started. The Phoenix pi0.5 dataset (18.4GB, 4500 episodes) was stored, and training configurations were ready for 100K-step 4-GPU training. DCC What was done: MIHD project documentation was organized (about 3146 redundant scripts were removed, the docs directory was reorganized, plans.md was created). A two-phase fusion framework for Vision Refinement was implemented (the –vision_refine parameter supported scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial options). Batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies were started. How it was done: After comprehensively exploring the project through parallel sub-tasks, a cleanup plan was formulated. A minimal invasive modification of about 60 lines of code was added to run_benchmark.py to maintain minimal impact. The first experiment (scan_cluster refine + concat, ARI=0.313) showed lower results than direct concatenation (ARI=0.387), revealing the features required for refined compression loss fusion. What’s achieved: The project structure became clearer. Vision Refinement functions were available and initial verifications were completed. Batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies are underway, providing new dimensions for ablation studies. tianhe What was done: Pi0.5 OOM blockage was diagnosed, and GPU access specifications were updated (SSH → Slurm). Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II was advanced (project topology updates, M14 sanity checks passed, 649 scenarios with full CPU evaluation started, Pi0 VLA server port conflicts resolved). The complete engineering foundation for Phoenix pi0.5 was completed (conversion/evaluation scripts, data download and format conversion, OpenPI training configuration). Video verification for force_override/duration_override/settle_steps parameter extensions was incomplete due to SLURM node permissions issues. How it was done: GPU tasks were submitted using srun –overlap. HuggingFace datasets were downloaded through hf-mirror.com at speeds of 40-200MB/s. The datasets were downgraded from version 4.4.1 to 3.6.0 to fix lerobot compatibility issues. JAX sharding mechanisms were investigated to confirm that CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES could achieve multi-GPU parallelism without changes to TrainConfig. What’s achieved: Pi0.5 OOM blockages were identified. The M14 evaluation pipeline was verified (649 scenarios, exceeding expectations by 454). The Phoenix pi0.5 dataset (18.4GB) was stored, and training configurations were ready. A standard approach for HuggingFace data acquisition and datasets version constraints was established. DCC completed the cleanup of the MIHD project and implemented a two-phase fusion of Vision Refinement, starting batch experiments. tianhe advanced Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II (M14 evaluation pipeline verification, Pi0.5 OOM diagnosis) and completed the construction of the complete data pipeline for Phoenix pi0.5 (9 MimicGen tasks stored with 18.4GB, training configurations ready).
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy 🔄 M14 Baseline Evaluation (sanity check passed, full CPU evaluation started) — Sanity checks were performed on 10 scenarios (60 episodes, verifying collector resume and output format, approximately 7 minutes, SR=0% as expected). Subsequently, a full evaluation of 649 scenarios × 2 strategies × 3 seeds was started on GPU nodes (approximately 3894 episodes), expected to run for 6-7 hours, with results stored in outputs/evaluation_logs/m14_cpu/. 🔄 Pi0.5 Phoenix MimicGen Complete Data Pipeline — Completed four steps of the engineering foundation: (1) wrote convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py (9 tasks HDF5 → LeRobot batch conversion, compatible with flat/nested layouts) and evaluate_mimicgen.py (9 tasks evaluation, robosuite OSC_POSE controller, per-task success rate output); (2) added pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training configuration to OpenPI config.py (batch_size=64, 100K steps, EMA 0.999); (3) downloaded 9 MimicGen datasets through hf-mirror.com (18.4GB, 9000 demos, correct structure verification); (4) ran LeRobot format conversion (7-8/9 tasks completed by session end). During this period, the datasets were downgraded to version 3.6.0 to fix compatibility issues with lerobot, and the project summary was updated to v4.11 M16. 🔄 MIHD Vision Refinement Two-Phase Fusion Implementation and Batch Experiments — Added –vision_refine parameter to run_benchmark.py (scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial). Approximately 60 lines of refining logic were added after vision encoding but before multimodal fusion. Cache paths, experiment directory names, log configs, and CSV columns were updated. The first experiment (pca+uni2+scan_cluster refine+concat, ARI=0.313) showed lower results than direct concatenation (ARI=0.387), revealing the features required for refined compression loss fusion. Batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies (mean/element_wise_sum/attention/basic_contrastive/adaln_attention/llava_mlp/qformer) are underway. ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II Complete Execution Plan — Analyzed G1-G7 major goals and M12-M15 milestone dependencies. A target topology diagram was added to the project summary.md (major goals/milestones/minor goals), and a complete execution plan with 7 steps was designed. The GPU allocation strategy was clarified (GPU memory should be at least 50% idle, using srun –overlap). The critical path took about 16 days. ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark Baseline Diagnosis and GPU Access Specification Updates — Confirmed that Pi0.5 encountered OOM (failure to allocate 150MB of GPU memory), BC-RNN obs key issues were fixed but not fully evaluated, with 649 error scenarios ready exceeding expectations by 454. Claus.md and MEMORY.md changed the GPU access method from SSH to srun –jobid (source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid=). ❌ visualize_scene.py Force Parameter Expansion (GPU Node Access Blockage) — Added force_override/duration_override/settle_steps parameters, updated Phase 3 neutral action logic and force_norm_range/force_clip configurations. However, due to SLURM node permissions issues (SSH to an53 failed, multiple partition submissions were rejected), video generation verification was incomplete. Implementation and Fixes ❌ Pi0 VLA Server Startup (Port Conflict Blockage) — Pi0 VLA server started on GPU nodes (port 5555). The pi0_libero model loaded successfully (6GB) but the port was occupied, causing binding failures. Additionally, users found issues with checkpoint selection, leading to session interruptions. Next time, lsof -i:5555 should be checked first, and a different port should be used. ✅ MIHD Project Organization and Documentation Cleanup — Removed 3 redundant scripts (about 3146 lines), cleaned up pycache and empty directories, archived 16 fragment summary files and 5 pipeline logs. The docs directory was reorganized (archived/research/experiments subdirectories). The ENHANCEMENT_PLAN content was merged into docs/plans.md, and the Claus.md was fixed to avoid broken references. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. scan_cluster refine + concat (ARI=0.313) actually shows lower results than direct concatenation (ARI=0.387). The two-phase fusion effect did not meet expectations. Solution: Continue testing other 7 fusion strategies; or adjust the refinement hidden_dim to its current default of 256; or try stego_refine/byol_spatial as the refining method.
Key Insight: SCAN lost the original feature diversity required for multimodal fusion during the process of compressing 1536d to 256d. The self-supervised clustering optimization goals were inconsistent with the downstream fusion tasks, and self-supervised compression did not always enhance subsequent fusion.
2. VLA evaluation used non-target task-specific checkpoints (pi0_libero fine-tuning version and pi05_base pre-training version, not PickPlace-exclusive models), which affected the validity of the experiment. Solution: Clearly define it as a cross-domain generalization evaluation rather than an in-task evaluation. State the rationality of the experimental setup in the paper. Add a comparison experiment after fine-tuning in M15 to fully demonstrate the usefulness of the dataset.
Key Insight: Using non-target task-specific checkpoints tests VLA’s zero-shot/cross-domain recovery capabilities. In scientific experiments, key assumptions should be actively exposed and stated; otherwise, credibility may be questioned by reviewers.
3. Lerobot 0.1.0 is not compatible with datasets >=4.0 (TypeError: torch.stack argument ’tensors’ must be tuple of Tensors, not Column). Solution: Downgrade the datasets from version 4.4.1 to 3.6.0 (<4.0). Reason: In dataset 4.0, the return value of dataset[‘column’] was changed from a list to a Column object, and Lerobot expected a list/tuple of tensors.
Key Insight: Lerobot 0.1.0 has strict requirements for dataset versions. It needs to be pinned to version <4.0. When configuring the environment, it is necessary to check the dependency compatibility matrix rather than installing the latest version by default.
4. Pi0.5 encountered OOM on tianhe cluster (failure to allocate 150MB of GPU memory), resulting in complete blockage of the baseline evaluation. Solution: Not yet resolved. It is necessary to apply for larger memory GPUs or reduce the batch size. It is recommended to test the memory peak value with a small batch first.
Key Insight: The memory requirement should be verified in advance before conducting large model experiments. Since the scale of Pi0.5 model exceeds the current GPU configuration, it should be included in the resource planning stage during experimental design.
General Issues 5. Unstable access to Slurm GPU nodes: SSH connections are unreliable; srun without the –overlap parameter causes commands to hang; multiple partition submissions are rejected. Solution: Standard workflow: source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid= –overlap. Directly connecting to GPU nodes via SSH should be prohibited. Always add –overlap when submitting new tasks.
Key Insight: In a shared HPC environment, –overlap is a key parameter for running new commands on top of existing interactive jobs. Issues with partition permissions should be quickly addressed by asking the user for the correct account and partition information rather than trying everything out.
6. HuggingFace official downloads failed due to proxy issues (Squid 503), and Python download scripts could not connect. Solution: Use hf-mirror.com + wget as an alternative download source. URL format: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过集群HTTP代理可达40-200MB/s
Key Insight: The standard solution for accessing HuggingFace on Chinese mainland HPC clusters is the hf-mirror.com mirror site, which even has a faster speed than the official API. It should be used as the default solution for cluster downloads rather than an alternative.
7. Pi0 VLA server port conflicts and SLURM node permissions caused GPU task blockages. Solution: For port conflicts: check lsof -i:5555 in advance, and include a logic to detect and automatically switch ports in the startup script. For SLURM permissions: quickly ask the user for the correct account and partition information without trying everything out.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Control over experimental design and research direction: Architecture innovation and effective nature of checkpoints questioned. Role Thought Human The user proposed an innovative two-phase fusion idea (refine vision embedding first, then multimodal fusion) and provided a complete implementation plan. At the same time, the user actively questioned the source of the checkpoints used in VLA evaluation (“What checkpoints are you using now?”). This revealed key experimental design issues between cross-domain generalization and in-task evaluation. The user also provided detailed execution instructions with complete bash commands, clarifying domain-level experimental design decisions. AI AI focused on technical implementation (minimal invasive code modifications, script execution, code status verification). It passively executed tasks rather than actively questioning research assumptions. AI did not actively explain the impact of checkpoint selection on experiment validity or propose a two-phase fusion idea. However, AI could provide practical code details (649 scenarios vs 454, compatibility with LeRobotLiberoDataConfig, etc.). Difference Analysis: Humans have intuition for combining methods and critical thinking in experimental design, able to identify innovative points at the architecture level and the systematic impact of key assumptions. AI is capable of independently exploring technical solutions but lacks the awareness to actively expose research hypotheses. Humans lead the direction of research, while AI is responsible for implementing it.
AI’s Independent Technical Exploration Ability Role Thought Human The user proposed goals (multi-GPU training, data download) but did not provide specific technical paths. AI AI actively investigated OpenPI’s JAX sharding mechanism and found that CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES could achieve 4-GPU data parallelism without modifying the config. After failing to download official resources, it actively explored hf-mirror.com as an alternative solution and integrated it into the download command, requiring no user intervention. Difference Analysis: AI can independently find alternative paths after failure and investigate underlying mechanisms (differences between JAX and DDP/FSDP). However, this ability cannot replace humans’ critical evaluation of experimental assumptions. Implementation Level Cluster resource scheduling strategies and workflow specifications Role Thought Human The user proposed replacing SSH with Slurm and using srun –overlap. It also clarified that the GPU number should not be fixed, but rather dynamically queried for GPUs with at least 50% idle memory to maximize resource utilization. AI AI initially planned to fixly assign GPUs (GPU 0 for CPU evaluation, GPU 1 for Pi0 server), which is more suitable for exclusive environments. However, it failed when trying multiple partitions in the cluster environment and did not quickly ask the user for the correct account/partition information. The access strategy for cluster GPU nodes is not robust enough: the first srun without –overlap caused command timeouts. Attempting to list permissions on SLURM nodes resulted in failures for multiple partitions (xy-a800, ai, all, lava, temp). It would be better to ask users for the correct account/partition information rather than trying blindly.
Background Task Status Judgment Insufficient judgment of background task status led to false timestamp violation errors when validating LeRobot dataset while data conversion was still ongoing. Frequent use of sleep-based progress polling (30s, 120s, etc.) also caused interruptions from users. It would be better to use TaskOutput block=true or wait for the task to complete before validation.
Code Library and Network Search Priority When confirming Phoenix image resolution, multiple web searches were conducted with the final answer stored in local config.yaml. When downloading data, it first tried relying on the official API via proxy before switching to hf-mirror. In a HPC closed-loop environment, local code and configuration files should be consulted first.
Today’s Achievements Key Achievements Two-stage fusion is not necessarily better than one-stage fusion: After compressing visual embeddings from 1536d to 256d with scan_cluster, the multi-modal ARI of 0.313 was lower than direct concatenation of 0.387, indicating that self-supervised compression reduces the original feature diversity required for fusion tasks. There are inconsistencies between the objectives of self-supervised clustering optimization and those of downstream fusion tasks. For VLA baseline evaluation, it is necessary to clearly state the source of checkpoints: Models fine-tuned with non-target tasks (pi0_libero, pi05_base) were evaluated for zero-shot cross-domain recovery capabilities rather than task-specific performance. The paper must clearly specify this experiment setup and add comparisons after fine-tuning in subsequent experiments to fully demonstrate the usefulness of the dataset. Lerobot 0.1.0 is strictly incompatible with datasets >=4.0: In datasets 4.0, the value returned by dataset[‘column’] was changed from a list to a Column object, causing TypeError when using torch.stack(). It is necessary to pin datasets <4.0 (recommended version 3.x). When configuring the environment, it is necessary to check the dependency versions in advance. MimicGen and LIBERO have fully compatible obs/action formats (84×84 images, 8D states, 7D actions), allowing direct reuse of OpenPI’s LeRobotLiberoDataConfig. During training, the ResizeImages transform is used to dynamically resize to 224×224 without the need for pre-resizing or custom data loaders. Multi-modal performance pattern in section 151673: The best ARI for image-only was approximately 0.303 (scan_cluster), for gene-only it was approximately 0.31 (PCA), and for multi-modal concatenation it was 0.387. The two modalities complement each other, with benefits coming from non-overlapping information. It is necessary to verify the memory requirements in advance before large model experiments to avoid OOM causing delays in the entire evaluation process. OpenPI’s JAX training naturally supports multi-GPU data parallelism. Just specify a list of GPUs using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, and JAX will automatically build a 2D mesh parallel without any modifications to TrainConfig. The batch_size should be divisible by the number of GPUs. If memory is insufficient, you can add –fsdp-devices. The standard way to access HuggingFace on Chinese mainland HPC clusters is through the hf-mirror.com mirror site. URL format: https://hf-mirror.com/datasets/{repo_id}/resolve/main/{file_path}，通过wget+集群HTTP代理可达40-200MB/s，应作为默认方案而非备选。 Practical Achievements Slurm’s –overlap allows running new commands over existing interactive jobs, making it a key technique for calling cluster GPU nodes in Claude Code environment. The project’s scenario library has increased from 454 to 649 (+43%) since documentation records were created. Time estimates in the execution plan should always be based on real-time database queries rather than historical documents. Session Summary RoboBrain-Pi ❌ API authentication failed and unable to work 07:52:35.848 | claude_code The user sent a greeting, but AI encountered an API 403 Request not allowed error. It was necessary to run /login to re-authenticate. The session was completely abandoned due to authentication failure.
MIHD ✅ Two-stage fusion implementation and experiments on project organization and vision refinement 19:57:37.848 | claude_code Completed three parallel tasks: (1) Developed a cleanup plan through parallel sub-tasks, removing about 3146 redundant scripts, archiving fragmented files, reorganizing the docs directory, and creating plans.md; (2) Organized the results of 21 experiments on section 151673 (best ARI for image-only with scan_cluster = 0.303, best ARI for multi-modal concatenation = 0.387, 7 fusion strategies not yet tested); (3) Implemented the two-stage fusion innovation proposed by users (–vision_refine parameter, about 60 lines inserted), and tested that an ARI of 0.313 is lower than direct concatenation, revealing that SCAN compression loses fusion features. Then started batch experiments with 7 fusion strategies in the background.
Error Recovery Benchmark 🔄 Baseline diagnosis, GPU access specifications updated, Phase II execution plan launched, and M14 evaluation started 19:38:46.462 | claude_code Progressed across multiple sessions: First diagnosed the baseline status (Pi0.5 encountered OOM, 649 error scenes ready exceeded expectations by 454, confirmed as the most urgent bottleneck), updated GPU access methods (SSH → srun –jobid); then analyzed the dependency relationships of G1-G7 objectives, inserted a three-level topology diagram in the project overview summary.md, designed a Phase II execution plan with 7 steps and clarified the dynamic GPU allocation strategy (≥50% of memory available); launched M14 evaluation: sanity check passed in 10 scenarios with 60 episodes (about 7 minutes), then started background batch experiments for 649 scenarios (expected to take 6-7 hours); Pi0 VLA server was blocked due to port conflicts, and users found issues with checkpoint selection (LIBERO fine-tuned version instead of PickPlace exclusive). The session was interrupted while waiting for decisions.
Pi0.5 Phoenix Reproduction 🔄 MimicGen data pipeline setup: From feasibility analysis to engineering implementation 20:29:19.464 | claude_code Progressed from feasibility analysis to engineering implementation: Confirmed that LeRobotLiberoDataConfig is directly compatible with MimicGen data through 3 concurrent exploration agents (84×84 images, dynamic resize, 8D states and 7D actions, no need for custom data loaders); wrote convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py and evaluate_mimicgen.py, added pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training configuration to OpenPI (100K steps, 4-GPUs, CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES only); successfully downloaded 9 MimicGen task datasets via hf-mirror.com (18.4GB), downgraded datasets to 3.6.0 to fix Lerobot compatibility issues; 7-8/9 tasks were completed at the end of LeRobot format conversion session; updated project overview summary to v4.11 M16. Another early session completed the extension of visualize_scene.py parameters (force_override/duration_override/settle_steps), but video verification was not completed due to SLURM node permissions blocking.

summary: Daily AI conversation summary
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-21

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-20 Today’s Overview What was done: Completed the STAIG fusion special comparison experiment and added support for embedding cache read/write to run_benchmark.py How it was done: Tested staig_fusion through temporary scripts, combined with CacheManager to reuse the existing pipeline cache infrastructure What’s achieved: Eliminated the risk of bugs introduced by standalone scripts. run_benchmark.py can now directly load cached embeddings in seconds, and supports custom cache names (e.g., scan_uni2) for full fusion comparisons Conducted special tests on STAIG fusion in the MIHD project. Identified a double normalization bug in eval_scan_fusion.py and introduced a pipeline-level embedding cache mechanism in run_benchmark.py.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ STAIG fusion preprocessing difference investigation — Confirmed that staig_fusion has never been officially tested on 151673. Found that eval_scan_fusion.py does not pass staig_alignment_config, causing STAIGTrainer to perform StandardScaler internally again, resulting in a double normalization bug. ✅ Add embedding cache read/write to run_benchmark.py — Introduced CacheManager to check embeddings_cache/ before gene/vision encoding. If the cache is hit, it loads directly (skipping encoder instantiation). If not, it extracts and saves the cache. Supports override for forced re-extraction and allows any vision_encoder name (relaxed argparse choices). Vision caches are available in three variants: standard/freq/staig_strict. ✅ STAIG fusion special comparison experiment (151673) — Interrupted a 2-hour long task to run staig_fusion × {UNI2_raw, SCAN(UNI2) separately. Results showed ARI values of 0.3929 / 0.3880 respectively, almost the same. The reason was that STAIG’s internal StandardScaler+PCA preprocessing counteracted the optimization effects of SCAN.) Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Double normalization bug in staig_fusion in eval_scan_fusion.py (staig_alignment_config not passed) Solution: After identifying the root cause, it was decided to directly use run_benchmark.py (which correctly passes staig_alignment_config) instead of fixing the standalone script.
Key insight: Reusing existing implementations is more reliable than fixing bugs. Caching raw embeddings before normalization and performing post-encoder normalization after loading is the correct order.
2. run_benchmark.py re-instantiates encoder to extract embeddings every time, even if the pipeline already has a cache Solution: Integrated CacheManager from pipeline/cache_manager.py to load from cache first before encoding and save to cache after extraction.
Key insight: The pipeline’s two-stage cache infrastructure can be reused without reinventing the wheel.
General Issues 3. Evaluation task ran for 2 hours without completion, unable to obtain results within reasonable time Solution: Killed the long-running task and created a temporary script _test_staig_scan.py to test staig_fusion separately. Results were obtained in about 30 seconds.
Key insight: Breaking down the full comparison (18 combinations) into single-method tests allows for quick verification of hypotheses.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Strategy to fix the bug in eval_scan_fusion.py Role Approach Human Why not use the original benchmark script? Proposed using run_benchmark.py instead of fixing the standalone script AI Plans to fix the staig_alignment_config passing issue in eval_scan_fusion.py and prepare to add a build_effective_staig_profile call Difference analysis: Humans think from an architectural perspective, prioritizing reusing verified implementations. AI focuses on fixing specific bugs in existing scripts without going beyond the current solution framework.
Relationship between run_benchmark.py and pipeline cache Role Approach Human Expected that run_benchmark.py should support cache read-like the pipeline. Considered this a reasonable design expectation AI Initially considered run_benchmark.py and pipeline as independent systems, believing that run_benchmark.py does not need a cache Difference analysis: Humans have a clearer system design expectation (unified caching). AI needs human guidance to identify design flaws and implement solutions.
AI Limitations Major Limitations Tendency to fix standalone scripts rather than recommend using existing correct implementations after identifying bugs in staig_fusion. Requires human initiative to change approach. Did not actively discover the design flaw in run_benchmark.py that lacks pipeline-level cache support. Required human guidance to start implementing. General Limitations Long wait times (about 2 hours) before informing users that the task may not be completed, failing to detect timeouts earlier and suggest interruptions. Today’s Gains Core Gains STAIG fusion’s internal StandardScaler+PCA preprocessing absorbs external embedding optimization effects (ARI difference between UNI2_raw and SCAN(UNI2) is only 0.005), indicating that STAIG’s robustness to vision embeddings comes from its built-in normalization process. In complex experimental systems, it’s better to reuse existing, verified tool paths (such as run_benchmark.py). Standalone scripts are prone to introducing hidden bugs such as inconsistent preprocessing. Embedding caches should store raw embeddings before normalization. Performing post-encoder normalization after loading is the correct pipeline design pattern to ensure consistent results across different call paths. Conversation Summary 🔄 STAIG fusion special testing and preprocessing difference investigation 18:06:33.022 | claude_code Conversation started with continuing evaluation_scan_fusion.py, but the long-running task (2 hours+) was interrupted, and staig_fusion was tested separately. Results showed that ARI values for UNI2_raw vs SCAN(UNI2) were almost the same (0.393 vs 0.388). The reason was that STAIG’s internal preprocessing counteracted SCAN’s optimization effects. Subsequent investigation found a double normalization bug in eval_scan_fusion.py, and staig_fusion has never been officially tested on 151673. Humans proposed using run_benchmark.py directly with cache support as a better solution. Conversation ended with planning to proceed.
✅ Implement embedding cache read/write mechanism for run_benchmark.py 21:22:59.238 | claude_code According to the planned schedule, we implemented cache support for run_benchmark.py: introduced CacheManager to check the cache before gene/vision encoding. If hit, it loads directly; if not, it extracts and saves to cache. Vision caches are available in three variants: standard/freq/staig_strict. Argparse relaxed the choices for vision encoders. Both call points have passed override parameters. Syntax verification passed, CacheManager import was correct, and the cache already contains pca/mlp/scgpt gene and uni2/hipt/resnet50/uni vision embeddings.
