title: 错误日志 2026-02-20

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-19 Today’s Overview What was done: The system carried out three major tasks of the MIHD project: establishing a technical documentation system (RM-IDEAL bilingual instructions, visual encoder guidelines, literature review on pathological PFM, update of UNI/UNI2 evaluation analysis), in-depth research on H&E Image-Only clustering methods (content normalization baseline, failure cause analysis of the Foundation Model, and update of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md’s major goal 7), and complete implementation and comparative validation of three image-only self-supervised clustering enhancement schemes (STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN). How it was done: Through extensive web searches of academic literature (spEMO, HEST-1k, STAIG, etc.) and in-depth exploration of code libraries, five new model files were created and infrastructure files were modified. Comparative experiments with multiple methods were conducted on GPUs. Meanwhile, .npz cache files were used to regenerate UNI2 visualizations in batches, and all research conclusions were organized and written into ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md. What’s achieved: The SCAN method improved the image-only ARI from a baseline of 0.251 to 0.303 (+20.6%). Embedding with gene features could further improve it by +0.065 ARI. The system research filled the gap in literature where there was almost no evaluation on vision-only spatial domain recognition, establishing a reference benchmark for the project and a complete multi-stage enhancement roadmap. In the MIHD project, the system completed literature research on H&E Image-Only clustering methods (establishing a baseline of ARI 0.11–0.16 and analyzing five major causes of Foundation Model failure), established four core technical documents, and implemented and validated three self-supervised clustering enhancement schemes (STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN). SCAN improved the image-only ARI from 0.251 to 0.303 (+20.6%).
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Establishment of MIHD technical documentation system — Created four core technical documents: RM-IDEAL bilingual structure documents (WWL graph kernel, Wasserstein optimal transmission, complementary relationships with ARI/NMI), guidelines for using visual encoders (12-chapter end-to-end pipeline, including detailed comparisons between UNI2/UNI/HIPT/ResNet50), literature review on pathological PFM (patch extraction strategies and encoder selection methods from spEMO/HEST-1k/STAIG, etc.), evaluation analysis of UNI/UNI2 original papers (comprehensive documents with 34 clinical tasks + 8 benchmarks). ✅ Systematic research on H&E Image-Only clustering methods — Collected a comprehensive overview of MILWRM, F-SEG, Deep Contrastive Clustering and other methods. Specifically verified the DLPFC ARI values for image-only from ablation experiments (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11, the only available data currently). In-depth research on the application of BYOL/STEGO/SCAN in pathology (especially the precedent of using BYOL to train image encoders in STAIG), investigated cross-domain analogies between FGVC/medical images/remote sensing/material science, and sorted out the framework for solving the four-level domain gap in the CV community. ✅ Analysis of failure causes in spatial domain recognition of the Foundation Model — Systematically analyzed failure mechanisms from five dimensions: training data domain mismatch (mainly cancer tissues), pre-training task mismatch (patch classification/reconstruction vs inter-layer gradient recognition), extremely small morphological differences between brain tissue layers, feature redundancy, and lack of spatial context. Combined with the phenomenon of brown repeated patches in UNI2, specific cases were provided for support. ✅ Implementation of image-only clustering enhancement schemes (STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN) — Completed five-stage implementation: modified infrastructure (5 files including run_benchmark.py, config.yaml), created four model files for STEGOHead/BYOLAdapter/SpatialGATRefiner/SCANHead (all verified by AST syntax), created eval_image_only.py to compare and test in section 151673, updated models/init.py for lazy loading and configuration integration. SCAN achieved the best result with ARI=0.303 (baseline 0.251, +20.6%). 🔄 Joint evaluation of SCAN embedding and multi-modal fusion — Written eval_scan_fusion.py to compare all fusion methods of the optimized 256-dimensional visual embedding of SCAN with PCA gene features. Mean fusion ARI +0.065, llava_mlp fusion +0.018, proving complementarity. The bug in the coords dimension has been partially fixed, and the script is still being debugged. 🔄 Update of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md’s major goal 7 and planning for Image Encoder enhancement schemes — Wrote all the research results (literature review, failure cause analysis, six schemes, detailed explanation of BYOL, five-stage implementation roadmap, risk and verification plan) into ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md (expanding from ~400 lines to 907 lines). Parallelly analyzed the architectures of ImageEncoder.py/spatial_utils.py/datasets.py through three agents and generated an implementation plan. The subsequent implementation has not yet started. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Added H&E panel to UNI2 visualization and updated in batches — Modified scripts/run_benchmark.py to use a 1×3 layout for the visualization (H&E + GT + prediction). Fixed the issue of missing tissue_lowres_image.png in section 151510 (created hires→lowres symlinks). Used .npz cache files to regenerate visualizations for all 11 sections in batches, eliminating the need for re-inference. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. STEGO’s training loss is NaN throughout, causing the model not to converge Solution: Two-step fix: ① Perform L2 normalization on input image_emb to prevent excessive amplitude; ② Replace InfoNCE with a numerically stable version (subtract the maximum value first and then perform logsumexp), increase temperature to 0.1.
Key insight: The exponentiation operation after dividing a 3639×3639 dense similarity matrix by temperature=0.07 overflows at float32 precision. Log-sum-exp is a complete solution, and this form must be used for large-scale comparative loss calculations.
2. MILWRM was incorrectly classified as an image-only method, and when AI summarized the results for the first time, multi-modal methods were mixed into the image-only results, resulting in significant revisions to the first version of the research conclusions. Solution: Confirmed through reading the full text of PMC that MILWRM is actually based on gene expression using WebFetch. It was removed from the list of image-only methods. Considering the user’s two clear boundary constraints, re-focused on pure image scenarios and specifically mined image-only data points from ablation experiments.
Key insight: Literature summaries often contain misleading information. It is necessary to read the full method section to confirm the input modality. Most multi-modal methods’ ablation experiments never test image-only methods, so it is necessary to specifically retrieve them from a few articles such as SpaConTDS.
General Problems 3. Paid academic journals (Nature Medicine, Elsevier, etc.) returned 303/403 papers, and numerical values embedded in paper figures could not be extracted from web text. Solution: Switch to using PMC full-text mirrors, arXiv HTML versions, HuggingFace model cards, and GitHub README as alternative sources. When it is impossible to obtain numerical values embedded in figures, use textual qualitative conclusions instead and clearly label the data source and confidence level.
Key insight: PMC and arXiv HTML are effective ways to access paid journals. The key model performance values are usually fully listed in GitHub README, so they should be retrieved first. When precise values cannot be obtained, qualitative conclusions with source labels are better than speculation.
In eval_scan_fusion.py, the spatial coordinate dimension was abnormal (became (1,2)), and multiple fusion methods (basic_contrastive/qformer/staig_fusion) reported errors. Solution: Abandon calling load_spatial_coordinates() (barcode matching failed), and directly read coordinates from adata.obsm[‘spatial’]. Also fixed the issue of incorrect unpacking of return values in load_dlpfc_data (the function returned a single value instead of a tuple).
Key insight: Tool functions that rely on precise barcode matching are prone to failure when used across data sources. Directly accessing AnnData’s native fields is more reliable, and before calling project internal functions, the function signature should be verified in real time.
5. AI repeatedly triggered ExitPlanMode in document tasks (rejected twice), and model verification was performed using CPU by default, resulting in unnecessary interaction friction and efficiency loss. Solution: After explicit user instructions, pure document writing tasks directly called the Write tool. All model verifications were changed to run on GPUs, and three methods were executed in parallel in the background, significantly reducing the total time.
Key insight: In a HPC environment, GPU is the default computing device. Testing with CPU will掩盖 real performance problems. Pure document tasks do not require the “plan→exit plan” process.
6. When regenerating visualizations, it was necessary to avoid re-running UNI2 inference (which took hours), and section 151510 lacked tissue_lowres_image.png. Solution: It was found that pred_labels and gt_labels were already saved in .npz cache files. After directly loading them, the modified visualization function was called. All 11 sections were successful. Section 151510 was fixed by creating hires→lowres symlinks.
Key insight: MIHD’s caching design (embedding + labels saved together) completely decoupled visualization updates from inference. sc.read_visium defaulted to searching for lowres images, and using soft links for hires was the minimum change solution.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level The essential equivalence between supervised classification and unsupervised clustering Role Thought Human Intuitively, users pointed out that “Is UNI’s CRC-100K 9-class classification not just clustering?” This broke the paper’s classification framework from the essence of the task, actively establishing an analogy relationship between UNI evaluation and MIHD spatial domain recognition. AI AI described in detail according to the paper’s classification framework (ROI classification/clustering/segmentation/searching), without actively discovering the essential connection between the two. After being prompted, it explained the key differences between supervised (linear probe) and unsupervised (KMeans) methods, pointing out that this was a literature gap. Difference analysis: Humans broke through the paper’s framework from the essence of the task; AI remained within the literature description system. This insight was entirely proposed by humans, being the most important cognitive difference in this project, revealing that high accuracy of UNI cannot be directly inferred from unsupervised clustering effects.
Obtaining quantitative image-only data from ablation studies Role Thought Human The user actively asked “Are there any image-only parts in these articles’ ablation studies?” This strategy directly obtained few precise numbers such as SpaConTDS ARI=0.16. AI AI initially searched for independent papers specifically dealing with image-only, which was the correct direction but overlooked an important source of image-only baseline in multi-modal method ablation experiments. Difference analysis: Humans have a stronger intuition about paper structure (ablation experiments often include modality comparison), and can accurately target specific information; AI’s retrieval strategy is more macroscopic and requires user guidance to focus on the most effective information sources.
Systematic experimental design for joint evaluation of SCAN embedding and full fusion Role Thought Human After comparing three methods, the user actively proposed to jointly evaluate the optimized SCAN embedding with all fusion methods (including staig_fusion), designing a systematic ablation experiment that “first independently evaluated the quality of visual embeddings and then explored their complementarity with gene features.” AI AI was preparing to conclude after completing the comparison of three methods. It did not actively propose an extended experiment; its experimental design lacked foresight compared to the user’s. Difference analysis: The user had a clear experimental design mindset and could actively identify the two-stage logic of independent evaluation and fusion complementarity verification; AI tended to stop after completing the current goal, lacking the ability to extend research vision.
Precise definition of research boundaries and dominated by visualization needs Role Thought Human The user explicitly interrupted AI twice ( “I just need image-only methods/parts”) and actively proposed adding H&E original images as morphological references in visualizations, so that the biological meaning of brown repeated patches could be intuitively explained. AI When summarizing for the first time, AI habitually provided a multi-modal panorama view; in visualization implementation, it only outputted dual panels of GT+prediction, without actively suggesting adding original images. Difference analysis: Human researchers had clear prior knowledge about research boundaries and analysis objectives; AI tended to provide a complete background while ignoring constraints. The key details (morphological comparison) were proposed by humans.
Domain knowledge triggered by the association between BYOL and STAIG Role Thought Human The user actively mentioned “I remember there was a method using BYOL”, leading AI to the key precedent of using BYOL for unsupervised domain adaptation on target datasets in STAIG. AI When sorting out six unsupervised schemes, AI did not actively associate BYOL with STAIG’s known connection and listed BYOL as one of the general options, without highlighting its existing practice in the ST field. Difference analysis: The user’s domain prior knowledge triggered more precise information retrieval; there was an association in AI’s knowledge base but it was not actively activated. External clues were needed to trigger this.
AI Limitations Important Limitations Inadequate accuracy of literature classification and knowledge correlation: Mistakenly classified MILWRM as an image-only method (actually based on gene expression), requiring full-text reading with WebFetch for self-correction; did not actively establish a类比关系 between UNI evaluation tasks and MIHD spatial domain recognition when describing UNI evaluation tasks; did not actively activate the known connection between BYOL→STAIG when organizing unsupervised schemes. All three require user intervention to trigger or correct. Insufficient predictability of technical implementation: The stability problem of STEGO values (3639×3639 dense matrix at float32 precision boundary) was not预见 in the initial design stage; eval_scan_fusion.py had multiple API usage errors (function signature/return value unpacking), tending to rely on memory rather than real-time source code verification. Insufficient perception of task constraints and misjudgment of processes: Ignored the user’s core constraint (image-only) when summarizing for the first time, resulting in significant revisions to the first version; repeatedly triggered ExitPlanMode in document tasks (rejected twice); used CPU by default for model verification in a HPC environment. All these caused unnecessary interaction friction and efficiency loss. General Limitations Unable to access full text of paid journals, as well as unable to extract specific numerical values embedded in figures/charts in papers (F-SEG F1 curve, SpaConTDS DLPFC ARI scatter plot, etc.), resulting in a lack of quantitative data. Only qualitative conclusions or indirect sources could be relied on. Today’s Gains Core Gains- The ARI for pure Image-Only in DLPFC fine-grained classification tasks is only 0.11–0.16 (multi-modal reaches 0.45–0.64). This indicates that the morphological differences between brain tissue layers are minimal, and the training domain does not match the foundation model. This is a significant research gap, as multi-modal methods almost never test image-only scenarios independently (gene expression is considered core). There are five main reasons why the foundation model fails in spatial domain recognition: ① Training with cancer tissues (domain gap); ② The pre-training tasks (patch classification/reconstruction) do not match the inter-layer gradient recognition; ③ The morphological differences between brain layers are very small; ④ Image features and gene expression are highly redundant; ⑤ Single patches lack spatial context for independent encoding. UNI2’s brown repeated patch phenomenon is a direct manifestation of reasons ① and ③. SCAN achieves the best ARI in image-only spatial transcriptomics scenarios (0.303 vs baseline 0.251, +20.6%). Its core advantage lies in offline feature k-NN mining, which decouples embedding learning from clustering, avoiding numerical instability in STEGO. Its 256-dimensional optimized embedding fused with gene features has a high complementary effect (mean fusion +0.065, llava_mlp +0.018 ARI). STAIG uses BYOL for unsupervised domain adaptation on target datasets of H&E patches (discarding the projector/predictor after training, retaining encoder features). This is a direct precedent to introducing unsupervised domain adaptation into spatial transcriptomics. BYOL does not require negative samples, making it suitable for small batch ST scenarios (thousands of patches per section), and it is robust against H&E staining variations. When calculating InfoNCE comparative loss for large-scale dense similarity matrices (n>3000), it is necessary to use a numerically stable log-sum-exp function (minus the row maximum). Otherwise, exponential operations with temperature=0.07 under float32 precision will cause overflow and lead to NaN values. This is a key engineering constraint in implementing large-scale comparative learning using HPC. The CV community has developed a four-level consensus framework for dealing with “domain gap + fine-grained tasks + unlabeled” scenarios: Level 1 uses pre-trained features for clustering; Level 2 involves refining STEGO/SCAN features; Level 3 requires re-training SSL within the domain (BYOL/MAE); Level 4 involves using dedicated foundation models. The appropriate level should be chosen based on computational resources constraints. GPFM/CHIEF are the best PFM for spatial domain recognition ARI; UNI2 is the best for spot retrieval; 224×224 is the industry-standard patch size. In the original UNI paper, 34 supervised evaluation tasks (linear probe) and MIHD unsupervised clustering (KMeans) have similar tasks but different evaluation methods. The high accuracy of UNI cannot directly infer the performance of unsupervised clustering. HEST-1k shows that the size of PFM is logarithmically related to spatial gene expression prediction performance (R=0.81). Pathology-specific PFM is about 8.2% higher than ResNet50 in Pearson r. The literature review on spEMO reveals that GPFM/CHIEF are the best for spatial domain recognition clustering ARI; UNI2 has the best correlation in spot retrieval rankings; 224×224 is the industry-standard patch size, consistent with MIHD. This provides solid literature support for selecting the encoder for MIHD. Conversation Summary ✅ Establishment of technical documentation system and visualization extension of UNI2 (RM-IDEAL / Visual Encoder Guide / Literature Review / UNI2 Evaluation Analysis) 2026-02-19 | claude_code The morning session focused on building MIHD technical documentation: creating RM-IDEAL bilingual structure documents, a 12-chapter visual encoder usage guide (including comparisons with UNI2/UNI/HIPT/ResNet50), a literature review of pathology PFM (spEMO/HEST-1k/STAIG, etc.), and a comprehensive evaluation of UNI/UNI2 original papers (34 tasks + 8 benchmarks). During this time, we verified the performance data of various methods through extensive online literature searches. After users identified abnormalities in brown repeated patches, we extended the visualization of UNI2 clustering from a two-panel layout to a three-panel layout that included H&E original images. Using cached .npz files, we batch-recreated all 11 sections’ visualizations (all successful after fixing the 151510 symlink issue).
✅ In-depth research on image-only clustering methods and update of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md project goal 7 2026-02-19 | claude_code In the afternoon, we conducted a systematic study on pure image-only spatial domain clustering methods. We verified the ARI quantification baseline for image-only DLPFC through multiple online searches (SpaConTDS=0.16, stLearn=0.11). After two corrections of boundary constraints, users focused specifically on image-only scenarios. We analyzed the root causes of foundation model failures from five dimensions, investigated cross-domain analogies such as FGVC/medical imaging/remote sensing, and deeply analyzed BYOL’s domain adaptation application in STAIG (its natural advantage for small batch ST scenarios without negative sample design). We wrote all the research results (about 500 lines) into ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md project goal 7. The file grew from about 400 lines to 907 lines.
🔄 Three solutions for enhancing image-only clustering: STEGO/BYOL+GAT/SCAN, and joint evaluation of SCAN Fusion 2026-02-19 | claude_code In the afternoon, we completed five-stage implementation: modifying infrastructure files; creating four new model files (STEGOHead/BYOLAdapter/SpatialGATRefiner/SCANHead); comparing four methods on GPU with 151673 sections (SCAN ARI=0.303 was the best; all methods ran normally after fixing STEGO NaN loss issues); completing integration configuration. Then, we started writing eval_scan_fusion.py to evaluate SCAN embedding and multi-modal fusion jointly (mean fusion +0.065 ARI, verifying complementarity), fixing the coords dimension bug. We also analyzed the image encoder enhancement architecture through three parallel agents and generated an implementation plan, which will be implemented later.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-17

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W07 (2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15) This week, we focused on three areas: robot simulation, bioinformatics, and toolchain development. In the error_recovery_benchmark project, deep bottlenecks were encountered during force injection debugging. A 30N force applied to the OSC controller had no noticeable effect, indicating a fundamental issue with the controller’s countermeasure mechanism. For MIHD, the Chinese documentation for the enhancement plan was completed. ccusage implemented support for GLM multi-model billing and passed type/format checks. Robobrain_pi confirmed that the training process is ready. This week, AI showed obvious pattern-fitting defects in repeated errors (SSH lacking the cd command) and environmental assumptions (proxy, pnpm). Human intervention provided more efficient debugging ideas at critical decision points (extreme force testing, local GPU detection, reuse of existing pricing files).
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15 Active Days 4 / 7 Total Conversations 10 Projects Involved 6 Completed Tasks 10 Ongoing Tasks 2 Total Tokens 54,947,656 Total Cost $23.28 Claude Code Tokens 40,980,880 Claude Code Costs $19.02 Codex Tokens 13,966,776 Codex Costs $4.26 Average Daily Cost $5.82 Project Progress error_recovery_benchmark (2 active days) — 🔄 active Completed:
Verified the current state of approximately 6200 lines of code in the framework, identified high-priority bottlenecks (collision geometry name mapping, target object dynamic recognition) Created AGENTS.md contributor guide (372 words, covering structure/construction/testing/submission guidelines) Implemented –force_override command-line parameter and enhanced force injection mechanism (force range extended to [15,45]N, clip set to 60N, duration automatically cleared after completion) Generated a 30N force injection test video (451KB, 193 frames), establishing a visual force debugging toolchain Bottlenecks:
⚠️ No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N force: The OSC controller (kp=150) can cancel out the impulse within duration_steps=1 (about 20ms). Whether the force injection mechanism works at all remains unverified ⚠️ Complete E2E verification requires GPU node (an53), currently mitigated through hierarchical testing (CPU unit/smoke tests) MIHD (2 active days) — ✅ completed Completed:
Translated ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md into a structured Chinese version (6 major goals: normalization, UNI2+scGPT, Q-Former/LLaVA, niche query, batch correction, full configuration) Written the translated content in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md, preserving key details like file names, CLI parameters, and configuration fields ccusage (GLM billing support) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Implemented loadLocalPricing() in _pricing-fetcher.ts to load LiteLLM format pricing from CCUSAGE_PRICING_FILE environment variable or default path ~/.ccusage/ccusage.json, and merged it into the offline cache Fixed three issues: misuse of Result.try() API, filtering of LiteLLM metadata keys, and global variables in ESLint process All format/typechecks and targeted tests passed Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Global testing showed failures unrelated to this change for apps/amp. This needs confirmation before merging the PR robobrain_pi (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Confirmed datasets/demo_v2.hdf5 exists with 50 trajectories and 7-dimensional actions Verified that the complete SAC framework is in place (sac_agent.py, trainer.py, train_sac.py) Confirmed availability of 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Recommended use of GPU 1-3 for training. Provided training command instructions Reconstructed the full project timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 from .ccusage/summaries/ gadget/research documentation (1 active day) — ⏸️ paused Completed:
Initiated updates to research/CLAUDE.md. Users completed preliminary reading of the research directory structure Bottlenecks:
⚠️ Conversations were interrupted after users issued analysis requests. AI has not actually performed or written any analysis yet Key Tasks 🚫 Root cause of ineffective force injection with 30N (2026-02-14) — No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N to the Sawyer arm. Users proposed verifying the force injection mechanism using infinite force. Conversations were interrupted before implementation 🔄 ccusage GLM model billing support (2026-02-12) — Added logic for loading and merging local LiteLLM format pricing files in _pricing-fetcher.ts. Targeted tests passed, but global testing showed unrelated failures that need confirmation ✅ Prepare robobrain_pi SAC Pick-and-Place training environment (2026-02-10) — Confirmed availability of datasets, SAC framework, and 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Provided training command instructions ✅ Implement –force_override parameter and enhanced force injection (2026-02-14) — Added command-line parameters, expanded force range to [15,45]N, implemented automatic clearing after duration, fixed bug in ImpulseInjector nested configuration path ✅ Check progress of error_recovery_benchmark plan (2026-02-10) — Confirmed high-priority bottlenecks: collision geometry name mapping (collision.py/env_wrapper.py) and target object dynamic recognition. Complete E2E verification requires GPU node 🔄 Update gadget research/CLAUDE.md documentation (2026-02-10) — Users requested in-depth reading of the research directory before updating the design document. Conversations were interrupted. AI has not actually performed analysis yet ✅ Generate and verify 30N force injection visualization video (2026-02-14) — Ran 2_visualize_scene.py –force_override 30 to generate impulse_demo_0_step72.mp4 (451KB, 193 frames). No visible disturbance was observed visually ✅ Restore robobrain_pi project history from ccusage summary (2026-02-10) — Read .ccusage/summaries/ for 10 Markdown files to reconstruct the full timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09 Issues and Solutions 1. Complete E2E verification of error_recovery_benchmark is hindered by GPU node dependencies (EGL/robosuite runtime constraints) [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-10) Solution: Define a clear testing strategy: perform CPU unit tests and smoke tests first. Delay complete E2E verification until the GPU node (an53) is available to avoid blocking development
2. @praha/byethrow’s Result.try() API was misused: AI used it directly as an result, while it actually returns a reusable parser function [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: Store the return value of Result.try({…}) as a function, and explicitly call this function to obtain Result<T, E>
3. The LiteLLM pricing JSON contained metadata keys like sample_spec, which were treated as empty pricing entries after parsing with valibot schema, leading to test assertion failures [ccusage] (2026-02-12) Solution: After schema parsing, additionally check that input_cost_per_token or output_cost_per_token is not null. Filter out metadata entries without pricing data
4. Multiple attempts to SSH to the GPU node were rejected by pam_slurm_adopt (active SLURM job required), resulting in significant wasted time [error_recovery_benchmark] (2026-02-14) Solution: Users suggested checking whether the current node has a GPU using nvidia-smi -L. It was found that there were already 5 A800 GPUs on the current node, so no SSH transfer was required
Learnings Architecture Using external summary tools (ccusage) to export historical sessions is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context limitations: externalizing session summaries into files allows AI to quickly restore project context in new sessions, avoiding repeated background description Testing strategies for large robot RL projects should be clearly hierarchical: CPU unit tests, CPU smoke tests, GPU E2E testing. Avoid blocking the entire test process due to GPU unavailability Domain Knowledge The OSC controller (kp=150, Λ≈5-10kg) has strong resistance to external forces. An impulse with duration_steps=1 (about 20ms) is canceled out within the controller’s response cycle, effectively equivalent to no force application. To make disturbances visible, continuous force application must be maintained for several control cycles or use forces far beyond what the controller can handle MIHD enhancement plan is implemented in a strict dependency order: configuration basics (Idea 6 Phase 1) → normalization → UNI2+scGPT experiments → Q-Former/LLaVA → niche query → batch correction → full configuration integration. This order is designed to avoid cumulative refactoring costs Tools @praha/byethrow’s Result.try({try, catch}) returns a factory function (which must be called explicitly), rather than directly executing and returning a result. This differs from common Rust/fp-ts implementations of the Result monad. Special attention is needed In HPC clusters, use nvidia-smi -L to check whether the current node has a GPU before executing GPU tasks. If you need to SSH to remote nodes, you must explicitly change directory to the project folder and activate the conda environment in the remote command (SSH does not inherit the current shell working directory) ccusage’s offline pricing is pre-filtered using the isClaudeModel function in _macro.ts during construction. Extending multi-model support requires simultaneous modifications to both macro and _pricing-fetcher.ts ESLint rules for this project require all Node.js built-in objects to be explicitly imported via the node: protocol (e.g., import process from ’node:process’). Global objects cannot be used directly Debugging When debugging force injection or other physical mechanisms, it is important to first verify whether the mechanism itself works using extreme parameters (such as infinite force, 1000 consecutive steps), and then gradually adjust the parameters to a reasonable range. This should not be done starting from reasonable parameters AI Usage Notes Effective Solutions:
✓ Reconstructing cross-session project history by reading Markdown files in the ccusage summaries/ directory is an effective engineering approach for solving AI context window limitations ✓ Static code analysis (reading Makefile, test code, existing documentation) can effectively generate contributor guidelines when there is no git history ✓ Binary extreme testing ideas (first verifying the mechanism with infinite force, then adjusting parameters to a reasonable range) are more efficient than systematic code analysis when debugging physical simulations Limitations:
✗ Repeating the same error (SSH lacking the cd command) 15+ times within a single session. There is no ability to correct behavior based on repeated failures ✗ There are incorrect assumptions about library API behavior (Result.try() factory pattern). These can only be corrected after testing fails ✗ Failed to identify existing data assets (local ccusage.json pricing files). The default approach is to modify code logic rather than reuse existing files ✗ Mechanically following historical command templates (proxy settings) without verifying their applicability. Human intervention is needed to indicate that the task does not require network access Next Week’s Plans Next week will focus on three areas: (1) Debugging the root cause of force injection in error_recovery_benchmark — first verify whether the xfrc_applied mechanism works at all using extreme forces (infinite or 1000 consecutive steps), then investigate body mounting points and force direction issues. Ultimately, solve the collision geometry name mapping, which is a key E2E bottleneck; (2) ccusage GLM billing support — confirm that unrelated tests for apps/amp do not affect merging progress and move forward with the PR; (3) Update gadget/research CLAUDE.md — complete the interrupted documentation analysis and writing tasks. Robobrain.pi SAC training can be started in parallel when the GPU node is available, and we will observe the convergence curve.

title: 每周总结 2026-W07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-14 Today’s Overview What was done: Implemented force coverage parameters, extended force range, and zeroing logic for the robotic arm visualization script. A test video with a 30N force injection was successfully generated. How it was done: By modifying three files (visualization script, benchmark configuration, Impulse Injector) and running directly on the current GPU node (A800×5), unnecessary SSH transfers were avoided. What it means: A complete toolchain for visual force debugging was established. However, it was found that a 30N force is insufficient to produce visible displacement on the OSC controller’s Sawyer arm. This suggests there may be deeper issues with the force injection mechanism. An enhanced force injection solution was implemented for error recovery in the robotic arm. However, a 30N force still cannot produce visible disturbances in the video. The issue remains unresolved.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ❌ Root cause of ineffective 30N force injection debugging — Users requested testing with infinite forces to verify whether the force injection mechanism works at all. The session was interrupted before implementation. ✅ Implement –force_override parameter and enhanced force injection — Added the –force_override command-line parameter to 2_visualize_scene.py, overriding the force magnitude before Phase 2 injection. In Phase 3, xfrc_applied was automatically cleared after duration_steps. The configs/benchmark_v4.yaml was updated with force_norm_range increased from [3.0,15.0] to [15.0,45.0], and force_clip increased from 30.0 to 60.0. A bug in ImpulseInjector’s nested configuration path was fixed. ✅ Generate and verify a visualization video of 30N force injection — Ran 2_visualize_scene.py with –force_override set to 30 on the current A800 node, generating impulse_demo_0_step72.mp4 (451KB, 193 frames). However, visual inspection revealed no visible disturbances in the robotic arm. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Update GPU node usage guidelines in CLAUDE.md — Documented the need to use cd to the project directory and activate the conda environment when connecting to a GPU node. Added best practices for using nvidia-smi to check if there is already a GPU on the current node. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. No visible displacement in the video after applying 30N force to the Sawyer arm Solution: Not resolved yet. Users requested testing with infinite forces to diagnose whether the force injection mechanism doesn’t work at all.
Key insights: Possible reasons include: (1) duration_steps is set to 1, meaning the force is cleared after 20ms, allowing the OSC controller to counteract it; (2) incorrect application of xfrc_applied; (3) the direction of the force being orthogonal to the OSC axis, resulting in rapid decay.
2. Multiple attempts to connect to GPU nodes failed due to pam_slurm_adopt rejecting them (active SLURM job required), wasting a lot of time Solution: Users suggested checking if there is already a GPU on the current node using nvidia-smi -L. It was found that there were already 5 A800s available, so no SSH was needed.
Key insights: In a HPC cluster environment, it’s possible to be on the GPU node through a bastion host. Instead of trying to connect directly, it’s better to check for local GPUs first.
General Issues 3. AI repeatedly missed the cd command in SSH commands, resulting in 15+ occurrences of the same error (file path not found) Solution: After multiple corrections from users, AI understood that connecting to a remote node is equivalent to starting a new session. It needed to explicitly use cd to the project directory and activate the conda environment.
Key insights: SSH doesn’t inherit the working directory of the current shell. This is a systematic issue that needs to be recorded in CLAUDE.md.
4. AI added unnecessary proxy settings in visualization commands (source setproxy.sh) Solution: Users pointed out that the visualization script only operates on local files (NPZ, HDF5, MuJoCo rendering), so there’s no need for a network connection. Proxy is not necessary.
Key insights: Proxy is only useful for network operations like pip install/conda or accessing external networks. Local computing tasks don’t require it at all.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Thoughts after the 30N force failed Role Thought Human Propose testing with infinite forces to quickly verify whether the force injection mechanism works. (Binary troubleshooting) AI Prefers detailed mechanical analysis and code review. (Plans to modify parameters like duration_steps.) Difference analysis: Humans choose the fastest way to test fundamental assumptions. AI prefers systematic analysis but takes longer. For debugging, human thinking is more efficient.
How to get GPU access Role Thought Human Suggested that AI use nvidia-smi to check if there’s already a GPU on the current node, avoiding unnecessary SSH transfers. AI Got stuck in the mindset of “must connect to GPU nodes”. Tried various SLURM partitions but failed. Difference analysis: Humans make the simplest decisions based on the current situation. AI follows a preset workflow, ignoring the basic step of checking the status.
Do we need proxy settings? Role Thought Human Clearly stated that visualization is a local operation and there’s no reason to set proxy in the source script. Questioned why AI added this step. AI Blindly followed the proxy command template seen before in CLAUDE.md, without considering whether network access was actually needed for the task. Difference analysis: Humans derive tool usage from task requirements. AI mechanically follows a template. This shows that AI lacks understanding of the “why” behind commands.
AI Limitations Important limitations The same error (missing cd command in SSH) was repeated 15+ times, each time with exactly the same failure. This indicates that AI doesn’t have the ability to learn from repeated failures and correct its behavior within a single session. After trying all available partitions but failing, AI didn’t consider the simplest possibility of “there being already a GPU on the current node”. It needed human guidance. General limitations When applying templates, AI doesn’t verify their applicability (proxy settings are used for network operations but mechanically applied to local computing tasks). This shows a lack of semantic understanding of tool usage. Today’s Takeaways Key takeaways The OSC controller has strong resistance to external forces (kp=150, Λ≈5-10kg). A force with duration_steps=1 (about 20ms) is cleared before the controller can react, effectively meaning almost no force is applied. To make disturbances visible, continuous force application over several control cycles or using a force far beyond what the controller can handle is required. When using Claude Code in a HPC cluster, it’s necessary to use nvidia-smi -L to check if there’s already a GPU on the current node before starting any GPU tasks. If there’s already a GPU, no SSH is needed. If SSH is required, the user must explicitly use cd to the project directory and activate the conda environment in the remote command. When debugging force injection mechanisms, it’s better to first test with extreme parameters (such as infinite forces or 1000 consecutive steps) to verify that the mechanism works. Then, adjust the parameters step by step to a reasonable range, rather than starting from reasonable parameters and increasing them. Session Summary 🔄 Implemented enhanced force injection to make disturbances visible in videos 01:35:54.517 | claude_code According to the pre-planned plan, three files were modified: added the –force_override parameter to the visualization script, updated the benchmark configuration to increase the force range from 3-15N to 15-45N, and fixed a bug in ImpulseInjector’s nested configuration reading. However, a lot of time was wasted on GPU access (AI repeatedly missed the cd command in SSH, tried various SLURM partitions but failed). Finally, with user guidance, it was determined that there were already 5 A800s available, and the video was generated directly. After verification, it was found that a 30N force is insufficient to produce visible displacement on the Sawyer arm’s OSC controller. The session was interrupted while testing infinite forces.

title: 缺陷记录表 2026-02-14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-13 Today’s Overview What was done: Translated the English version of the MIHD project’s ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md document into a Chinese version. How it was done: Used the exec_command tool in Codex to read the original document content, and then wrote the new Chinese translation file using heredoc. What this means: Provides users with a readable MIHD enhancement plan document in Chinese, making it easier for the team to read English technical documents. Translated the MIHD project’s enhancement plan document into Chinese and saved it in a new file
Today’s Tasks Implementations and Fixes ✅ Translated ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md into Chinese — Read content from /hpc/group/yizhanglab/zt81/MIHD/docs/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md (including normalization, Q-Former, QueST integration, configuration refactoring, etc., 6 directions), translated it and saved it in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Implementation Level Execution method for document translation task Role Thinking Human The user explicitly requested to write the translation content into a file, rather than directly outputting it in the conversation. AI The AI first confirmed the directory structure using ls, then read the original text in segments, and finally wrote the new file using heredoc cat Difference analysis: Users focus on the final deliverable (file output), while AI focuses on the execution path (reading step by step before writing). Both are consistent and complementary.
AI Limitations Important Limitations The AI only read the first 400 lines of the original document (two sed commands). If the document exceeds 400 lines, the translation content may be incomplete. Today’s Achievements Practical Achievements In a HPC environment (Linux remote server), Codex can directly operate the remote file system using exec_command, which is suitable for batch document processing tasks. Conversation Summary ✅ Translated MIHD enhancement plan English document into Chinese and saved it 05:52:05.645 | codex The user requested to translate ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md on the HPC server into Chinese and save it in a new file. The AI first confirmed the directory structure using ls, then read the original content in segments using sed (including normalization, Q-Former, QueST batch correction, etc., 6 enhancement directions), and finally wrote it into ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md using heredoc. The task was successfully completed. The AI provided the final file path and suggested adjusting the translation style as needed.

title: 错误日志 2026-02-13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-12 Today’s Highlights What was done: Two independent tasks were completed across two devices: one was to translate and organize the English enhancement plan for the MIHD project into a structured Chinese version; the other was to add GLM model billing support to the ccusage tool and complete format/type checking verification. How it was done: The DCC side generated a Chinese summary by compressing the original Markdown file using AI. The tianhe side explored the pricing loading mechanism in the ccusage code library, added logic for reading and merging local JSON files in [[[HYMTPH_0]], and made multiple rounds of TypeScript/ESLint fixes to make it pass the checks. What’s useful: The MIHD plan document is now more readable, which helps team alignment. ccusage can now automatically read the local ccusage.json file to calculate GLM model costs, solving the core problem that previous versions only supported Claude billing. DCC What was done: Read and translate the MIHD project [[[HYMTPH_1]], generating a structured Chinese version of the enhancement plan. How it was done: AI read the original Markdown file and compressed it step by step according to 6 BIG AIMs. Key details such as filenames, CLI parameters, and configuration fields were retained. What’s useful: This provides a clear Chinese reference document for implementing MIHD’s normalization, Q-Former, batch correction, and other functions in stages. tianhe What was done: Implemented GLM/local pricing file loading functionality for ccusage, fixing multiple TypeScript and ESLint errors until all format/typecheck/tests passed. How it was done: Explored the [[[HYMTPH_2]], [[[HYMTPH_3]], and packages/internal/pricing.ts] links. In the [[[HYMTPH_5]] function, used the [[[HYMTPH_6]] to read local JSON and merge it with pre-fetched Claude pricing. Iterated to fix issues such as incorrect [[[HYMTPH_7]] call methods, ESLint errors for global variables in [[[HYMTPH_8]], and metadata being misplaced in the pricing table.) What’s useful: ccusage now supports loading any LiteLLM format pricing through the [[[HYMTPH_10]] environment variable or default path [[[HYMTPH_11]]. GLM billing issues have been completely resolved. The tianhe device completed the code implementation and debugging for ccusage GLM billing support, while the DCC device completed the organization of the MIHD enhancement plan in Chinese.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy 🔄 ccusage GLM model billing support — Added the [[[HYMTPH_13]] function to the apps/ccusage/src/_pricing-fetcher.ts, supporting loading pricing data from local LiteLLM format JSON files (default is [[[HYMTPH_14]], which can be overridden by [[[HYMTPH_15]])] and merging it into the offline cache. Format/typecheck and [[[HYMTPH_16]] have been tested successfully; global testing showed that [[[HYMTPH_17]] had failures unrelated to this change.) Implementation and Fixes ✅ MIHD enhancement plan translation — Translated the 6 BIG AIMs in ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md into a structured Chinese version, preserving key implementation details such as filenames, CLI parameters, and configuration fields. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. [[[HYMTPH_18]] returns a function rather than direct results in the [[[HYMTPH_19]] library. The AI’s initial code used it as a direct result, leading to TypeScript type errors. Solution: Change [[[HYMTPH_20]] to [[[HYMTPH_21]]
Key Insight: [[[HYMTPH_22]] is a higher-order function that returns a reusable parser function. Calling this function results in [[[HYMTPH_23]]. This is a unique feature of this library compared to common Result monad implementations.
2. The local ccusage.json contains metadata keys like [[[HYMTPH_24]]. After parsing with valibot schema, it is treated as an empty pricing entry, causing the test assertion “Only entries containing token cost fields should be loaded” to fail. Solution: Add an additional check for [[[HYMTPH_25]] after schema parsing to filter out entries without pricing data.
Key Insight: The LiteLLM pricing JSON contains both description documents ([[[HYMTPH_26]]) and actual model entries. Business semantic filtering needs to be added outside the schema layer.
General Problems 3. ESLint rules prohibit using the global [[[HYMTPH_27]] object directly. The AI used [[[HYMTPH_28]] in its new code, causing lint failures. Solution: Add [[[HYMTPH_29]] at the beginning of the file.
Key Insight: This project requires all Node.js built-in objects to be explicitly imported through the [[[HYMTPH_30]] protocol. This is a strict requirement of ESLint configuration.
4. The pnpm command does not exist in the ccusage environment. corepack cannot download pnpm due to network permission EPERM issues, resulting in failure to run format/typecheck/test. Solution: Users need to install pnpm themselves and then re-run [[[HYMTPH_31]] with elevated permissions. This successfully installed 930 dependencies.
Key Insight: The HPC cluster environment does not have write permissions for the [[[HYMTPH_32]] directory, and the npm registry has limited network access. Users need to install the package manager manually.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level GLM Pricing Data Source Role Thinking Human The user pointed out that there was already a local [[[HYMTPH_33]] file containing GLM entries, and directly asked the AI to use this existing file. AI The initial plan for the AI was to manually add a GLM provider prefix in the code and extend the [[[HYMTPH_34]] filtering function. The AI didn’t realize that the user already had a complete local pricing file. Difference Analysis: Humans know about existing data assets, avoiding duplicating work. AI’s default thinking is to modify the code logic rather than reuse existing data files.
Implementation Level Handling pnpm Environment Issues Role Thinking Human The user installed pnpm to solve environment issues and reported “now I installed pnpm”. AI The AI tried to bypass permission issues through corepack, adjusting XDG_CACHE_HOME, etc., but failed. Difference Analysis: For toolchain installation on the HPC cluster, humans can directly solve the root problem (installing tools) more efficiently than AI’s迂回 approach.
AI Limitations Important Limitations Misuse of the [[[HYMTPH_35]] library’s [[[HYMTPH_36]] API: Assuming its behavior is the same as common Result monad implementations (directly returning results), it actually returns a reusable parser function. Additional calls are needed to obtain the result, resulting in two rounds of fixes. When implementing it for the first time, the AI didn’t consider that the LiteLLM pricing JSON contained non-pricing metadata keys like [[[HYMTPH_37]]. This led to test failures until business filtering logic for token cost fields was added later. General Limitations The AI couldn’t predict the network and permission restrictions of the HPC cluster environment. Multiple attempts to install pnpm through corepack failed due to EPERM or network fetch issues, resulting in a lot of iterations before it became clear that users needed to solve this problem manually. Today’s Gains Key Gains The [[[HYMTPH_38]]’s [[[HYMTPH_39]] returns a function (factory pattern), rather than directly executing and returning Result. This is different from common Rust/fp-ts-style Result implementations, so this needs special attention. The offline pricing in ccusage is pre-filtered with the [[[HYMTPH_40]]’s [[[HYMTPH_41]] function during construction, retaining only Claude-related models. Extending support for multiple models requires同步修改这个宏和[[[HYMTPH_42]]。 The MIHD project plan is implemented in a strict dependency order: configuration basics (Idea 6 Phase 1) → normalization → UNI2+scGPT experiment → Q-Former/LLaVA → Niche query → batch correction → complete configuration integration. This order was designed to avoid accumulating refactoring costs. Practical Gains When using Node.js projects on the HPC cluster, package managers (pnpm) and corepack may fail due to file system permissions or network isolation. It’s necessary to confirm the availability of the toolchain in advance. Conversation Summary MIHD ✅ MIHD enhancement plan translation: Structured Chinese version of 6 BIG AIMs 23:05:59.274 | codex The user requested that the ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md file for the MIHD project be translated into Chinese. After AI read the file, it compressed the content step by step according to normalization, UNI2+scGPT experiment, Q-Former/LLaVA integration, Niche query, batch correction, and full process configuration 6 modules. Key implementation details such as filenames, CLI parameters, and YAML configuration fragments were retained. Finally, a complete Chinese version of the plan document was generated, including the dependency order, implementation stages, and verification plans.
ccusage 🔄 Adding GLM model billing support to ccusage: Implementation of local pricing file loading and multiple rounds of debugging 15:47:15.640 | codex The user first asked about the basic usage of ccusage and then requested that GLM model costs be calculated. AI explored the pricing mechanism [[[HYMTPH_43]] → [[[HYMTPH_44]] → packages/internal/pricing.ts, and found that the offline mode only pre-fetched Claude models. The user pointed out that there was already a local ccusage.json file containing GLM entries. AI then implemented the [[[HYMTPH_46]] function. During debugging, four issues were encountered: pnpm being missing, TypeScript errors caused by the misuse of [[[HYMTPH_47]], ESLint errors for global variables in [[[HYMTPH_48]], and metadata being misplaced in the pricing table. Finally, format/typecheck and [[[HYMTPH_49]] were tested successfully, while [[[HYMTPH_50]]’s unrelated tests failed but were not fixed.

title: 错误日志 2026-02-12

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-10 Today’s Highlights Activities: Organized documentation and history for two robot projects. Prepared the environment for SAC reinforcement learning training of Pick and Place task. How it was done: Conducted static code analysis, read ccusage session summaries, and checked GPU status with nvidia-smi. Evaluated each project’s current state and generated standardized documents. What this means: Obtained a complete contributor guide for error_recovery_benchmark. The training link for robobrain_pi is ready (4 A100-80GB GPUs available). Documentation updates for gadget research have started. Progress across three projects: improving contributor documentation for robot benchmark projects, organizing robobrain_pi history and preparing SAC reinforcement learning training. Documentation updates for gadget research module are underway.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Prepare the training environment for robobrain_pi SAC Pick-and-Place — Confirmed datasets/demo_v2.hdf5 exists (50 trajectories, 7-dimensional actions). Found a complete SAC framework in place (sac_agent.py, trainer.py, train_sac.py). Checked GPU status and confirmed 4 A100-80GB GPUs are available. Recommended using GPUs 1-3 (GPU 0 is already occupied with 5GB). Provided training command. ✅ Check progress of error_recovery_benchmark plan — Reviewed PLAN_CURRENT_STATUS.md and EXECUTION_STATUS.md. Confirmed about 6200 lines of code have been completed. Current high-priority blockers are collision geometry name mapping (collision.py/env_wrapper.py) and target object dynamic recognition. Complete E2E verification depends on GPU node (an53). 🔄 Update gadget research/CLAUDE.md documentation — Users requested a detailed review of the research directory structure and core code. Updated design documents after understanding the overall architecture. Session records are dated until user messages are received. AI has not started actual analysis yet. ✅ Restore robobrain_pi project history from ccusage summaries — Read 10 Markdown summary files in .ccusage/summaries/. Reconstructed a complete timeline of the project from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09: environment setup → data integration → stabilization → training framework improvement. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Create error_recovery_benchmark AGENTS.md contributor guide — Read project structure, Makefile, test code, and existing documents. Generated a 372-word Repository Guidelines document without git history. Covers project structure, build commands, coding standards, testing guidelines, and submission specifications. ✅ Analyze robobrain_pi ccusage token statistics — Read .ccusage/ccusage.json. Calculated that 11 sessions consumed about 21.09 million tokens. The largest single session (fix tests, optimize code) used 6.57 million tokens. All fee fields were $0 (subscription plan). No created_at timestamps existed in any sessions. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Complete E2E verification of error_recovery_benchmark is blocked by GPU node dependencies (EGL/robosuite runtime constraints) Solution: Run unit tests and smoke tests on CPU. Postpone complete E2E verification until GPU nodes are available (an53).
Key Insight: Robot simulation framework tests should be clearly stratified: CPU-ready unit/logic tests vs rendering/physics tests that require GPUs/EGL. Avoid test blocking.
General Issues 2. The created_at field in ccusage.json is N/A for all sessions, making it impossible to provide a timeline analysis directly. Solution: Instead, read independent Markdown summary files in .ccusage/summaries/. These contain time information.
Key Insight: ccusage stores timestamps in summary files rather than the main JSON index. Combining both is necessary to obtain complete information.
3. error_recovery_benchmark has no git history, making it impossible to infer coding standards from commit history. Solution: Extract standards from existing documents (README_V4.md, Makefile, CLAUDE.md, test code) and generate AGENTS.md.
Key Insight: Contributor guides can be constructed based on static analysis of existing code structure and documentation without relying on git history. However, this limitation must be clearly stated.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Method for restoring cross-session context Role Approach Human Humans actively design and use ccusage to export historical session summaries. Then, AI is asked to read the summaries and reconstruct project context. AI AI passively accepts summary content and reconstructs the timeline. It did not propose this toolchain approach itself. Difference Analysis: Humans proposed an engineering solution for AI context limitations (externalizing memory + reading). This was a design pattern that AI failed to suggest.
Choice of training scheme for robobrain_pi: IL+RL vs pure SAC Role Approach Human Users chose to first verify the correctness of the basic training process using pure SAC, rather than using more complex schemes. AI AI actively recommended an IL pre-training + RL fine-tuning scheme. It believed this would be more efficient with 50 demonstration data available. Difference Analysis: Humans tend to proceed by verifying step-by-step (first running smoothly, then optimizing). AI tends to recommend better solutions. For debugging stages, humans’ strategies can quickly identify issues with the environment/framework.
AI Limitations Major Limitations In error_recovery_benchmark, it is impossible to actually run make smoke tests to verify the framework’s operation status. Only static documentation analysis is possible. There is no runtime verification of the project’s health status. General Limitations Did not actively propose ccusage summary files as an alternative source for timeline information. Stopped after reporting that created_at was N/A, relying on user guidance to find the summaries/ directory. Today’s Achievements Core Achievements Using external summary tools (ccusage) to export historical sessions is an effective engineering approach to solve AI context limitations. It allows AI to quickly restore a complete project context in new sessions, avoiding repeated description of background. Testing strategies for large robot RL projects should be clearly stratified: CPU unit tests, CPU smoke tests, GPU E2E tests. This prevents the entire testing process from being blocked by unavailable GPUs. The current key blockers in error_recovery_benchmark are collision geometry name mapping and target object硬编码 issues. These are bugs at the framework integration level, not training algorithm problems. Session Summaries ErrorRecoveryBenchmark ✅ Checked current project plan progress and blockers 22:53:09.527 | codex Users asked about the current status of the project plan. AI checked PLAN_CURRENT_STATUS.md and EXECUTION_STATUS.md. Confirmed that about 6200 lines of code have been completed (Detectors, Injectors, Validators, Replay, Database, Metrics, Workflow scripts are all ready). Current blockers are bugs in collision geometry name mapping and target object硬编码 issues. Complete E2E verification depends on GPU node (an53).
✅ Generated AGENTS.md contributor guide 22:53:09.527 | codex AI read project structure, Makefile, test files, and CLAUDE.md. Found no git history in the repository. Through static analysis of existing code and documentation, generated a 372-word Repository Guidelines document covering project structure (error_framework/, scripts/, configs/), build commands (make test/smoke), Python coding standards, and pytest testing guidelines.
RoboBrainPi 🔄 Checked GPU resources and prepared for SAC reinforcement learning training 04:03:07.000 | codex Users decided to first verify the process using pure SAC (rather than IL+RL combination). AI used nvidia-smi to find 4 A100-80GB GPUs. Recommended using GPUs 1-3 as GPU 0 is already occupied with 5GB. Confirmed that datasets/demo_v2.hdf5 (50 trajectories of 600 steps, 7-dimensional actions) and the complete SAC framework are ready. Provided nohup background training commands, waiting for users to confirm the start.
✅ Restored project history context through ccusage summaries 03:52:35.762 | codex Users exported historical summaries using ccusage tools. AI was asked to read them and reconstruct the project history. AI read 10 Markdown files in .ccusage/summaries/. Organized a timeline from 2026-01-15 to 2026-02-09, summarizing key technical decisions: OSC_POSE controller, no image observation space, SAC automatic entropy adjustment framework are all ready.
✅ Calculated token consumption and costs for historical conversations through ccusage.json 03:26:16.993 | codex Users requested statistics on tokens and costs for all historical conversations. AI read ccusage.json. Found that 11 sessions consumed about 21.09 million tokens. The largest single session (fix tests + optimize code) used 6.57 million tokens, accounting for 30.6% of the total. All fee fields were $0, confirming a subscription plan. Due to missing created_at fields, it was not possible to provide a timeline analysis.
gadget 🔄 Started updating research/CLAUDE.md architecture documentation 06:41:23.706 | claude_code Users asked AI to first thoroughly read the research directory structure and core code. After fully understanding the overall architecture, AI updated the design document in CLAUDE.md. Session records are dated until user messages are received. AI has not started actual analysis yet. The task is still in the initial stage.

summary: 在三个项目中全面推进：为机器人基准测试项目完善贡献者文档；整理robobrain_pi的历史记录，并为SAC强化学习训练做好准备；启动gadget research模块的文档更新工作。
title: 错误日志 2026-02-10

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-09 Today’s Highlights What was done: Added ARI/NMI/Silhouette metrics to clustering visualization charts, added Spearman/P@K metrics to RM-Ideal heatmaps. Also ran benchmark tests for 11 DLPFC slices and performed RM-Iideal evaluations in bulk. How it was done: Reorganized the save= parameter mode in scanpy to use an explicit fig/axes layout. Added text boxes for metrics using fig.text(). Used a single GPU sequential execution strategy with caching skipped to avoid OOM errors. What’s achieved: Quantitative metrics are now available for all successfully sliced results, eliminating the need to consult logs and improving the efficiency of experiment comparisons. RM-Iideal evaluations were also completed for 7 slices. Added quantitative overlay layers to MIHD project clustering and RM-Iideal visualization charts. Ran benchmark tests and RM-Iideal evaluations for all DLPFC slices in bulk.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Added ARI/NMI metrics to clustering visualizations — Reorganized create_clustering_visualization() in utils/visualization.py and run_benchmark.py, switching from scanpy save= mode to explicit fig/axes layout. Added metrics parameters and rendered ARI/NMI/Silhouette text boxes at the bottom of the chart. ✅ Added Spearman/P@K metrics to RM-Iideal visualizations — Added metrics parameters in evaluate_rm_ideal.py’s visualize() method, rendering text boxes for Spearman r, p-values, P@10/20/50, and SameLabel@50. Implementation and Fixes 🔄 Ran pca_uni_staig_fusion benchmark tests in bulk (all DLPFC slices) — Used PCA+UNI+STAIG_FUSION configuration to run benchmark tests on 11 DLPFC slices, skipping cache results for 151508/151509. Successfully completed multiple new slices; 151510/151672 failed due to missing spatial coordinates. 🔄 Ran RM-Iideal evaluations in bulk (all available slices) — Parallelly ran RM-Iideal evaluations on 7 slices with embeddings. –niche_label all was used to evaluate all tissue labels. 5 slices were completed, while 2 (151508/151509, multiple embedding methods) are still running. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. A local copy of create_clustering_visualization existed in run_benchmark.py, completely obscuring the同名 function in utils/visualization.py. Solution: Located the local copy at line 1644 of run_benchmark.py and applied the same metrics parameters and fig/axes reorganization. Key insight: AI only modified theutils module without noticing the local copy, resulting in ineffective verification runs. It was discovered through Grep search.
2. scanpy’s sc.pl.spatial(save=filename) does not expose a figure object, making it impossible to add text to charts. Solution: Reorganized to use an explicit fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2) layout, calling sc.pl.spatial() with ax parameter, and then using fig.text() and fig.savefig() for rendering. Key insight: scanpy’s save= parameter is a convenient API that sacrifices control over the chart object. It’s necessary to comment out figure.show() and use bbox_inches=‘tight’ to ensure text boxes aren’t cropped.
General Issues 3. Parallelly launching 3 GPU training processes (PCA/GCN/E2E + STAIG_FUSION) led to GPU OOM errors, causing processes to be killed. Solution: Stopped all parallel tasks and switched to sequential execution for each configuration.
4. AI misjudged the optimal configuration and tried to re-run uni_staig_fusion (without a gene encoder). Solution: The user explicitly instructed to use the pca_uni_staig_fusion folder. After checking, AI confirmed using PCA+UNI+STAIG_FUSION+-skip_cached. Key insight: It’s necessary to review the existing directory structure before examining historical experiment results.
5. Slices like 151510/151672 reported ValueError: staig_fusion requires spatial coordinates. Solution: This was a data issue (missing spatial coordinate files in slices). Skipped these slices and continued with others. Key insight: Some DLPFC slices have incomplete data, and staig_fusion relies heavily on spatial coordinates. It’s necessary to check the completeness of these slices later.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Choose the correct historical optimal configuration. Role Thought Human The user knows that pca_uni_staig_fusion contains the best results and directly pointed out that incorrect configurations should not be used. AI By reading all summary CSV files, AI compared ARI scores but still chose the wrong direction (preferred uni_staig_fusion). Difference analysis: The user has direct knowledge of the experimental history, while AI needs to systematically search to make mistakes in judgment. The user corrected AI’s analytical conclusions.
Implementation Level Do not overwrite existing results. Role Thought Human The user explicitly emphasized not to overwrite existing files and should skip cached results. AI In previous runs, AI used the –override parameter, which would overwrite existing embeddings and visualizations. Difference analysis: The user focused on protecting experimental results. AI defaults to using –override to ensure that latest code takes effect. This is a trade-off between workflow safety and correctness.
Determine the feasibility of parallel GPU tasks. Role Thought Human The user did not explicitly mention parallel issues, but acknowledged switching to sequential execution after tasks were killed. AI AI actively chose to run 3 GPU-intensive processes in parallel to speed up tasks, resulting in OOM errors. Difference analysis: AI prioritized speed over GPU memory limits. In a HPC shared GPU environment, serial processing should be the default approach.
AI Limitations Major Limitations Failed to detect a local copy of the create_clustering_visualization function within run_benchmark.py during initial code modifications. Only modified theutils module, resulting in ineffective verification runs. Misjudged the optimal configuration without thoroughly reviewing the experimental history. Chose the wrong benchmark configuration (uni_staig_fusion vs pca_uni_staig_fusion). General Limitations Started 3 training processes simultaneously on a HPC GPU environment, ignoring memory limits and resulting in OOM errors. Could not directly view PNG images when reading file; visual quality was determined by file size or indirect metrics. Today’s Takeaways Key Takeaways Before modifying functions in large scripts, use Grep to search for local copies of the same function. Ensure no local copy obscures module-level implementations. Scanpy’s spatial visualization can be integrated with matplotlib using an explicit fig, axes layout. It’s key to use fig.savefig(bbox_inches=‘tight’) instead of scanpy’s save parameter. Practical Takeaways In a HPC single-GPU environment, STAIG_FUSION requires high memory for training. Parallel processing is not feasible; batch experiments should use && concatenation or separate slurm jobs. Among the 11 DLPFC dataset slices, some slices like 151510/151672 lack spatial coordinates or have full-resolution images. Staig_fusion and UNI-based models rely heavily on these data. Session Summary ✅ Implementation: Reorganized visualization functions and added ARI/NMI/Spearman text boxes 21:50:31.276 | claude_code The user provided a complete plan, requiring AI to implement directly. AI modified utils/visualization.py (explicit fig/axes reorganization + metrics parameters), evaluate_rm_ideal.py (metrics parameters + Spearman/P@K overlay), and the call points in run_benchmark.py. During verification, it was found that a local copy of the function existed in run_benchmark.py, obscuring the utils module. After additional modifications, the verification passed syntax checks, and PNGs with metrics were generated (ARI=0.1807).
🔄 Planning: Added quantitative overlay layers to clustering and RM-Iideal visualizations 21:46:17.845 | claude_code The user requested NMI/ARI scores displayed on clustering visualization charts and benchmark results like p-values displayed on RM_Ideal_evaluation visualizations. AI explored the implementation of two visualization systems, identified the limitations of scanpy’s save= parameter, and drafted four-file modification plans. The user rejected the automatic exit of ExitPlanMode and chose to manually control the implementation pace.
🔄 Rerun pca_uni_staig_fusion configuration: Skipped caching and completed evaluations for 7 slices 22:30:00.000 | claude_code The user pointed out that the best results from the pca_uni_staig_fusion folder should be used. After checking, AI confirmed using the –skip_cached parameter to skip 151508/151509 and ran PCA+UNI+STAIG_FUSION+STAIG alignment configuration on the remaining 9 slices. 151510/151672 failed due to data issues; all other slices were completed. The ARI range was 0.23-0.55, and PNGs with metrics were generated for all successful slices.
🔄 Batch RM-Iideal evaluations: Parallelized evaluation of WWL chart cores on 7 slices 22:57:20.476 | claude_code The user requested RM-Iideal evaluations for all available slices to generate visualizations. AI checked the availability of embeddings for each slice and started 7 evaluation tasks in parallel (–niche_label all covering all tissue labels). 5 single-method slices were completed quickly (about 2 minutes); 151508/151509 (including multiple embedding method comparisons) are still running. The results included PNGs with heatmaps containing Spearman correlation coefficients and P@K metrics.
❌ Batch benchmark tests: STAIG_FUSION+UNI2 multi-configuration runs on all slices (OOM failure) 22:10:00.000 | claude_code The user requested running STAIG_FUSION+UNI2+GCN/PCA/None configurations on all 12 DLPFC slices. AI started 3 GPU processes in parallel, but the PCA process was killed due to OOM errors. After switching to sequential execution, 151510 failed due to missing spatial coordinates. The task was interrupted, and the user requested re-planning based on historical optimal results.
