title: Bug Journal 2026-02-09

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W06 (2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08) This week (2026-02-06~07), the work was focused on two main areas: first, systematically diagnosing the ARI performance gap between staig_fusion in the MIHD project and the original STAIG (0.21→target 0.56). Five key implementation differences were identified and quantified. Additionally, we completed the code layer work for strict semantic alignment. Second, we engineered improvements to the benchmark tool, fixed the bug in rendering bar charts, and implemented an end-to-end GitHub Pages automatic publishing workflow along with CLI interaction for uploading files. Furthermore, I practiced English speaking for TOEFL (test of oral proficiency).
Week Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08 Active Days 1 / 7 Total Conversations 2 Projects Involved 2 Completed Tasks 7 Ongoing Tasks 2 Total Tokens 88,552,896 Total Cost $28.25 Claude Code Tokens 4,682,683 Claude Code Costs $1.00 Codex Tokens 83,870,213 Codex Costs $27.25 Average Daily Cost $14.12 Project Progress MIHD / STAIG Fusion (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Compared the original STAIG notebook with MIHD implementation, identified five quantifiable key differences and ranked them by priority. Identified the root cause: coordinates of full-resolution images (13332×13332) still used a scaling factor of 0.15, and patch points deviated significantly from the tissue area. Completed code modifications for strict semantic alignment in staig_fusion (4 core files), implementing default mclust, HVG original features, STAIG hyperparameters, and spatial majority vote refinement. Fixed the issue where mclust silently downgraded to kmeans when unavailable, added pre-validation for embedding shape. Identified issues with reverse gene preprocessing order (MIHD first HVG then normalize/log/scale; opposite in original STAIG). Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Mixing coordinate scales is the top priority issue. Verification during runtime is still needed after modifications. ⚠️ The root cause of mclust dimension being zero (which step generated an empty embedding) needs to be confirmed after next run. ⚠️ HPC environment did not fully install rpy2 and R mclust packages, so the risk of silent downgrading remains. Benchmark Tool (gadget) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Fixed the bug in rendering short bars for high scores using Plotly JS CDN version, forced list type, and set rangemode=‘tozero’. Implemented an end-to-end GitHub Pages publishing workflow: verification/duplication/masking → repository_dispatch → queue files → daily batch processing → automatic deployment. Added scripts/ingest_submissions.py (verification/duplication/cleaning) and scripts/submit_result.py. Implemented three GitHub Actions workflows: accept-submission, daily-publish, pages-deploy. Added interactive upload prompts to the benchmark CLI, supporting –upload/-no-upload and environment variable configuration. Removed the Historical Trends block and fixed an AttributeError. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ The issue of low GPU utilization (both CPU and GPU were low, with single-threaded I/O waiting as a bottleneck) has not been fully resolved. Users need to choose an optimization plan. English Learning (TOEFL) (1 active day) — 🔄 active Completed:
Completed practice for Question 4 in TOEFL speaking (Bystander Effect), achieving a score of 4.5/5. Practiced 4 questions this week, improving from 3.5 to 4.5. The main weakness was grammar errors related to subject-verb agreement. Key Tasks ✅ Diagnosing the ARI/NMI performance gap between STAIG fusion and original STAIG (2026-02-06) — Compared two code paths, identified and quantified five root causes: ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC is unavailable; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference of 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformation differences. ✅ Implementing GitHub Pages automatic publishing workflow (2026-02-06) — Added a new relay architecture, three GitHub Actions workflows to achieve end-to-end data collection, duplication, cleaning, and automatic publishing. 🔄 Implementing strict STAIG semantic alignment in staig_fusion (2026-02-06) — Modified 4 core files to achieve strict STAIG semantic alignment. Syntax verification passed. Verification of mclust dimension errors is still needed during runtime. ✅ Fixing the bar chart rendering bug in benchmark reports (2026-02-06) — Locked Plotly JS CDN version to 3.3.1, changed Series to list format, set rangemode=‘tozero’ to completely fix short bars issue for high scores. 🔄 Fixing the mclust dimension is zero runtime error (2026-02-06) — Added pre-validation for embedding shape at the Python level (2D, non-zero rows/columns, sample count >= cluster count). The root cause will be confirmed after next run. ✅ Adding interactive upload prompts to the benchmark CLI (2026-02-06) — Asked users if they wanted to upload after running, supporting –upload/-no-upload and environment variables BENCHMARK_RELAY_URL. Problems and Solutions 1. MIHD staig_fusion’s ARI (0.21/0.4849) is much lower than the original STAIG’s (0.562), root cause unknown [MIHD / STAIG Fusion] (2026-02-06) Solution: Compared two code paths, identified five root causes and ranked them by priority: ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC is unavailable; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference of 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters not fully aligned.
2. Plotly bar chart rendering error: Short bars for high scores, visual logic reversed [Benchmark Tool] (2026-02-06) Solution: The root cause was that Plotly 6.x used binary-encoded arrays serialization, which was incompatible with older CDN versions. Solution: Locked the CDN version to match the Python package version (3.3.1), forced a Python list input, and set rangemode=‘tozero’.
3. Strict STAIG mode forces visual encoder to UNI, users think it’s using UNI2, behavior unclear [MIHD / STAIG Fusion] (2026-02-06) Solution: Added clear hints about encoder override in logs; the UNI2 progress bar was not displayed because UNI was actually used.
4. create_trend_chart AttributeError: Method is commented out but still called in generate_html [Benchmark Tool] (2026-02-06) Solution: Added a stub method that returns an empty chart and completely removed the calling code.
Learning Outcomes Architecture The correct layered architecture for GitHub Pages submissions: Public users → relay (verification/throttling/masking) → repository_dispatch → queue files → daily batch processing (duplication/cleaning/appending CSV) → commit/push → automatic pages deployment. The relay layer is a key isolation layer to protect write permissions of the repository. Interface names must strictly match actual behavior: The name ‘staig_fusion’ naturally implies the same semantics as STAIG. Allowing significant differences in default behavior can lead to many hidden errors. Global override behaviors (such as forcing UNI as the encoder) must be clearly indicated in logs. Debugging Plotly 6.x uses binary-encoded arrays serialization by default, which causes bar length parsing errors when it’s incompatible with older CDN versions. Solution: Strictly lock the CDN version to match the Python package version, forcing a Python list input instead of pandas Series. Low CPU and GPU utilization indicates that the program is waiting in single-threaded I/O, not just CPU. Visual feature extraction performance bottlenecks usually occur in data preprocessing (patch extraction) rather than model forward passes. When R interfaces (like mclust) fail in Python, the error message isn’t intuitive. Sufficient pre-validation should be added at the Python level, and clear error messages with shape information should be provided. If an R package is unavailable, a clear warning must be given for silent degradation. The algorithm behavior shouldn’t be changed without any hints. Domain Knowledge There are five quantifiable key differences between MIHD and original STAIG (ranked by priority): ① Mixing coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading; ③ Reverse gene preprocessing order; ④ Pseudo-label cluster count difference; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformation differences. The ARI gap (0.21→0.56) is mainly determined by points ① and ②. Notes on AI Usage Effective Modes:
✓ Systematic cross-repository code comparison: By comparing two code libraries line by line, we quantified and identified multiple implementation differences and ranked them by priority. ✓ Multi-turn tool invocation combined with human observation: AI detected coordinate scale issues (technical details), while users noticed encoder override problems (runtime observations), creating a complementary approach. ✓ Architecture design supplementation: Humans proposed core security intuitions (confirmation before upload, secure isolation), while AI translated them into complete executable technical architectures. Limitations:
✗ Insufficient fix-ups: When removing trend charts, only the calling function was removed initially, and additional fixes were needed. ✗ Easy to overlook existing global override logic: Didn’t realize that strict mode would force visual encoder override when adding UNI2 tqdm. ✗ Didn’t check HPC environment availability of modules (like scanpy) in advance, resulting in a ModuleNotFoundError during verification. This increased the number of debugging rounds. ✗ Provided multiple options for low GPU utilization issues, but didn’t actively recommend using profile tools (py-spy, nvprof) to accurately identify the actual bottleneck. Next Week’s Plan The highest priority next week is to verify the runtime effects of strict STAIG alignment modifications: ① Fixing the issue with mixing coordinate scales (full-resolution images should no longer use scaling factors), which is the most critical step for improving ARI; ② Confirm the root cause of mclust dimension being zero and fix it; ③ Install rpy2 and R mclust packages to eliminate the risk of silent degradation in the HPC environment. For the benchmark tool, we can selectively promote GPU utilization optimization (multi-process DataLoader or patch cache pre-extraction schemes). In terms of English learning, we will continue to practice for grammar weaknesses like subject-verb agreement.

However, I can create mock questions that closely mimic the style and difficulty of actual questions. The results will be similar to practicing with real questions. If you want to access real questions, you can refer to: - 📚 **The Official Guide to the TOEFL Test** (ETS official guide) - 🌐 **TOEFL Official Practice Questions** (toefl.org) - 📱 **TOEFL Practice Online (TPO)** (official full simulation question bank, paid option) --- Do you want me to generate more mock questions? Or do you have other topics you'd like to practice on? 😊 👤 User: Okay, let’s try another mock question. 🤖 Assistant: Alright! Here comes the fourth mock question 💪 --- ### 📖 Reading Section (45 seconds) **The Bystander Effect** The bystander effect refers to the phenomenon where individuals are less likely to offer help in an emergency situation when other people are present. Research has shown that as the number of bystanders increases, the likelihood that any one person will intervene decreases. This occurs for two main reasons: **diffusion of responsibility** — the belief that someone else will take action — and **pluralistic ignorance** — the tendency to look to others for cues and, seeing no one act, assume that the situation is not actually an emergency. Understanding the bystander effect has important implications for public safety and emergency response training. --- ⏱ **45 seconds have passed. Please read now!** Are you ready? 😊 👤 User: Okay. 🤖 Assistant: Alright! Here comes the listening summary: --- ### 🎧 Listening Summary (Simulated Lecture Content) The professor describes a classic psychology experiment conducted in a laboratory setting. In the experiment, participants were placed either alone in a room or in a group of three. While completing a questionnaire, smoke began to slowly fill the room through a vent — simulating an emergency. When participants were **alone**, approximately **75%** reported the smoke within two minutes. However, when participants were in a **group of three**, only about **38%** reported the smoke — and many waited much longer before acting. Researchers noticed that participants in the group would glance at each other, and since no one was reacting with alarm, they seemed to conclude that the situation was not serious. The professor emphasizes that this experiment clearly demonstrates why people in groups often fail to respond to emergencies, even when the danger is obvious. --- ### 💬 Question **Using the example from the lecture, explain how it illustrates the concept described in the reading passage.** ⏱ **60 seconds have passed. Please start answering in English!** 👤 User: The reading passage describes the bystander effect: the tendency of people to be less likely to offer help in an emergency when other people is around. This can be attributed to two main reasons: the diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance. The professor illustrated this concept using a classic experiment. In the experiment, participants were placed either alone or in groups. Then smoke started to fill the room. The result shows that when participants were alone, 75% of them reported the smoke. However, when placed in groups, only 38% of participants reported the smoke, and many waited longer before acting. This illustrates the bystander effect because participants in the group was less likely to act compared to those alone. This may also be an illustration of pluralistic ignorance because participants were looking at each other and didn’t act upon the emergency. 🤖 Assistant: Excellent! That is a very complete answer so far 🏆 Let's look at the comments: --- ### ✅ Advantages - **Accurate theoretical introduction**: You mentioned both reasons (diffusion of responsibility + pluralistic ignorance), which is a great point. Many answers only mention one reason. - **Precise data usage**: The comparison between 75% vs 38% is very effective in supporting your argument. - **Well-structured ending**: You linked the bystander effect to pluralistic ignorance, making your answer more organized. This is a characteristic of high-scoring answers. - **Clear logic**: Your paragraphs flow smoothly without any jumps in logic. --- ### 🔧 Suggestions for improvement | Issue | Original Text | Suggestion | |------|--------------|-------------| | Subject-Verb Agreement | "when other people **is** around" | changed to "when other people **are** around" | | Subject-Verb Agreement | "participants in the group **was** less likely" | changed to "participants in the group **were** less likely" | | Expression clarity | "didn’t act upon the emergency" | changed to "**failed to respond to** the emergency" | | Minor details | Add "This aligns with the reading's explanation that people look to others for cues and assume nothing is wrong." | --- ### 📊 Score (5-point scale) | Criteria | Score | |------|------| | Answer completeness | 5/5 | | Language accuracy | 4/5 | | Coherence & expression | 4.5/5 | | Overall score | **约4.5/5** 🎉 | --- ### 📈 Your progress | Question | Topic | Score | |------|------|------| | Question 1 | Cognitive Load | 3.5~4 | | Question 2 | Mere Exposure Effect | 4 | | Question 3 | Operant Conditioning | 4~4.5 | | Question 4 | Bystander Effect | **4.5** | You've made significant progress! The only remaining issues are minor grammar errors (subject-verb agreement) and improving the academic tone of your language. You’re close to a perfect score. Still want to practice? content:
Benchmarking Tools How it was done: By comparing code line by line using tools like ripgrep/sed/codex, tracing parameters across repositories (original STAIG notebook, train_img_config.yaml, data-processing.py), and implementing patch modifications. MIHD modifications covered 4 core files, while the benchmark modifications were verified end-to-end locally. What’s it for: The staig_fusion now strictly aligns with the original STAIG semantics (default mclust+HVG features+STAIG hyperparameters). Key differences such as mixed coordinate scales have been quantified and are pending fixing. The benchmark tools have undergone a significant upgrade from local scripts to publicly run and automatically publishing capabilities, allowing users to submit results directly to public rankings. DCC What was done: Completed all diagnostics and implementations in the HPC cluster: identified 5 root causes of STAIG performance differences, implemented strict alignment modifications, fixed mclust errors and missing tqdm, and deeply diagnosed four major issues including mixed coordinate scales in slide 151508. How it was done: Compared the original STAIG notebook and configuration files with MIHD implementations, measured image size (13332x13332) and coordinate ranges, and edited code directly in the /hpc/group/yizhanglab/zt81/MIHD directory. What’s it for: Completed the core work of strictly aligning staig_fusion with the original STAIG semantics. Mixed coordinate scales have been identified as the top priority issue to be fixed, laying the code foundation for subsequent ARI improvements. TzJsDesktop What was done: Fixed bugs in the benchmark HTML report bar charts and implemented end-to-end GitHub Pages automatic publishing along with CLI interaction upload functionality. How it was done: Locked the Plotly JS CDN version, forced a numeric list to solve rendering issues; implemented public submission pipelines through three GitHub Actions workflows and a relay architecture; added interactive upload prompts in the CLI. What’s it for: The benchmark tools have undergone a significant upgrade from local scripts to publicly run and automatically publishing websites. Across DCC clusters and TzJsDesktop, the system diagnosed and quantified five root causes of performance differences between MIHD staig_fusion and the original STAIG. At the same time, the bar chart fixes in the benchmark tools, GitHub Pages automatic publishing pipelines, and CLI interaction upload functions were engineered and delivered.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Diagnosing ARI/NMI performance differences between STAIG fusion and the original STAIG — The system compared the complete code paths of the original STAIG notebook (ARI=0.562) with MIHD benchmarks (ARI=0.21/0.4849), identifying five key differences: ① Mixed coordinate scales for full-resolution images (most critical, coordinates incorrectly compressed from x:2579-11821 to x:386-1773); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC is unavailable; ③ Gene preprocessing order reversed (MIHD first HVG then normalize/log/scale vs original STAIG); ④ Pseudo-label cluster count 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformations differences ✅ Implementing a GitHub Pages automatic publishing pipeline — Added scripts/ingest_submissions.py (verification/deduplication/cleaning), scripts/submit_result.py (relay/dispatch submission), three GitHub Actions workflows (accept-submission, daily-publish, pages-deploy), and data/queue files to achieve end-to-end public data collection and automatic publishing 🔄 Implementing strict alignment with STAIG fusion — Modified 4 files including models/STAIGTrainer.py, scripts/run_benchmark.py to achieve strict STAIG semantics: default mclust, original HVG features, STAIG hyperparameter profile, spatial majority vote refine, pseudo-label cluster count 300/80. Syntax verification passed, but runtime errors with mclust dimension remain pending full validation ✅ Fixing bugs in benchmark report bar charts — Fixed high-score short bars: locked Plotly JS CDN to version 3.3.1, changed Series to Python list, set y-axis rangemode=‘tozero’ 🔄 Fixing runtime error ‘mclust dimension is zero’ — mclust reported ‘svd(data, nu=0): a dimension is zero’, added shape protection checks at the Python level (2D, no rows/columns, sample count >= cluster count). The root cause (which step produced an empty embedding upstream) will be confirmed after the next run ✅ Adding interactive upload prompts to benchmark CLI — Added interaction logic in benchmark/cli.py asking users if they want to upload after execution, supporting –upload, –no-upload, –relay-url, BENCHMARK_RELAY_URL environment variables. No upload by default in non-interactive mode Implementation and Fixes ❌ Diagnosing low GPU utilization issues — Both CPU and GPU usage were very low. Analysis indicated a single-threaded I/O wait bottleneck (single-threaded patch extraction, batch_size=32, calling empty_cache() per batch). Proposed solutions include increasing batch_size, DataLoader multi-processing, and patch cache pre-extraction, pending user selection ✅ Removing the ‘Historical Trends’ block in reports and fixing AttributeError — Removed trend chart generation calls from benchmark/report.py, added compatibility wrappers to prevent AttributeError, completely removing the call path ✅ Generating AGENTS.md contributor guide — Created standard-format AGENTS.md for both MIHD repository (310 words) and benchmark repository (329 words), including sections on project structure, build commands, code standards, testing guidelines, submission specifications ✅ Adding tqdm progress bars for UNI/UNI2 image encoding — Added tqdm in the batch inference loop of scripts/run_benchmark.py. It was also noticed that strict STAIG mode would force visual encoder to UNI instead of user-specified UNI2, which is opaque to users Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. MIHD staig_fusion’s ARI (0.21/0.4849) far below the original STAIG notebook’s (0.562), root cause unknown Solution: The system compared two code paths, identifying five root causes: ① Mixed coordinate scaling for full-resolution images (13332x13332) still multiplied by a hires scaling factor (0.15), leading to severe deviation of patch points from tissue areas (most critical); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC was unavailable; ③ Gene preprocessing order reversed (MIHD first HVG then normalize/log/scale vs original STAIG); ④ Pseudo-label cluster count 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters not fully aligned and image transformations differences Key insight: The name staig_fusion inherently promises ’equivalent to STAIG’ semantics. Allowing significant differences in default behavior can lead to many hidden errors. Mixed coordinate scaling is the top priority issue to fix
2. Plotly bar chart rendering error: High scores correspond to short bars, visual logic inverted Solution: Locked the Plotly JS CDN version to match the Python installation version (3.3.1), changed Series to list, and set y-axis rangemode=‘tozero’ Key insight: Plotly 6.x uses binary-encoded array serialization, which causes data parsing errors when incompatible with plotly-latest CDN version. Version consistency must be strictly maintained
3. mclust clustering reports error ‘svd(data, nu=0): a dimension is zero’, and silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC environment is unavailable Solution: Added shape verification at the Python level (2D, no rows/columns, sample count >= cluster count). The root cause will be confirmed after the next run. Need to install rpy2 and R mclust packages to eliminate silent degradation issues Key insight: Error messages from the R side are not intuitive. Sufficient pre-verification should be added at the Python level. Silent degradation must include clear warnings rather than changing clustering methods without any prompts
General Issues 4. Strict STAIG mode forces visual encoder to UNI, users unaware (thinking they’re using UNI2, tqdm not appearing) Solution: Added clear hints about encoder override in logs; UNI2 progress bars not displayed because UNI was actually run Key insight: Global override behavior is opaque to users. Clear hints must be added in logs; otherwise, users will spend time debugging non-existent problems
5. Low GPU utilization (both CPU and GPU usage were very low) Solution: Not fully resolved. Analysis indicated a single-threaded I/O wait bottleneck: patch extraction was the main bottleneck. Proposed solutions include adjusting batch size, DataLoader multi-processing, and patch cache pre-extraction Key insight: Low CPU and GPU usage together indicates program is waiting in single-threaded IO, not CPU being the bottleneck. Performance bottlenecks for visual feature extraction are usually found in data preprocessing rather than model forward passes
6. create_trend_chart AttributeError: Function was commented out but still called in generate_html Solution: Added a stub method that returns an empty chart and completely removed the calling code Key insight: Commenting out function definitions does not equal removing them. All calling parties must be removed simultaneously
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Direction of diagnosis and decision-making for STAIG performance differences Role Thought Human Humans directly pointed out the problem (staig_fusion scores far below original STAIG), provided example.ipynb as a ground truth, and decided ‘staig_fusion’s semantics are equivalent to STAIG, so it should be overridden without retaining old behavior’. Continuously asked ‘why is the effect poor’ to drive deeper analysis. The additional observation that ‘CPU usage was also low’ directly ruled out the assumption that CPU was the bottleneck AI AI systematically traced code (measured image size 13332x13332, calculated coordinate compression ratio, line-by-line comparison of two code bases), identified 5 quantitative differences after multiple tool calls. However, it required users to clearly decide on the direction to proceed Difference analysis: Human decisions are more strategic (names inherently imply semantics, direct override allows significant differences in default behavior without causing many hidden errors). Mixed coordinate scaling is the top priority issue to fix
Design of public submission architecture for GitHub Pages Role Thought Human Humans actively proposed using interactive prompts to ask users if they wanted to upload results and explicitly required relay to protect repository write permissions AI AI supplemented with a complete queueing architecture (relay→dispatch→queue file→daily batch processing→commit/push→Pages), strict schema verification, hash deduplication, and IP masking mechanisms to prevent abuse Difference analysis: Human intuition includes ‘confirm consent before uploading’ and ‘security isolation’. AI is responsible for translating intuition into executable technical architectures
Identification of global override logic and gene preprocessing order Role Thought Human Humans quickly noticed logs showing ‘UNI’ instead of ‘UNI2’ and directly pointed out the problem. Only by asking ‘why is the effect poor’ did they drive the analysis forward AI AI compared MIHD prepare_gene_features with original STAIG adata_processing.py, discovered that the order of normalize/log/scale and HVG selection was swapped. However, when implementing tqdm, it overlooked existing global override logic Difference analysis: Humans are more aware of their own running environment and expected behavior, quickly identifying log anomalies. AI can systematically compare code details but easily overlook existing global logic, requiring users to observe to compensate
AI Limitations Important Limitations Inadequate thoroughness of multiple operations: When fixing mclust dimension errors, only shape protection checks were added without tracing the root cause upstream. When removing trend charts, only the calling was removed at first without handling function definitions simultaneously, requiring a second fix. Systematic comparison across code bases is inefficient, and gap analysis is gradually identified through multiple tool calls, failing to output a complete structured list at once General Limitations Easy to overlook existing global override logic: When adding UNI2 tqdm, it was not realized that strict STAIG mode would force visual encoder override. Before implementing STAIG alignment modifications, no active checks were performed on numerous unsubmitted dirty files. Users must be explicitly informed to proceed No pre-check of available modules in the HPC environment (e.g., scanpy), resulting in ModuleNotFoundError errors when verifying Python scripts. Only indirect verification methods could be used, increasing debugging iterations. Optimistic estimates of local tool reliability were made (Windows environment conda run occasionally failed) Multiple options for GPU utilization low issues were provided, but no profile tools (e.g., py-spy, nvprof) were actively suggested to accurately locate the actual bottleneck. Instead, guesses were made based on code reading Today’s Takeaways Core Takeaways There are five quantifiable key implementation differences between MIHD and the original STAIG (priority ranked): ① Mixed coordinate scaling for full-resolution images (most critical, coordinates incorrectly compressed from x:2579-11821 to x:386-1773, patch points severely deviating from tissue areas); ② mclust silently downgrading to kmeans when HPC was unavailable; ③ Gene preprocessing order reversed (HVG first vs normalize/log/scale first); ④ Pseudo-label cluster count 300 vs 80; ⑤ Hyperparameters and image transformations differences. The ARI gap (0.21→0.56) is mainly determined by ①②③ Correct hierarchical architecture for GitHub Pages static publishing and public data collection: Public users → relay (verification/rate-limiting/masking) → repository_dispatch → queue file → daily batch processing workflow (deduplication/cleaning/append CSV) → commit/push → automatic Pages deployment Interface names must strictly match actual behavior: The name ‘staig_fusion’ inherently promises equivalent semantics to STAIG. Allowing significant differences in default behavior can lead to many hidden errors. Global override behaviors (e.g., forcing encoder to UNI) must be clearly indicated in logs Plotly 6.x uses binary-encoded array serialization by default; when incompatible with older CDN versions (plotly-latest), it causes bar length errors. Solution: Lock the CDN version to match the Python package version and force the input to be a Python list Practical Takeaways Error messages from R interfaces (e.g., mclust) are not intuitive in Python. Sufficient pre-verification should be added at the Python level, with clear error messages including shape information. Silent degradation of mclust and other R packages must include clear warnings; clustering methods cannot be changed without any prompts Session Summary MIHD 🔄 Complete diagnosis and strict alignment implementation of MIHD STAIG fusion performance differences 23:02:06.469 | codex Completed the entire process of diagnosing MIHD STAIG fusion across multiple sessions: generated AGENTS.md contributor guide; compared original STAIG notebook with MIHD implementations, identified 5 key differences (clustering, gene input, hyperparameters, FFT preprocessing, pseudo-label cluster count). Users decided to ‘override directly’; modified 4 core files to implement strict alignment (default mclust+HVG features+STAIG hyperparameters), syntax verification passed; deeply diagnosed issues like mixed coordinate scaling in slide 151508 (most critical difference), reverse gene preprocessing order, and mclust problems when HPC was unavailable. At the same time, practical issues such as runtime mclust dimension errors, missing UNI2 tqdm, and low GPU utilization were addressed
benchmark✅ Fixing bugs in the benchmark report and implementing a complete pipeline for automatic GitHub Pages publishing 04:04:15.843 | codex Starting from generating the AGENTS.md contributor guide, we fixed an issue with inverted bar chart rendering (locked to Plotly 6.x CDN version). We removed the Historical Trends block and implemented a complete pipeline for automatic GitHub Pages updates (relay architecture + three GitHub Actions + strict verification/deduplication/cleaning scripts). We added interactive upload prompts for the CLI (–upload/–no-upload flags) and fixed an AttributeError in create_trend_chart. All changes have been locally verified, and the code is ready to be pushed.

title: bug journal 2026-02-06

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-05 Today’s Overview What was done: Completed two key tasks for the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project: fixed four critical implementation differences between STAIGTrainer and the original STAIG paper (adaptive edge dropout, gene clipping, edge weight direction, tau decay), as well as fully implemented and fixed the end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion module (**kwargs bug fixes, flexible gene preprocessing support). Additionally, we identified the root cause of performance differences through parameter comparison and designed a new end-to-end GCN training architecture. How it was done: Identified implementation differences by comparing lines of original STAIG code (net.py/staig.py/example.py), then fixed them accordingly. This was confirmed through 10 integrated verification tests. We also read the original STAIG configuration files to quantify parameter differences. Finally, we successfully ran the entire end-to-end pipeline (PCA+UNI2+staig_fusion_e2e, ARI=0.05, 14 seconds). What’s achieved: Eliminated fundamental implementation defects and runtime crashes that affected the performance of GCN+STAIG. We confirmed that hyperparameters were the main source of performance differences rather than algorithmic errors. This laid a solid foundation for improving ARI from 0.16 to the original STAIG level (0.45+, even up to 0.68). On the DCC cluster, we fixed four critical differences between STAIG implementation and the original paper, fully implemented and fixed the end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion module (including **kwargs bugs and flexible gene preprocessing support). We also quantified the root cause of performance differences between MIHD and the original STAIG through parameter comparison.
Today’s Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Fully implemented and improved the end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion module — Created STAIGTrainerE2E.py, fixed the fatal bug in apply_fusion() that lacked **kwargs parameters. Added support for gene preprocessing parameters in four modes (auto/standard/clip/none), allowing any gene encoder input (original 3000-dimensional expression or PCA/GCN/MLP encoded embeddings). Updated run_benchmark.py to support the staig_fusion_e2e strategy. The entire pipeline was successfully executed, and all 10 integration tests passed. ✅ Identified four critical implementation differences between the original STAIG — By comparing lines of original STAIG code, we fixed four issues in STAIGTrainer.py: ① Adaptive edge dropout (adaptive_dropout_adj, based on edge probability rather than uniform random); ② Gene feature np.clip(-10,10); ③ Edge weight direction changed from softmax_neglog to softmax_log; ④ Added a tau decay mechanism. All 10 verification tests passed. ✅ Analyzed the training results of GCN+STAIG+UNI2 and identified the root cause of performance differences — Analysis of experimental data revealed that ARI of GCN+STAIG (0.0009-0.3679, average ≈0.16) was much lower than that of PCA+STAIG (0.45+) and extremely unstable (CV ≈106%). By reading the original STAIG configuration file, we found that MIHD learning rate (0.001 vs 0.0005), number of GCN layers (2 vs 1), number of training iterations (550 vs 300), and weight decay (1e-4 vs 1e-5) were inconsistent with the original configuration. Hyperparameter differences were the main source of performance differences. 🔄 Designed a new end-to-end GCN training architecture — Referring to the train() process in STAIG example.py, we designed a new architecture where feat=concat(gene_expr, img_emb) is directly input into GCN for end-to-end optimization. The design has been formulated, but implementation has not yet begun. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Improved the benchmark CLI (support for GCN gene encoders and GPU verification) — Added ‘gcn’ to the argparse gene_encoder option and added corresponding processing logic (k-NN space graph construction, embed_dim=50). The default benchmark device is set to ‘auto’, automatically detecting CUDA availability. ✅ Fixed General environment dependencies and deterministic training configurations — Iteratively installed missing dependencies such as python_dateutil, pyarrow, packaging, psutil, scikit-learn, pillow, ensuring the General environment could load the benchmark script. Added the CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG environment variable at the top of core modules to ensure GPU operations were deterministic. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. GCN+STAIG performance is extremely poor and unstable (ARI average ≈0.16, CV ≈106%). The root cause is four critical differences between the implementation and the original STAIG paper. Solution: By comparing lines of the original STAIG’s net.py and staig.py, we identified four differences: missing adaptive edge dropout, gene clipping, incorrect edge weight direction, and missing tau decay. All verification tests passed.
Key insight: The original STAIG’s adaptive edge dropout (rand() >= edge_prob, where higher similarity edges were more likely to be dropped) was contrary to intuition. However, this mechanism prevented overfitting within the domain. Multiple deviations could lead to performance crashes, and the impact of each fix cannot be evaluated separately.
2. There are two key defects in the end-to-end fusion module: ① Misunderstanding of the gene encoder architecture (AI misinterpreted E2E as “no need for a gene encoder” and designed a gene_encoder=none interface); ② The apply_fusion() function signature lacked **kwargs, causing a runtime NameError crash. Solution: After the user corrected the misunderstanding about the architecture, AI redesigned the gene preprocessing parameters to support any input dimension. At the same time, **kwargs was added to the end of the apply_fusion() signature, ensuring that the fusion_kwargs dictionary could be passed correctly. Both defects were fully fixed in the second round of testing, and the end-to-end pipeline passed verification.
Key insight: The core of end-to-end training is the joint optimization of GCN and STAIG, not skipping gene encoding. Mismatches between function signatures and parameters are exposed during runtime. Uniform checks should be performed at the design stage to ensure consistency in interfaces.
3. MIHD’s STAIG fusion ARI (0.452) is lower than that of the original STAIG, but the reason is unknown. Solution: By comparing train_img_config.yaml and default parameters in run_benchmark.py, we found four parameter differences: learning rate was twice as high, one more layer in GCN, 250 more epochs, and weight decay was 10 times higher. Performance differences were caused by hyperparameters rather than algorithmic logic.
Key insight: Comparative learning models are highly sensitive to hyperparameters. Smaller learning rates and fewer layers may actually improve performance in STAIG, indicating a high risk of overfitting. When evaluating performance differences, hyperparameters should be checked first, rather than suspecting algorithmic errors.
4. The UNI2 model throws a shape invalid error when timm_kwargs is not provided (shape invalid for size 391680). Solution: It is necessary to provide all of timmkwargs (img_size, patch_size, depth, etc.) so that timm can correctly calculate the position encoding grid size.
Key insight: The size of the pre-trained weight position encoding table on HuggingFace Hub does not match timm’s default inference. Accurate architecture parameters must be provided when loading this data.
General Issues 5. Missing conda environment dependencies: AI environment lacked torch_geometric, and the General environment lacked pandas and other dependencies. Solution: The problem with torch_geometric was solved by switching to the General environment. The General environment was fixed by iteratively installing missing dependencies using pip/conda. Condos were prioritized for fixing underlying dependencies with inconsistent metadata.
Key insight: The project uses two conda environments (scgpt_3 and General), which must be switched based on tasks. Using both pip and conda in the same environment can lead to inconsistent metadata. It’s better to use condos for fixing underlying dependencies more reliably.
Human Thinking vs AI Thinking Strategic Level Understanding of E2E GCN training architecture and research direction Role Thoughts Human The user pointed out that the design with gene_encoder=none was incorrect (E2E should support any gene encoder). After reviewing STAIG example.py, they proposed a research direction for end-to-end GCN training in MIHD, with clear goals. AI AI misinterpreted E2E as “no need for a gene encoder” and simply using the original expression input to GCN. After being corrected, AI redesigned the gene preprocessing parameters and designed the implementation by exploring existing GCN architectures and referring to STAIG’s train() process. Difference analysis: The user has a deeper understanding of the original STAIG architecture (GCN itself is a gene encoder, and E2E focuses on joint optimization). They led the research direction. AI was responsible for designing the implementation and implementing the plan.
Identifying differences in STAIG implementation and the root cause of performance differences Role Thoughts Human The user actively proposed comparing the original STAIG code (provided with specific paths) and, based on experience, pointed out that MIHD’s results were inferior to those of the original project. This directly led to the direction of the problem. AI AI identified five specific implementation differences by comparing lines of code. By reading the configuration file, it quantified the parameter differences, providing specific values and root cause analysis. Difference analysis: The user is better at identifying directional problems (“not performing well compared to the original code”). AI is good at turning intuition into a list of quantitative differences and root cause analysis.
Implementation Level Environment repair strategy Role Thoughts Human The user adopted a practical approach of “just running things and fixing what’s missing” first, using pip before considering conda. AI AI initially tended to perform a comprehensive environment check but was corrected by the user to an iterative repair method. Difference analysis: The user’s direct approach is more efficient in scenarios where dependencies need to be fixed. AI’s systematic inspection approach is too cumbersome in this scenario.
AI Limitations Important Limitations There were two design flaws in the implementation of the E2E module: misunderstanding “end-to-end training” as “skipping gene encoding steps” (not understanding that GCN itself was a gene encoder in the original STAIG), and failing to check the consistency of **kwargs parameters in the apply_fusion() function signature, resulting in a runtime crash. Both needed to be fixed after being discovered by the user. General Limitations There was a deviation in identifying the root cause of UNI2’s error (mistakenly judged as GPU incompatibility, actually due to missing timmkwargs). The dependency installation efficiency was low (no pre-analysis of the complete dependency graph, multiple failed retries), and too many redundant documentation files were generated (multiple IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY class documents, which is more work than actual code). Today’s Achievements Core Achievements The original STAIG’s adaptive edge dropout (rand() >= edge_prob, where higher similarity edges were more likely to be dropped) was contrary to intuition. However, this mechanism prevented overfitting within the domain and enhanced cross-domain learning. Compared to uniform random dropout, this difference had a fundamental impact on GCN message passing quality. The performance ceiling of end-to-end training (GCN embedded within STAIG for joint optimization with multi-modal comparison) was much higher than that of two-stage separation (first extracting fixed GCN embeddings and then training STAIG). The original STAIG could achieve an ARI of 0.68, while the two-stage separation was only approximately 0.16. The difference stemmed from whether the GCN embedding contained cross-modal supervision signals. The configuration of STAIG hyperparameters was counterintuitive: smaller learning rates (0.0005), fewer GCN layers (1 layer), shorter training duration (300 epochs), and smaller weight decay (1e-5) actually led to better performance. This indicates that comparative learning models have a high risk of overfitting, and performance differences should be prioritized by checking hyperparameters rather than suspecting algorithmic logic. The ‘auto’ strategy for gene preprocessing (StandardScaler+Clip when dim>100, otherwise just Clip) is a simple and universal solution for handling genes from different sources. This avoids repeated normalization of standardized embeddings. Practical Achievements Two key points in project environment management: ① Use two conda environments (scgpt_3 for scGPT/gene analysis, General for image embedding/PCA). Switch between them based on tasks. ② CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG must be set at the top of the program (before all imports), otherwise CUDA operators will choose an uncertain path during initialization, and setting it later will be ineffective. Session Summary 🔄 GCN+STAIG fusion training work throughout the day: Fixed implementation differences, improved end-to-end modules, analyzed performance, and designed a new architecture 22:20:54.558 | claude_code Throughout the day, five core tasks were carried out related to MIHD’s GCN+STAIG fusion training: ① Identified and fixed four critical implementation differences by comparing original STAIG code (adaptive edge dropout, gene clipping, edge weight direction, tau decay). All 10 verification tests passed. ② Analysis of GCN+STAIG+UNI2 experimental data revealed extremely unstable ARI values (average ≈0.16). By reading the original STAIG configuration file, we quantified the difference in hyperparameters (lr/number of layers/epochs/weight decay) as the main source of performance differences. ③ Fixed the fatal bug in apply_fusion() that lacked **kwargs parameters. Reorganized STAIGTrainerE2E to support four gene preprocessing modes (auto/standard/clip/none). The end-to-end pipeline ran successfully with ARI=0.05, 14 seconds, and all 10 tests passed. ④ Fixed General environment dependencies and improved the benchmark CLI’s GCN support. ⑤ The user proposed a new direction for end-to-end GCN training (directly inputting feat=concat into GCN for joint optimization). The design has been formulated, but implementation has not yet begun.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-05

content:
Today’s problem 3432. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference
Intuition In this question, we need to partition the array into two parts. And the difference between these two parts are even.
Now, both parts must have the same module to 2. That is, they are both even or both odd. So, the sum of the array needs to be even.
Now, if the sum of the array is even, then every partition must have the same module to 2. Otherwise, the sum of the array would be odd.
Therefore, the answer would be 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Approach Return 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Space complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Code class Solution: def countPartitions(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: if sum(nums) % 2 == 1: return 0 return len(nums) - 1
summary: 1. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference

content:
Question Burst Balloons
Intuition While the ballons burst, it will not affect the score of another ballon, and the length of the list will always decrease. This means that we can use dynamic programming to solve this problem.
Approach Consider a case, when $dp[i][j]$ means the maximum score you can get by bursting all the balloons between i and j. In this case, our answer would be $dp[0][n-1]$, if n is the length of the list.
Now, let’s figure out how to get $dp[i][j]$. As we mentioned in part Intuition, the length of the balloon is always decreasing, which means that we might be able to get $dp[i][j]$ by first counting a shorter interval, and the increase the interval to $0 \to n-1$.
Now, we have our solution:
First, we iterate the length of the interval.
Next, we can break the interval $i \to j$ into two sub intervals $i \to k-1$ and $k+1 \to j$.
Now we can compute the score of interval $i \to j$ by adding up sub intervals $i \to k-1$, $k + 1 \to j$, and burst the balloon k.
Because the balloons in sub interval $i \to k-1$ and $k + 1 \to j$ are already gone, so the score addition would be $nums[k] \times nums[i-1] \times nums[j+1]$.
The only thing we now need to consider is the edge of these subintervals. Knowing that the balloon we are now bursting can be the edge of the interval $i \to j$, meaning that we need to iterate $[i, j]$ inclusively.
Finally, you will get your result in $dp[0][n-1]$.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^3)$. N is the length of the list. Space complexity: $O(N^2)$. N is the length of the list. Code class Solution: def maxCoins(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: n = len(nums) nums = nums + [1] dp = [[0 for _ in range(n+1)] for _ in range(n+1)] for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = nums[i] * nums[i-1] * nums[i+1] for i in range(1, n): for l in range(n): if i + l == n: break r = i + l for k in range(l, r+1): dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], dp[l][k-1] + dp[k+1][r] + nums[k] * nums[l-1] * nums[r + 1]) # print(dp) return dp[0][n-1]
summary: Interval Dynamic Programming with clear explaination and how you can get to the solution
title: LeetCode Question P.312 Burst Balloons

content:
Today’s problem 2894. Divisible and Non-divisible Sums Difference
Intuition We are given an integer n and a divisor m, and we want to compute the difference between:
The sum of numbers from 1 to n that are not divisible by m. The sum of numbers from 1 to n that are divisible by m. This means we want to partition the numbers 1 to n into two groups based on divisibility by m, sum each group, and return the difference.
Approach We can solve this problem using two methods:
Method 1: Formula-Based Use the formula for the sum of the first n natural numbers: n * (n + 1) // 2 to get the total sum. Count how many numbers from 1 to n are divisible by m: k = n // m. The divisible numbers are: m, 2m, ..., km, and their sum is m * (1 + 2 + ... + k) = m * (k * (k + 1) // 2). Subtract the divisible sum from the total to get the sum of non-divisible numbers, then subtract. Method 2: Brute-Force Iteration Iterate from 1 to n. If the number is divisible by m, add it to num2. Otherwise, add it to num1. Return the difference num1 - num2. Complexity Time complexity:
Method 1: $O(1)$ (constant time using formulas) Method 2: $O(n)$ (linear time iteration) Space complexity:
Both methods: $O(1)$ (only a few variables used) Code class Solution: def differenceOfSums(self, n: int, m: int) -> int: # Method 1: Formula-Based total_sum = n * (n + 1) // 2 k = n // m divisible_sum = m * (k * (k + 1) // 2) return total_sum - divisible_sum class Solution: def differenceOfSums(self, n: int, m: int) -> int: # Method 2: Brute-Force Iteration num1, num2 = 0, 0 for i in range(1, n + 1): if i % m == 0: num2 += i else: num1 += i return num1 - num2 Advertisement For more solutions, please visit My blog
summary: Traverse and Mathmatics!

content:
Today’s problem 2131. Longest Palindrome by Concatenating Two Letter Words
Intuition There are in total two ways to form a palindrome.
a string that has an inverse string in the list a string that is a palindrome itself. In this case, the string that is palindrome can only exisit in the middle of the palindrome. Approach Therefore, we can use a hash to solve this problem. Note that we will first run test 1 before test 2. If there is an inverse string in the list, then put that string and the current string into the list.
Then test whether this string is a palindrome itself.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of words.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of words.
Code class Solution: def longestPalindrome(self, words: List[str]) -> int: cnt = Counter(words) ans = 0 sp = 0 for word, t in cnt.items(): # print(word, t) if word[0] == word[1]: ans += (t - t % 2) sp |= (t % 2) else: ans += min(t, cnt[word[::-1]]) return (ans + sp) * 2 Advertisement For more solutions, please visit My blog
summary: Hash!

content:
Today’s problem 3068. Find the Maximum Sum of Node Values
Important: all the methods below are based on this fact: xor even times equals xor zero times. Method 1: Tree DP Intuition and Approach In this problem, if we only consider one direction, e.g., from root to leaf, then the process will not have after effect (later decisions will not affect previous ones). Therefore, we can use DP to solve this problem.
The hardest part is the definition of the dp. As we have a prerequisite of a direction, a better way to define the dp formula is to exclude the effect of current node. Also, for each node, there are two status, as described above, each node can either xor odd times or even times.
Therefore, we have our DP definition. $dp[x][0/1]$ means the largest value the children of x can achieve when the node x is changed (1) or unchanged (0).
Now, for each child c of node x, we can do two operations: either do xor for both node x and c, or do not do xor for neither x nor c.
The dp formula of these two operations will be: (Note: the priority of $\oplus$ is lower than $+$, so it is very important to add a parentheses.)
Do the xor operation $dp[x][0] = max(dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][0] + dp[c][1] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. $dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][1] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][1] + dp[c][1] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. NOT do the xor operation $dp[x][0] = max(dp[x][1] + dp[c][1] + nums[c], dp[x][1] + dp[c][0] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. $dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + nums[c], dp[x][0] + dp[c][0] + (nums[c] \oplus k))$. Note that the dp[x][0] and dp[x][1] should be renewed at the same time.
Moreover, another important thing is the initialization of the dp array. For all $dp[x][1]$, we will give it a value of $-inf$, so that we can avoid the case when c is a leaf node and the number is $\oplus$ with k contributes to the $dp[x]$ array.
The final result will be $max((dp[0][0] + nums[0]), (dp[0][1] + (nums[0] ^ k)))$
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp = [[0 for _ in range(2)] for _ in range(n)] for i in range(n): dp[i][1] = -10_000_000_000 edge = [[] for _ in range(n)] for x,y in edges: edge[x].append(y) edge[y].append(x) def dfs(x, fa): for to in edge[x]: if to == fa: continue dfs(to, x) c0 = max(dp[to][0] + nums[to], dp[to][1] + (nums[to] ^ k)) c1 = max(dp[to][0] + (nums[to] ^ k), dp[to][1] + nums[to]) dp[x][0], dp[x][1] = max(dp[x][0] + c0, dp[x][1] + c1), max(dp[x][1] + c0, dp[x][0] + c1) dfs(0,-1) return max((dp[0][0] + nums[0]), (dp[0][1] + (nums[0] ^ k))) Method 2: Tree DP with better memory Intuition and Approach In the previous code, we find that the $dp[x]$ will only use two times. Once in calculating the result of $dp[x]$, once in calculating the result of $dp[fa]$.
Therefore, we can return the value of $dp[x][0]$ and $dp[x][1]$ to avoid the extra space of the dp array.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) edge = [[] for _ in range(n)] for x,y in edges: edge[x].append(y) edge[y].append(x) def dfs(x, fa): dp0,dp1 = 0,-1e9 for to in edge[x]: if to == fa: continue c0, c1 = dfs(to, x) dp0, dp1 = max(dp0 + c0, dp1 + c1), max(dp0 + c1, dp1 + c0) return max(dp0 + nums[x], dp1 + (nums[x] ^ k)), max(dp0 + (nums[x] ^ k), dp1 + nums[x]) return dfs(0,-1)[0] Important: all the methods below are based on this fact: there are always a path between two nodes on a tree. Therefore, we can $\oplus$ all the nodes on this path, resulting the $\oplus$ of any two nodes on the tree. Method 3: DP without tree Intuition and Approach For each node, we have two status, whether to $\oplus$ k or not. Therefore, the definition of the DP array will be: $dp[i][0/1]$ means whether there are odd (1) or even (0) $\oplus$ k operations when traversing to the ith node.
We then have the formular:
When this node $\oplus$ with k: $dp[i][0] = max(dp[i-1][0] + nums[i], dp[i-1][1] + (nums[i] ^ k))$ When this node do not $\oplus$ with k: $dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][1] + nums[i], dp[i-1][0] + (nums[i] ^ k))$ Note that there are always even $\oplus$ operations, so the answer would be $dp[n-1][0]$.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp = [[0 for _ in range(2)] for _ in range(n)] dp[0][0] = nums[0] dp[0][1] = (nums[0] ^ k) for i in range(1, n): dp[i][0] = max(dp[i-1][0] + nums[i], dp[i-1][1] + (nums[i] ^ k)) dp[i][1] = max(dp[i-1][0] + (nums[i] ^ k), dp[i-1][1] + nums[i]) return dp[-1][0] Method 4: DP without tree with better memory Intuition and Approach Same as Method 2, we also find out that the dp[i] formular only use twice. In this case, we can use two variables instead of the whold array to have a better memory usage.
Also, the $max$ operations is too slow in python, so a better way is to use if else equations instead of max.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: n = len(nums) dp0, dp1 = 0, -10_000_000_000 for i in range(n): a = nums[i] b = a ^ k new_dp0 = dp0 + a if dp0 + a > dp1 + b else dp1 + b new_dp1 = dp0 + b if dp0 + b > dp1 + a else dp1 + a dp0, dp1 = new_dp0, new_dp1 return dp0 Method 5: Greedy algorithm Intuition and Approach Another way to look at this method without of tree is using greedy algorithm. Because we know that we can $\oplus$ k as long as we can find a pair of nodes, we can use greedy algorithm to find the pairs that has the most differences after $\oplus$ k.
That is, we can first $\oplus$ every element with k, calculating the difference between the new array and the previous array, then find all the pairs that has a difference that is larger than zero, then we get our answer.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of nums. Code class Solution: def maximumValueSum(self, nums: List[int], k: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: ans = sum(nums) diff = [(x ^ k) - x for x in nums] cnt,l,r = 0,inf,-inf for x in diff: if x > 0: cnt += 1 if x < l: l = x ans += x else: if r < x: r = x if cnt % 2 == 1: ans += max(-l, r) return ans I don’t know why using sort to do greedy algorithm is so neat and fast. Just as the one in the official solution.

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/zero-array-transformation-iii/
Intuition This question requires us to find the number of sections that is “useful”, or in other words, the smallest number of sections that is enough to make the array a zero array.
The key to solve this question is to change a view of how we look at this problem: if we take each element in the array seperately, then we can use greedy algorithm to solve this problem.
The thing is, if we look at each element seperately, i.e., to make the entire array a zero array, we must make each element zero.
In this case, for each element, the best way is to find a section that contains this elemnt, while it has a further tail. This is because a furtuer tail means to cover more elements in the future, which will be always better compared with the sections that has a shorter tail.
Therefore, now we need a data structure to store the current “farest tail”. This data structure need to add element dynamically and delete the largest item, when a heap will be the best way to store the “tail”.
Approach Therefore, we can form our algorithm.
First, we need to sort the array using the left end of the query as keyword. In this case, we can find which queries has a left end that is to the left of our current index. Then we need to create a difference array to deal with the section add operation; a heap to store the right end of the queries; and an index to show where we are currently at when we traverse all the queries. The next step is to traverse the number array: for each element in the number array, we first need to push all the queries that has the left end that is less the current index. This makes all the elements in the heap potentially available to use to decrease the current element. Then we will deal with the current element, finding all the available queries for the current element, then deal with the section decrease operation. (In this case, we ensure every operation is valid by checking that the endpoint of the heap top is larger or equal to the index, so that the left end of the array will be less or equal to the current index, and the right end of the array will be larger or equal to the current index. Therefore, we guarentee that the operation is valid). Finally, if the number is still larger than 0, we will return -1; otherwise, we will return the remaining element in h, which is all the unused elements. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N \times log(m))$
Space complexity: $O(N + M)$
Code class Solution: def maxRemoval(self, nums: List[int], queries: List[List[int]]) -> int: queries.sort(key = lambda x: x[0]) diff = [0] * (len(nums) + 1) h = [] idx, now = 0,0 for i in range(len(nums)): while idx < len(queries) and queries[idx][0] <= i: heappush(h, -queries[idx][1]) idx += 1 now += diff[i] while h and now < nums[i] and -h[0] >= i: now += 1 diff[-heappop(h) + 1] -= 1 if now < nums[i]: return -1 return len(h) For more solutions, please visit My blog
summary: Greedy Algorithm, look at each element seperately!

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-smallest-number-from-di-string/
Intuition This problem let us find a sequence of numbers under some constrains.
First we observe that the N, the length of the sequence, is very small. Thereofre, we can use a dfs to traverse all possible situations and get the result.
Another way to do that is using a stack. When the array is increasing, the smallest way to get a valid array is to traverse all the numbers, filling in the smallest number possible. When the array is decreasing, becuase we still want the smallest array, we need to put the smallest number available, which should be also larger to the next number. In this case, the smallest number we can put at current place is the position + the number of consequtive ‘D’s afterward. Therefore, we can use a stack to temporarily sotre the number we are traversing, and add it back to the current array when we meet a ‘I’ or reach to the end of the array.
Approach For Solution 1, the dfs solution, all we need to do is to traverse all the solutions and get the first one that fullfills the requirement.
For Solution 2, the stack solution, when we meet ‘I’, we can put “idx + 1” to our current array, then fill all the elements in a stack into our current array in reverse order, then flush the stack. When we meet ‘D’, we can put “idx + 1” to our stack for our future use.
Trick DFS: Because we are required to find the smallest valid sequence, so the first sequence that is not None is our target. This means that we can return this answer as soon as we get a valid result.
Stack: Here I intentionally add a “D” to the end of the sequence. Intuitively speaking, the last element is the largest element in the sequence, so to put it into our current sequence, it requires a “D” operation. In this case, if the original last character is ‘I’, then we can directlly put the largest number to the end of our original sequence, which is the same as a ‘D’ operation. This is because the ‘I’ operation will flush the stack, so there will only be one element in the stack, making it the same whether adding as a normal sequence or a inverted sequence. If the original last character is ‘D’, then the largest character should be at the position whether the consequtive sequence of ‘D’ starts. In this case, this means that there should be a ‘D’ operation to put this number into the right position. Though this trick makes the code more tidy and elegant, it sacrifices readability, which is not encouraged.
Complexity Time complexity for dfs solution: $O(N!)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Time complexity for stack solution: $O(N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Space complexity for dfs solution: $O(N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Space complexity for stack solution: $O(N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Code class Solution: def smallestNumber(self, pattern: str) -> str: arr = [] n = len(pattern) + 2 def dfs(arr): if len(arr) == n - 1: return arr now = len(arr) - 1 res = None if pattern[now] == 'I': for i in range(arr[now] + 1, n): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res else: for i in range(1, arr[now]): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res return res for i in range(1, n): ans = dfs([i]) if ans is not None: return ''.join(map(str, ans)) # return ans return None class Solution: def smallestNumber(self, pattern: str) -> str: arr = [] n = len(pattern) + 2 def dfs(arr): if len(arr) == n - 1: return arr now = len(arr) - 1 res = None if pattern[now] == 'I': for i in range(arr[now] + 1, n): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res else: for i in range(1, arr[now]): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res return res for i in range(1, n): ans = dfs([i]) if ans is not None: return ''.join(map(str, ans)) # return ans return None For more solutions, please visit My blog.
summary: DFS beats 100% and O(N) stack with trick

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/set-matrix-zeroes/
Intuition This question asks us to change the rows and columns to 0 if there exists an 0 in the row or column. Therefore, we can store the rows and columns and then change all these rows and colums to zero.
Approach Store all the columns and rows that contains 0 Change all these columns and rows Complexity Time complexity: $O(N \times M)$, N is the length of the array, M is the width of the array.
Space complexity: $O(N \times M)$, N is the length of the array, M is the width of the array.
Code class Solution: def setZeroes(self, matrix: List[List[int]]) -> None: """ Do not return anything, modify matrix in-place instead. """ change_row_idx = set([]) change_col_idx = set([]) # Note that here I use set to avoid recording the same row or column multiple times. for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): if matrix[i][j] == 0: change_row_idx.add(i) change_col_idx.add(j) for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): if i in change_row_idx or j in change_col_idx: matrix[i][j] = 0 For more solutions, please visit My blog
summary: Do what the question asks!

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/zero-array-transformation-i/
Intuition This problem means to decrease one in a range (l, r) for each query. To deal with the change of a range, we can consider prefix sum. Note that the question says “Select a subset of indices”, this means that we do not necessarily need to minus 1 for all indices in the range. In this case, because we only care whether the final array is a zero array or not, so instead of testing whether the final array is zero or not, we can test whether the final array is less or equal to zero or not becuase of the subset mentioned in the question.
Approach For each query, we can add 1 to the difference array at l and add -1 to the difference array at r + 1. Then when we calculate the final answer, we can use the prefix sum to add them up and get the change of the array. Finally, when we want to know whether the final array is zero array or not, we can add the difference array to the original array and test whether each index is less or equal to zero or not to get the answer.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N + M)$, N is the length of the original array, M is the length of the query.
Space complexity: $O(N)$.
Code class Solution: def isZeroArray(self, nums: List[int], queries: List[List[int]]) -> bool: diff = [0] * (len(nums) + 1) for l, r in queries: diff[l] -= 1 diff[r + 1] += 1 for i in range(len(nums)): if i > 0: diff[i] += diff[i-1] if nums[i] + diff[i] > 0: # print(i, nums[i], diff[i]) return False return True For more solutions, please visit My blog
summary: Use Chafen!

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/painting-a-grid-with-three-different-colors/
Intuition In this question, we find that m is relatively small compared with n. As $m \le 5$, and $n \le 1000$. Then we may consider to enumerate all solutions for a column, then elaborate it to the whole matrix. When elaborating it to the whole matrix, we figure out a thing: whatever a column is painted, the only influence is its next column, while future columns will not influence previous columns. This makes DP possible.
Approach Therefore, here is our approach:
First, we need to find out how many valid patterns are there in a column. Therefore, we can perform a dfs to search for all possible combinations. Second, we need to know which two patterns can be in adjcent columns, so we enumerate through each pair of patterns, and then test whether they can be in adjcent rows or not. Third, we use DP to elaborate from one column to the next. In this case, the DP formular will be: $DP[col][case_x] = \sum DP[col-1][case_y]. \forall \text{casex and casey can be in two adjcent columns}$. Finally, all we need to do is to add up all the cases of the final column of DP to get our answer. Complexity Time complexity: $O(3^{2m} \times n)$
Space complexity: $O(3^{2m})$
Code class Solution: def colorTheGrid(self, m: int, n: int) -> int: pat = [] col = [0, 1, 2] def dfs(x, s): if x == m: pat.append(s) return for i in col: if x == 0 or s[x - 1] != i: dfs(x + 1, s + [i]) dfs(0, []) # till this step, we find all valid patterns for a column and store it in the pattern list. l = len(pat) valid = [[True for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(l)] for i in range(l): for j in range(i + 1, l): for k in range(m): if pat[i][k] == pat[j][k]: valid[i][j] = False break # till this step, we find all the pattern pairs that is valid. dp = [[0 for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(n)] mod = 1_000_000_007 for i in range(l): dp[0][i] = 1 # for column 0, each pattern is valid. for i in range(1, n): for x in range(l): for y in range(x + 1, l): if valid[x][y]: dp[i][x] = (dp[i][x] + dp[i-1][y]) % mod dp[i][y] = (dp[i][y] + dp[i-1][x]) % mod # we elaborate to the next column according to the DP formula. ans = 0 for i in range(l): ans = (ans + dp[-1][i]) % mod # finally, we add up all the answers. return ans
summary: First DFS then DP!

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/letter-tile-possibilities/description/
Intuition In this question, we want to know how many different tiles we can generate.
Approach Therefore, we can use backtracking to enumerate all the solutions.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(2^N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the sequence.
Code class Solution: def numTilePossibilities(self, tiles: str) -> int: counter = defaultdict(int) for ch in tiles: counter[ch] += 1 def dfs(counter): total = 0 for ch in counter: if counter[ch] == 0: continue # Choose character total += 1 counter[ch] -= 1 total += dfs(counter) counter[ch] += 1 # backtracking return total return dfs(counter)
summary: 1. Letter Tile Possibilities
